infected r — recovered vaccination herd immunity threshold the sir differential equations: worked example — a school outbreak: final size equation extensions of the sir model: seir sis sirs age-structured network-based effective reproduction number network-based models degree distribution variance ecology-and-populations ecology & population science how populations grow, ecosystems connect, and species survive — or don't. dancing-deer-of-loktak-lake kaziranga-grass elephant-corridor golden-hilsa girl-grew-forest honey-hunters-lesson what is an ecosystem? elephantecosystemdiagram imagine a lake. now zoom in. there's water, mud, sunlight, bacteria in the sediment, algae on the surface, fish in the middle, birds overhead, and deer drinking at the edge. all of it — living and non-living — interacting together. that's an ecosystem.

 every ecosystem has three types of players:

 | role | what they do | examples |
 |------|-------------|----------|
 | producers | make food from sunlight (photosynthesis) | grass, algae, phytoplankton |
 | consumers | eat other organisms for energy | insects, fish, deer, tigers |
 | decomposers | break down dead matter, recycle nutrients | fungi, bacteria, earthworms |

 energy flows in one direction: sun → producers → consumers → heat. but nutrients cycle — carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus loop endlessly through soil, water, organisms, and atmosphere.

 an ecosystem can be enormous (the amazon basin) or tiny (a rotting log). loktak lake in manipur is an ecosystem: water, floating phumdi islands, sangai deer, fish, insects, and bacteria all connected. a rice paddy is another. the brahmaputra floodplain is another. what matters is not size — it's the web of connections.

 think of it this way: an ecosystem is like a city. producers are the farms and power plants. consumers are the residents. decomposers are the waste-management system. remove any one and the city breaks down. how productive is an ecosystem?

 ecosystem productivity is measured in grams of carbon fixed per square metre per year:

 | ecosystem type | npp (g c/m²/year) | why? |
 |----------------|-------------------|------|
 | tropical rainforest | 1,000–3,500 | warm, wet, year-round sunlight |
 | temperate grassland | 200–1,500 | seasonal, moderate rain |
 | freshwater wetland | 1,000–2,500 | nutrient-rich, sunlit shallows |
 | open ocean | 50–200 | nutrient-poor surface waters |
 | desert | 0–250 | water-limited |

 gpp (gross primary productivity) is the total energy fixed by photosynthesis. npp (net primary productivity) = gpp minus respiration — the energy actually available to the rest of the food web.

 the 10% rule: roughly 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels. the rest is lost as heat (second law of thermodynamics). if producers fix 10,000 kj/m²/year:

 | trophic level | energy available (kj/m²/yr) |
 |---------------|----------------------------|
 | producers | 10,000 |
 | primary consumers (herbivores) | 1,000 |
 | secondary consumers (small predators) | 100 |
 | tertiary consumers (top predators) | 10 |

 this is why food chains rarely exceed 4–5 levels — there simply isn't enough energy left at the top. compartment models describe ecosystem dynamics with differen

biodiversity hotspots key biodiversity areas (kbas) systematic conservation planning edge species the extinction debt: half-earth hypothesis ecosystems-and-biomes ecosystems & biomes energy flows, nutrients cycle, and biomes from kaziranga grasslands to himalayan alpine meadows. kaziranga-grass elephant-corridor girl-grew-forest dancing-deer-of-loktak-lake energy flow through ecosystems energypyramiddiagram every ecosystem runs on a single power source: the sun. but sunlight doesn't reach animals directly. it passes through a chain of living things, losing energy at every step.

 think of it like a relay race where each runner drops 90% of the baton's contents before passing it on. by the fourth runner, almost nothing is left.

 the trophic levels:

 | level | role | examples | energy available |
 |-------|------|---------|------------------|
 | 1 — producers | capture sunlight via photosynthesis | grasses, algae, phytoplankton | 100% (base) |
 | 2 — primary consumers | eat producers | rhinos, deer, grasshoppers, zooplankton | 10% |
 | 3 — secondary consumers | eat herbivores | tigers, frogs, small fish | 1% |
 | 4 — tertiary consumers | top predators | eagles, large sharks | 0.1% |
 | all levels — decomposers | break down dead matter | fungi, bacteria, earthworms | recycle nutrients |

 why only 10%? when a one-horned rhino eats 50 kg of tall elephant grass, roughly 90% of the energy in that grass was already used by the grass itself (for growth, respiration) or is lost as heat during the rhino's digestion and movement. only about 10% becomes new rhino body mass — muscle, fat, bone. this is the 10% rule.

 worked example — kaziranga energy budget:

 | trophic level | energy (kj/m²/year) | calculation |
 |---------------|--------------------|--------------|
 | grass (producers) | 40,000 | captured from sunlight |
 | rhinos, deer (herbivores) | 4,000 | 40,000 × 10% |
 | tigers (carnivores) | 400 | 4,000 × 10% |
 | scavengers/parasites | 40 | 400 × 10% |

 this is why food chains rarely exceed 4–5 links — there simply isn't enough energy left for another level. it also explains why a single bengal tiger needs a home range of 20–100 km²: the prey density, itself limited by plant productivity, can only support so many predators.

 quick check: a grassland produces 10,000 kj/m²/year. how much energy is available to a hawk that eats snakes that eat mice that eat seeds?

 answer: 10,000 × 0.1 × 0.1 × 0.1 = 10 kj/m²/year — just 0.1% of the original. measuring productivity:

 ecologists quantify energy flow using productivity metrics:

 | metric | definition | unit |
 |--------|-----------|------|
 | gpp (gross primary productivity) | total energy fixed by photosynthesis | g c/m²/year |
 | npp (net primary productivity) | gpp minus plant respiration | g c/m²/year |
 | nep (net ecosystem productivity) | npp minus all heterotrophic respiration | g c/m²/year |

 npp varies enormously across ecosystems:

 | ecosystem | npp (g c/m²/year) | why |
 |-----------|-------------------|-----|
 | tropical rainforest | 1,000–2,000 | warm, wet, year-round sunlight |
 | tropical grassland (e.g., kaziranga) | 1,500–2,000 | monsoon flooding brings nutrients |

searchText:
electricity & current how electrons flow, circuits work, and lightning strikes over the brahmaputra. ohms law resistance capacitance inductance kirchhoff coulomb voltage divider what is electric charge? current, voltage, and resistance match the electrical quantity to its unit and meaning series and parallel circuits match the circuit property to its behaviour static electricity and lightning match each lightning stage to what happens electrical safety proton neutron electron electrically neutral static electricity like charges repel unlike charges attract coulomb's law the triboelectric series try the diagram above f = kq₁q₂ / r² e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ c worked example — two point charges: 0.072 n (attractive) e = f/q = kq/r² worked example — field from a point charge: 8,990 v/m maxwell's equations k = 1 / (4πε₀) = 0 | gauss's law (magnetic) | no magnetic monopoles |
' + '| ∮ e = −∇v e = σ / (2ε₀) electric current voltage current amperes ohm's law v = i × r quick check: 4 amps ohm's law in full: v = ir power p = iv p = i²r p = v²/r worked example — car headlight: 4 a 48 w 172,800 j electron drift velocity v_d = i / (nae) worked example: resistivity r = ρl/a the drude model v_d = eeτ/m σ = ne²τ/m j = σe drude sommerfeld band theory bcs theory superconductivity: series circuit same shared adds up entire parallel circuit splits decreases others 18ω series circuits — worked example: 20 ω 0.6 a 2.4 v 3.6 v 6.0 v 12 v parallel circuits — worked example: 3 ω 2 a kirchhoff's laws kcl kvl v_out = v_in × r₂ / (r₁ + r₂) 3.33v analysis techniques for complex circuits: mesh analysis nodal analysis superposition thevenin's theorem norton's theorem v_th = i_n × r_n ac circuits z = r + jx s = p + jq maximum power transfer theorem: z_load = z_source lightning how lightning forms inside a thundercloud: positive negative lightning bolt lightning in numbers: thunder safety rule of thumb: 3 × 10⁶ v/m worked example — lightning energy: 5 c 1.5 × 10⁹ j ~400 kwh types of lightning: the thunder-distance method: the 30-30 rule: the stepped leader process: stepped leader streamers connection return stroke dart leaders transient luminous events (tles) sprites elves blue jets gigantic jets the global electric circuit: 240 volts ac how much current is dangerous? can't let go ventricular fibrillation key insight: 240 ma fatal protection devices in your home: fuse mcb rccb 30 ma why do birds sit safely on power lines? earthing (grounding): the danger threshold in detail: lethal how an rccb works: under 30 milliseconds fuse calculations: 1.69 w 100 w i²t rating worked example — choosing a fuse: 10 a 13 a physiological effects of electric shock: t-wave most dangerous arc flash hazards nfpa 70e modern protection — afcis: assam context: electricity-and-current electricity & current engineering resistance capacitance inductance voltage divider how electrons flow, circuits work, and lightning strikes over the brahmaputra. firefly-festival-of-majuli festival-lights fireflies-dont-burn what is electric charge? staticelectricitydiagram every atom is built from three kinds of particles:

 | particle | charge | location | mass |
 |----------|--------|----------|------|
 | proton | positive (+) | nucleus (centre) | 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg |
 | neutron | none (0) | nucleus (centre) | 1.67 × 10⁻²⁷ kg |
 | electron | negative (−) | orbiting outside | 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg |

 in most everyday objects, protons and electrons are equal in number, so the object is electrically neutral. but electrons sit on the outer edges of atoms and can be knocked loose relatively easily — rub a balloon on your hair, and electrons transfer from your hair to the balloon, leaving your hair positively charged and the balloon negatively charged.

 this transfer of electrons is the basis of static electricity. the fundamental rule is simple:

 - like charges repel — positive pushes positive away, negative pushes negative away
 - unlike charges attract — positive pulls negative toward it

 the force between charges follows coulomb's law — it is proportional to the product of the two charges and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. double the distance and the force drops to one quarter.

 the triboelectric series ranks materials by how easily they gain or lose electrons when rubbed together:

 | tends to lose electrons (become +) | tends to gain electrons (become −) |
 |--------------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
 | human hair | rubber |
 | glass | teflon |
 | nylon | pvc |
 | wool | polyethylene |
 | silk | polystyrene |

 this is why rubbing a plastic comb through your hair lets the comb pick up tiny scraps of paper — the comb acquires a net negative charge that induces a positive charge on the near side of each paper scrap, creating an attractive force strong enough to overcome gravity.

 try the diagram above — rub different materials together and watch electrons transfer. coulomb's law gives the exact force:

 > f = kq₁q₂ / r²

 where k = 8.99 × 10⁹ n·m²/c² and q is charge in coulombs. the elementary charge is e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ c — the charge of one proton (or the magnitude of one electron's charge).

 worked example — two point charges:

 two charges of +1 μc and −2 μc are separated by 0.5 m. find the force.

 | step | calculation |
 |------|------------|
 | identify values | q₁ = 1 × 10⁻⁶ c, q₂ = 2 × 10⁻⁶ c, r = 0.5 m |
 | apply formula | f = 8.99×10⁹ × (1×10⁻⁶ × 2×10⁻⁶) / (0.5)² |
 | simplify numerator | = 8.99×10⁹ × 2×10⁻¹² = 0.01798 |
 | divide by r² | = 0.01798 / 0.25 = 0.072 n (attractive) |

 the electric field e = f/q = kq/r² has units n/c or v/m.

 worked example — field from a point charge:

 at 1 m from a +1 μc point charge: e = 8.99×10⁹ × 10⁻⁶ / 1² = 8,990 v/m.

 | quantity | symbol | unit | meaning |
 |----------|--------|------|---------|
 | charge | q | coulomb (c) | amount of electric charge |
 | force | f | newton (n) | push or pull between charges |
 | electric field | e | v/m or n/c | force per unit charge |
 | potential | v | volt (v) | energy per unit charge |

 triboelectric charging transfers roughly 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁶ c, producing voltages of thousands of volts but negligible current — which is why a static shock stings but doesn't kill. coulomb's law is the electrostatic limit of the full electromagnetic theory described by maxwell's equations. the permittivity of free space ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² f/m relates to k by:

 > k = 1 / (4πε₀)

 | maxwell's equation | name | what it says |
 |--------------------|------|-------------|
 | ∮e·da = q/ε₀ | gauss's law (electric) | charges create electric fields |
 | ∮b·da = 0 | gauss's law (magnetic) | no magnetic monopoles |
 | ∮e·dl = −dφb/dt | faraday's law | changing magnetic fields create electric fields |
 | ∮b·dl = μ₀i + μ₀ε₀ dφe/dt | ampere-maxwell law | currents and changing electric fields create magnetic fields |

 in quantum electrodynamics (qed), electromagnetic forces are mediated by virtual photon exchange between charged particles. the electric field is the gradient of the scalar potential: e = −∇v.

 gauss's law provides an elegant way to calculate fields for symmetric charge distributions — a uniformly charged sphere behaves exactly like a point charge from outside. for a uniformly charged infinite plane: e = σ / (2ε₀), independent of distance.

 recent research (2019, nature) showed that even identical materials can develop charge differences due to nanoscale surface roughness and local electron energy variations, challenging the classical contact-potential model of triboelectric charging. current, voltage, and resistance ohmslawdiagram electric current is the flow of electric charge through a conductor — typically electrons moving through a metal wire. think of it like water flowing through a pipe.

 | electrical concept | water analogy | unit |
 |--------------------|---------------|------|
 | volt

lithium-sulfur sodium-ion zinc-air aluminium-ion ragone plot india's battery future: electrochemistry electrochemistry electrolysis galvanic cell nernst equation where chemistry meets electricity — batteries, corrosion, and electroplating. firefly-festival-of-majuli festival-lights redox reactions — electron transfer redoxdiagram electrochemistry is built on one idea: electrons moving from one substance to another. these are called redox reactions — short for reduction-oxidation.

 the analogy: imagine two people passing a ball. the person who throws the ball (loses it) is oxidised. the person who catches it (gains it) is reduced. the ball is an electron.

 the mnemonic oil rig makes this stick:
 - oxidation is loss (of electrons)
 - reduction is gain (of electrons)

 a reaction you can see: dip a zinc strip into blue copper sulphate solution:

 | what happens | half-reaction | name |
 |-------------|---------------|------|
 | zinc atoms lose 2 electrons each | zn → zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ | oxidation (zinc is the "thrower") |
 | copper ions gain 2 electrons each | cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → cu | reduction (copper is the "catcher") |

 you can watch this happen: the zinc strip becomes coated with reddish copper metal, and the blue solution gradually fades as cu²⁺ ions are removed.

 redox is everywhere:

 | everyday process | what's oxidised | what's reduced |
 |-----------------|----------------|----------------|
 | iron rusting | iron (fe → fe³⁺) | oxygen (o₂ → o²⁻) |
 | wood burning in an assamese cooking fire | carbon in wood | oxygen in air |
 | photosynthesis in kaziranga's forests | water (h₂o) | carbon dioxide (co₂) |
 | your cells making energy (respiration) | glucose (c₆h₁₂o₆) | oxygen (o₂) |
 | a battery powering your phone | metal at the anode | metal ion at the cathode |

 check yourself: when iron rusts, is iron oxidised or reduced?

 iron is oxidised — it loses electrons to oxygen. the rust (iron oxide) is the product of iron giving up its electrons. oxidation states — the bookkeeping system:

 oxidation states (or oxidation numbers) let you track electron transfer in any reaction. rules:

 | rule | example |
 |------|--------|
 | elements in their natural form = 0 | fe = 0, o₂ = 0, zn = 0 |
 | oxygen is usually −2 | in h₂o: o = −2 |
 | hydrogen is usually +1 | in h₂o: h = +1 |
 | sum of oxidation states = charge on species | in so₄²⁻: s + 4(−2) = −2, so s = +6 |

 worked example — balancing a redox equation:

 balance: fe²⁺ + mno₄⁻ → fe³⁺ + mn²⁺ (in acidic solution)

 | step | action | result |
 |------|--------|--------|
 | 1 | write half-reactions | fe²⁺ → fe³⁺ and mno₄⁻ → mn²⁺ |
 | 2 | balance atoms (add h₂o, h⁺) | mno₄⁻ + 8h⁺ → mn²⁺ + 4h₂o |
 | 3 | balance electrons | fe²⁺ → fe³⁺ + e⁻ and mno₄⁻ + 8h⁺ + 5e⁻ → mn²⁺ + 4h₂o |
 | 4 | equalise electrons (×5 for fe) | 5fe²⁺ → 5fe³⁺ + 5e⁻ |
 | 5 | add half-reactions | 5fe²⁺ + mno₄⁻ + 8h⁺ → 5fe³⁺ + mn²⁺ + 4h₂o |

 mn goes from +7 to +2 (gains 5e⁻ = reduced). each fe goes from +2 to +3 (loses 1e⁻ = oxidised). five iron atoms supply the five electrons that one permanganate ion needs. electrochemical series and predicting reactions:

 the standard electrode potential (e°) measures a species' tendency to gain electrons. the more positive e°, the stronger the oxidising agent:

 | half-reaction | e° (v) | tendency |
 |--------------|--------|----------|
 | f₂ + 2e⁻ → 2f⁻ | +2.87 | strongest oxidiser |
 | au³⁺ + 3e⁻ → au | +1.50 | gold never corrodes |
 | ag⁺ + e⁻ → ag | +0.80 | noble |
 | cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → cu | +0.34 | moderate |
 | 2h⁺ + 2e⁻ → h₂ | 0.00 | reference |
 | fe²⁺ + 2e⁻ → fe | −0.44 | reactive |
 | zn²⁺ + 2e⁻ → zn | −0.76 | more reactive |
 | al³⁺ + 3e⁻ → al | −1.66 | highly reactive |
 | li⁺ + e⁻ → li | −3.04 | strongest reducer |

 prediction rule: a species lower in the table (more negative e°) will reduce a species higher in the table. zn (−0.76 v) spontaneously reduces cu²⁺ (+0.34 v) because e°cell = e°cathode − e°anode = +0.34 − (−0.76) = +1.10 v (positive = spontaneous).

 disproportionation — a species simultaneously oxidises and reduces itself. example: cu⁺ in water: 2cu⁺ → cu²⁺ + cu. this happens because e° for cu⁺/cu (+0.52 v) > e° for cu²⁺/cu⁺ (+0.16 v), making cu⁺ unstable in aqueous solution.

 latimer and frost diagrams provide compact summaries of an element's redox chemistry across multiple oxidation states — essential for understanding multi-electron transfer in transition metals like manganese (which has stable oxidation states from 0 to +7). match each term to its meaning in electrochemistry loss of electrons — the substance that "throws" electrons (zn → zn²⁺ + 2e⁻) gain of electrons — the substance that "catches" electrons (cu²⁺ + 2e⁻ → cu) oxidising agent the substance that gets reduced — it causes oxidation in others (cu²⁺ in a daniell cell) reducing agent the substance that gets oxidised — it causes reduction in others (zn in a daniell cell) voltaic cells — chemistry makes electricity voltaiccelldiagram in the zinc-copper reaction above, electrons transfer directly from zinc to copper — releasing energy as heat. but what if you separate the two halves and force the electrons to tra

title: Energy & Work
searchText:
energy & work kinetic energy potential energy conservation of energy joule watt power efficiency forms of energy match each energy form to its real-world example work = force x distance conservation of energy match each energy transformation to its real-world example power and efficiency renewable energy — rivers, sun, and biomass money which form kinetic gravitational pe chemical pe elastic pe thermal electrical nuclear electromagnetic the key insight: try the diagram above quick check: the two energy formulas you must know: worked example 1 — falling mango: 19.6 j 14.0 m/s worked example 2 — why speed matters more than mass: 1,254 96,605 386,420 966,050 quadruples energy density — how much energy is packed into different fuels: the lagrangian formulation l = t - v euler-lagrange equation noether's theorem (1918) energy momentum angular momentum hamiltonian work is done when a force moves an object in the direction of that force. w = f x d x cos(theta) analogy: zero work why can you feel exhausted without doing physics-work? the unit of work is the joule (j): the work-energy theorem w_net = delta ke = ½mv_2² - ½mv_1² worked example — tea crate sliding down a ramp: 98.1 n 169.9 n 42.5 n 55.6 n +490.5 j -212.5 j 278.0 j 5.27 m/s worked example — pushing at an angle: 72.5 n 725 j variable forces w = integral of f dx path integral w = integral_c f . dr conservative the virial theorem law of conservation of energy a falling mango in a jorhat orchard: a pendulum at any point during free fall: worked example 1 — mango drop (m = 0.3 kg, h = 8 m): 23.54 j 11.77 j 0 j 8.86 m/s 12.53 m/s worked example 2 — roller coaster with friction: 147,150 j 49,050 j 15,000 j 83,100 j 18.2 m/s = 65.7 km/h 19.8 m/s = 71.3 km/h energy conversion chains: time-translation symmetry where energy conservation gets subtle: pair production power = work / time 147 w 49 w efficiency = (useful output / total input) x 100% two forms of the power equation: worked example 1 — cyclist on a guwahati road: 167 w 1,333 w |

' + 'doubling speed requires worked example 2 — hydroelectric power: 313.9 mw 282.5 mw 275 mw worked example 3 — led vs incandescent cost: rs 1,051 rs 175 savings 146 kwh rs 876/year the carnot cycle ~25% ~33% ~60% coefficient of performance (cop) 10.1 exergy second-law efficiency hydroelectric power 40% of india's total hydroelectric output how it works (energy conservation in action): solar energy biomass hydropower calculation: p = eta x rho x g x q x h worked example — kopili hep: 282 mw worked example — rooftop solar in guwahati: 2,000 w (2 kw) 9 kwh/day 270 kwh/month 150 kwh worked example — biogas from cattle dung: 0.86 mj = 0.24 kwh 2.4 kwh/day renewable sources comparison: capacity factor levelized cost of energy (lcoe) the intermittency challenge: pumped-storage hydro green hydrogen energy-and-work energy & work engineering kinetic energy potential energy conservation of energy efficiency honey-hunters-lesson kite-festival the-little-boat ferrymans-riddle forms of energy energybarchartdiagram energy is the capacity to do work — to push, pull, heat, light, or move something. think of energy as money: you can hold it in different currencies, spend it in different ways, but the total amount in your wallet only changes when you earn or pay.

 your body, a bicycle, a river, a bonfire — they all run on energy. the trick is recognising which form it takes:

 | form | what it is | everyday example |
 |------|-----------|------------------|
 | kinetic | energy of motion | a flowing brahmaputra, a spinning bicycle wheel |
 | gravitational pe | stored by height above ground | a mango hanging on a tree in jorhat |
 | chemical pe | stored in molecular bonds | rice, petrol, firewood, your lunch |
 | elastic pe | stored in stretched/compressed objects | a drawn bamboo bow, a compressed spring |
 | thermal | random motion of molecules | a hot cup of assam tea |
 | electrical | moving electrons through conductors | current flowing through a guwahati street light |
 | nuclear | bonds holding atomic nuclei together | the sun, nuclear power plants |
 | electromagnetic | energy carried by waves | sunlight, radio waves, x-rays |

 the key insight: energy is always converting from one form to another. the sun's nuclear energy becomes light (electromagnetic), which plants absorb to make food (chemical pe), which you eat and burn to run (kinetic + thermal). nothing is created or destroyed — just transformed.

 try the diagram above — toggle between a falling mango, a pendulum, and a roller coaster to see how pe and ke bars trade places at each position.

 quick check: a honey hunter in a karbi anglong forest climbs 15 metres up a tree. does he gain or lose gravitational pe?

 he gains pe — the higher he climbs, the more stored energy he has relative to the ground. if he slips, all that pe converts to ke on the way down. the two energy formulas you must know:

 | quantity | formula | units | what each variable means |
 |----------|---------|-------|-------------------------|
 | kinetic energy | ke = ½mv² | joules (j) | m = mass (kg), v = velocity (m/s) |
 | gravitational pe | pe = mgh | joules (j) | m = mass (kg), g = 9.81 m/s², h = height (m) |

 worked example 1 — falling mango:
 a 0.2 kg mango hangs 10 m above the ground.
 - pe at top = mgh = 0.2 x 9.81 x 10 = 19.6 j
 - just before hitting the ground (h = 0): all pe has become ke
 - speed: v = sqrt(2 x 19.6 / 0.2) = 14.0 m/s (about 50 km/h!)

 worked example 2 — why speed matters more than mass:

 | vehicle | mass (kg) | speed (km/h) | speed (m/s) | ke (j) |
 |---------|-----------|-------------|-------------|--------|
 | bicycle | 80 | 20 | 5.6 | 1,254 |
 | car | 1,000 | 50 | 13.9 | 96,605 |
 | same car | 1,000 | 100 | 27.8 | 386,420 |
 | truck | 10,000 | 50 | 13.9 | 966,050 |

 notice: doubling the car's speed from 50 to 100 km/h quadruples the ke (because v is squared). this is why high-speed crashes are so much more devastating — and why speed limits exist.

 energy density — how much energy is packed into different fuels:

 | fuel | energy density | comparison |
 |------|---------------|------------|
 | uranium-235 | 82,000,000 mj/kg | 2 million x petrol |
 | petrol | 46 mj/kg | baseline |
 | chocolate bar | 22 mj/kg | half of petrol |
 | wood / bamboo | 16 mj/kg | common in rural assam |
 | li-ion battery | 0.5 mj/kg | 90x less than petrol |

 this table explains why electric cars need massive battery packs — batteries store far less energy per kilogram than liquid fuels. the lagrangian formulation replaces newton's f = ma with a single elegant function:

 l = t - v (kinetic minus potential energy)

 all equations of motion follow from the euler-lagrange equation:

 d/dt(dl/dqdot) - dl/dq = 0

 this works in any coordinate system and naturally handles constraints — far more powerful than tracking forces.

 noether's theorem (1918) — the deepest connection in physics:

 | symmetry | conserved quantity |
 |----------|-------------------|
 | time-translation (physics is the same today as yesterday) | energy |
 | space-translation (physics is the same here as there) | momentum |
 | rotational (physics is the same in every direction) | angular momentum |

 energy conservation is not an accident — it is a consequence of time-translation symmetry. if the laws of physics changed over time, energy would not be conserved.

 this actually happens in cosmology: the expansion of the universe breaks time-translation symmetry. photon energy decreases with cosmological redshift — and that energy is genuinely "lost," not transferred elsewhere.

 the hamiltonian h = t + v governs quantum evolution via the schrodinger equation: ihbar dpsi/dt = h psi. energy sits at the centre of both classical and quantum physics. match each energy form to its real-world example kinetic energy a brahmaputra river ferry moving at 15 km/h gravitational pe water stored behind the k

title: Engineering Design Process
tagline: How engineers solve problems — from defining the challenge to building, testing, and improving.
searchText:
engineering design process how engineers solve problems — from defining the challenge to building, testing, and improving. the engineering design loop match each design stage to its core activity materials and trade-offs structural engineering basics mechanical systems — the machines inside machines engineering in north-east india match each ne india engineering example to its key principle define the problem research brainstorm prototype test improve decision matrix 67 points failure mode and effects analysis (fmea) strength vs. weight cost vs. durability sustainability vs. performance 264 kn·m/kg tension compression bending moment load distribution triangles are the strongest shape 200 mpa lever class 1 lever class 2 lever class 3 lever gears gear ratio linkages living root bridges bioengineering bamboo construction narrow-gauge railways bio-integrated design engineering-design engineering design process engineering how engineers solve problems — from defining the challenge to building, testing, and improving. bridge-that-grew little-train boy-who-built-a-library the engineering design loop engineering is not about finding the one "right answer" — it is about finding the best workable solution given real constraints of time, money, materials, and physics. the engineering design process is an iterative loop with six core stages: define the problem (what exactly needs to be solved, and for whom?), research (what solutions already exist, what are the constraints?), brainstorm (generate many possible solutions without judging them), prototype (build a simplified version of the most promising idea), test (does the prototype actually work? measure its performance against specific criteria), and improve (use test results to refine the design, then cycle back through prototyping and testing).

the crucial word here is iterative. engineers rarely get it right on the first try. the wright brothers tested over 200 wing shapes in their wind tunnel before settling on the design that flew at kitty hawk. thomas edison famously tested thousands of filament materials before finding one that made a practical light bulb. each failure is not a setback but data — it tells you what does not work and narrows the search space. the design loop is meant to be repeated: prototype, test, learn, improve, repeat.

defining the problem well is often the hardest and most important step. a poorly defined problem leads to elegant solutions for the wrong question. "build a bridge" is vague. "build a pedestrian bridge spanning a 15-metre river gap that can support 500 kg, costs under ₹2 lakh, uses locally available materials, and can be constructed by a team of four in under two weeks" — that is an engineering problem definition. every constraint (budget, materials, time, load capacity) shapes the solution space. students often want to jump straight to building, but experienced engineers spend the majority of their time in the define-and-research phases because the cost of changing a design increases dramatically the further along you are. quantifying design requirements uses measurable criteria. for a pedestrian bridge: load capacity ≥ 500 kg (5 people × 100 kg), span = 15 m, deflection < span/300 = 50 mm under full load, materials budget ≤ ₹2 lakh, construction time ≤ 14 days, lifespan ≥ 20 years. a decision matrix scores each design against weighted criteria: if safety is weighted 5, cost weighted 3, and aesthetics weighted 1, a design scoring (8, 6, 9) totals 5×8 + 3×6 + 1×9 = 67 points. the highest-scoring design wins — but judgment is still needed for criteria that resist quantification. design for manufacturing (dfm) and design for assembly (dfa) are advanced methodologies that optimize products for efficient production. dfm minimizes machining operations, standardizes fasteners, and avoids tight tolerances where unnecessary — each tolerance reduction of 0.1 mm can increase manufacturing cost by 10-30%. failure mode and effects analysis (fmea) systematically identifies what could go wrong, rates each failure mode by severity, occurrence probability, and detectability, then multiplies these to get a risk priority number (rpn). high-rpn failures get redesigned. the aerospace and automotive industries require fmea before any design is approved for production. match each design stage to its core activity define the problem identify what needs to be solved, for whom, and under what constraints study existing solutions, materials, physics, and user needs brainstorm generate many possible ideas without immediate judgment build a simplified, testable version of the best idea measure performance against specific criteria and requirements use test data to refine the design and iterate materials and trade-offs every engineering decision involves trade-offs — you cannot optimize every property simultaneously. a material that is incredibly strong may also be incredibly heavy. a cheap material may corrode quickly. a lightweight material may lack the rigidity needed for structural applications. understanding these trade-offs is central to engineering thinking.

strength vs. weight: steel is very strong (high tensile strength of around 400–550 mpa for structural steel) but heavy (density 7,850 kg/m³). aluminium is about one-third the weight of steel but also about one-third the strength, so the strength-to-weight ratio is similar. carbon fiber composites offer the best strength-to-weight ratio but are expensive and difficult to repair. bamboo — abundant in ne india — has a tensile strength comparable to mild steel (around 140–230 mpa) but weighs far less. this is why bamboo has been used as scaffolding and construction material across asia for millennia.

cost vs. durability: concrete is cheap and durable in compression but weak in tension (about 1/10th its compressive strength), which is why it is reinforced with steel rebar. stainless steel resists corrosion beautifully but costs 3–5 times more than carbon steel. in ne india's humid, monsoon-heavy climate, corrosion resistance is not a luxury — it is a necessity. bridges and buildings that would last decades in dry climates may deteriorate in years if the wrong materials are chosen.

sustainability vs. performance: modern engineering increasingly considers environmental impact. concrete production accounts for about 8% of global co₂ emissions. bamboo, by contrast, is a rapidly renewable resource that sequesters carbon as it grows (a bamboo grove absorbs more co₂ per hectare than most tree forests). using bamboo where possible — for scaffolding, housing frames, and even reinforcement in concrete — reduces the carbon footprint of construction. the trade-off is that bamboo requires treatment to resist insects and moisture, and its properties are less uniform than manufactured materials. engineering is the art of navigating these trade-offs to find the solution that best satisfies all constraints simultaneously. comparing materials quantitatively: specific strength = tensile strength / density. mild steel: 400 mpa / 7,850 kg/m³ = 51 kn·m/kg. aluminium 6061: 310/2,700 = 115 kn·m/kg. bamboo (along grain): 185/700 = 264 kn·m/kg — over 5× steel's specific strength. carbon fiber: 3,500/1,600 = 2,188 kn·m/kg — but costs ₹3,000/kg vs bamboo at ₹5/kg. the ashby chart plots strength vs density for all engineering materials, revealing clusters and trade-off frontiers that guide material selection for any application. smart materials respond to environmental stimuli: shape memory alloys (nitinol) return to a preset shape when heated — used in stents, eyeglass frames, and satellite antennas. piezoelectric materials (pzt, quartz) generate voltage when stressed and deform when voltage is applied — used in sensors, actuators, and energy harvesting from vibrations. self-healing polymers contain microcapsules of adhesive that rupture when cracked, automatically repairing damage — inspired by biological wound healing. auxetic materials have negativ

title: Environmental Science & Pollution
tagline: Air, water, soil — how pollution affects ecosystems and what we can do about it.
searchText:
environmental science & pollution air, water, soil — how pollution affects ecosystems and what we can do about it. air pollution water pollution soil contamination plastic and waste management solutions — renewable energy and community action match each solution to its environmental impact particulate matter nitrogen oxides sulfur dioxide carbon monoxide ground-level ozone volatile organic compounds guwahati air quality index (aqi) pm2.5 health impact source apportionment photochemical smog hysplit satellite remote sensing health impact assessment industrial effluent agricultural runoff sewage plastic waste brahmaputra river eutrophication heavy metals pesticides petroleum hydrocarbons plastic microparticles bioremediation soil capping phytoremediation microplastics nanoplastics mawlynnong reduce reuse recycle compost renewable energy community resource management community forests environmental-science environmental science & pollution conservation air, water, soil — how pollution affects ecosystems and what we can do about it. girl-grew-forest elephant-corridor cloud-refused-rain air pollution carboncyclediagram air pollution is the contamination of the atmosphere by harmful substances — gases, particulate matter, and biological molecules — at concentrations that damage health, ecosystems, or materials. the major air pollutants include particulate matter (pm10 and pm2.5 — tiny particles less than 10 or 2.5 micrometres in diameter), nitrogen oxides (nox, from vehicle engines and power plants), sulfur dioxide (so₂, from burning coal and industrial processes), carbon monoxide (co, from incomplete combustion), ground-level ozone (o₃, formed when nox and volatile organic compounds react in sunlight), and volatile organic compounds (vocs, from paints, solvents, and vehicle emissions).

pm2.5 is particularly dangerous because particles that small can penetrate deep into the lungs, enter the bloodstream, and reach every organ. long-term pm2.5 exposure is linked to heart disease, stroke, lung cancer, and chronic respiratory diseases. the world health organization recommends pm2.5 levels stay below 5 μg/m³ annually, but most indian cities far exceed this. guwahati, the largest city in ne india, regularly records pm2.5 levels of 50–100 μg/m³ during winter months, driven by vehicle emissions (the number of registered vehicles has doubled in the past decade), brick kilns along the city's outskirts, construction dust, and biomass burning. the city's geography — a valley surrounded by hills — traps pollutants under temperature inversions, where a layer of warm air prevents vertical mixing.

sources of air pollution range from local (vehicle exhaust, cooking with wood and coal, brick kilns, garbage burning) to regional (crop residue burning in agricultural areas, forest fires) to global (industrial emissions carried by wind patterns). in ne india, seasonal slash-and-burn agriculture (jhum cultivation) contributes to air pollution spikes between february and april, when farmers clear forested hillsides by burning. while jhum has been practiced sustainably for centuries at low population densities, increasing population pressure has shortened fallow periods and increased the total area burned, intensifying air quality impacts. air quality is measured by the air quality index (aqi), which converts concentrations of pm2.5, pm10, no₂, so₂, co, and o₃ into a single 0–500 scale. an aqi above 100 is unhealthy for sensitive groups; above 300 is hazardous for all. the pm2.5 health impact is quantified by epidemiological dose-response functions: each 10 μg/m³ increase in annual pm2.5 exposure raises all-cause mortality by approximately 6–8% (global burden of disease studies). india’s annual excess deaths attributable to air pollution exceed 1.6 million. source apportionment studies use chemical fingerprinting (measuring trace metals, organic markers, and isotope ratios in filter samples) to identify contributions from vehicles, biomass burning, industry, and dust. in guwahati, vehicle emissions and brick kilns dominate winter pm2.5, while agricultural burning dominates in spring. atmospheric chemistry governs pollutant transformations. photochemical smog forms when nox and vocs react in sunlight via a radical chain mechanism: no₂ + hν → no + o; o + o₂ → o₃ (ground-level ozone). vocs (from vehicles, solvents, vegetation) regenerate no₂ from no, sustaining the cycle. the hysplit (hybrid single-particle lagrangian integrated trajectory) model traces air-mass back-trajectories, revealing that winter pollution episodes in guwahati coincide with westerly transport of indo-gangetic plain pollutants. satellite remote sensing (sentinel-5p/tropomi) maps no₂, so₂, co, ch₄, and aerosol columns at 3.5×7 km resolution daily, enabling near-real-time pollution monitoring. health impact assessment combines exposure modelling, population data, and concentration-response functions to estimate disease burden: the global burden of disease 2019 ranked air pollution as the leading environmental risk factor, responsible for 6.7 million deaths annually worldwide. true-false pm2.5 particles are small enough to pass from the lungs into the bloodstream. at less than 2.5 micrometres (about 30 times thinner than a human hair), pm2.5 penetrates deep into the lungs and can enter the blood, reaching every organ. the who recommended annual pm2.5 limit is 25 μg/m³. the who tightened its guideline in 2021 to just 5 μg/m³ annually — most indian cities exceed this by 10-20 times. guwahati's valley geography helps disperse air pollution. the opposite — the surrounding hills and temperature inversions trap pollutants in the valley, making air quality worse. ground-level ozone is formed by chemical reactions between nox and vocs in sunlight. unlike stratospheric ozone (which protects us from uv), ground-level ozone is a pollutant that irritates lungs and damages crops. water pollution watercyclediagram water pollution occurs when harmful substances contaminate rivers, lakes, groundwater, or oceans, degrading water quality and harming aquatic life and human health. the major sources include industrial effluent (heavy metals, chemicals, and heated water discharged by factories), agricultural runoff (fertilizers, pesticides, and animal waste), sewage (untreated or partially treated human waste), and plastic waste.

the brahmaputra river, one of the world's great rivers and the lifeline of assam, faces multiple pollution pressures. oil refineries at noonmati (guwahati) and numaligarh discharge treated (but not pristine) effluent. paper mills at jagiroad and panchgram release lignin, chlorine compounds, and organic waste. urban sewage from guwahati, dibrugarh, tezpur, and other cities enters the river largely untreated — guwahati alone generates about 270 million litres of sewage daily, but its treatment capacity handles only a fraction. agricultural runoff carrying fertilizers and pesticides from the vast tea gardens and rice paddies of the brahmaputra valley adds nutrient loading that can cause eutrophication — excessive algal growth that depletes dissolved oxygen, suffocating fish.

water pollution has direct health consequences. contaminated drinking water causes diarrheal diseases, which kill an estimated 1,200 children under five in india every day. arsenic contamination of groundwater, caused by naturally occurring arsenic dissolving from sediments, affects parts of the brahmaputra flood plain — some tube wells in the barak valley have arsenic levels exceeding the who safe limit of 10 μg/l by ten times or more. fluoride contamination in some areas of karbi anglong causes dental and skeletal fluorosis. these are not abstract problems — they affect real communities in ne india, and solving them requires a combination of monitoring, treatment infrastructure, and community awareness. soil contamination salinisationdiagram soil is a living ecosystem — a single teaspoon of healthy soil contains more micro

de-extinction debate: evolution-and-natural-selection evolution & natural selection s one-horned rhino.", relatedstories: [ ], understand: [ // ── section 1: darwin darwin's theory of evolution grassrhinohabitatdiagram in 1831, a young english naturalist named charles darwin sailed on hms beagle for a five-year voyage around the world. on the galapagos islands, he noticed that finches on different islands had distinctly different beak shapes — thick for cracking seeds, slender for probing flowers, sharp for catching insects — yet they were clearly related. how could such diversity arise among closely related birds?

 darwin spent two decades developing his answer, publishing on the origin of species in 1859. his theory rests on four pillars:

 | pillar | what it means | animal example |
 |--------|--------------|----------------|
 | variation | individuals differ in measurable ways | kaziranga's one-horned rhinos vary in horn size, skin fold depth, and body mass |
 | inheritance | offspring resemble their parents | a hoolock gibbon's long arms are passed from parent to offspring |
 | overproduction | far more offspring are born than can survive | a female golden mahseer lays 30,000 eggs per season, but only a handful survive to adulthood |
 | differential survival | better-suited individuals survive and reproduce more | rhinos with thicker skin folds may better resist parasites and thorny vegetation |

 the kaziranga rhino story: the indian one-horned rhinoceros (rhinoceros unicornis) was hunted to fewer than 200 individuals by the early 1900s. today, kaziranga national park in assam shelters over 2,600 — about two-thirds of the world population. explore the diagram above to see how this grassland habitat supports the rhino.

 over many generations, traits that help survival become more common, while less useful traits fade. darwin called this natural selection — nature does the selecting, not a conscious breeder. given enough time (thousands to millions of generations), natural selection can transform one species into another.

 | common misconception | reality |
 |---------------------|---------|
 | "evolution has a goal" | no — evolution has no direction or purpose; it responds to current conditions |
 | "organisms try to evolve" | no — individuals don't change; populations change over generations |
 | "survival of the fittest means strongest" | "fittest" means best-suited to the environment — a small, camouflaged frog is fitter than a large, visible one if predators hunt by sight |
 | "humans evolved from monkeys" | humans and monkeys share a common ancestor; neither "became" the other | quantifying fitness

 darwin's "fitness" can be measured precisely:

 | term | symbol | definition | example |
 |------|--------|------------|---------|
 | absolute fitness | w | average offspring produced by a genotype | green beetle: w = 3; brown beetle: w = 5 |
 | relative fitness | w | w / wmax (normalised to fittest) | wgreen = 3/5 = 0.6; wbrown = 1.0 |
 | selection coefficient | s | 1 − w (disadvantage of less-fit genotype) | sgreen = 1 − 0.6 = 0.4 |

 worked example — how fast can selection work?

 the time for a beneficial allele to go from 1% to 99% frequency:

 t ≈ (2/s) × ln(99)

 | selection coefficient (s) | time to fixation (generations) | in years (if generation = 5 yr) |
 |--------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------------|
 | 0.01 (weak) | 920 | 4,600 years |
 | 0.05 (moderate) | 184 | 920 years |
 | 0.10 (strong) | 92 | 460 years |

 even "weak" selection (s = 0.01) drives an allele to fixation in under 5,000 years — fast on

title: Fluid Mechanics
searchText:
fluid mechanics bernoulli viscosity reynolds number pressure buoyancy archimedes navier-stokes laminar turbulent pressure in fluids buoyancy & archimedes\ bernoulli\ match each bernoulli phenomenon to its explanation viscosity & drag forces match each drag concept to its description hydraulic systems pressure = force / area 5,890,000 depth p = ρgh 98,100 pa pascal's principle why does the brahmaputra's embankment need to be thickest at the base? the complete hydrostatic pressure formula p = p_atm + ρgh worked example — hydraulic press: 2,000 n 40× gauge pressure 46.7 psi navier-stokes equations /∂t + ) = −∇p + μ∇² + ρ millennium prize problems /∂t) | local acceleration | unsteady flow changes |
' + '| ρ( | viscous diffusion | internal friction between layers |
' + '| ρ | body force | gravity pulling fluid down |

' + 'the hydrostatic paradox: buoyant force but steel ships float! on the brahmaputra: archimedes' formula: f_buoyant = ρ_fluid × v_displaced × g 8.3% worked example — will the brahmaputra ferry float safely? 120 tonnes 35,000 kg submarines salt water vs fresh water: plimsoll line metacentric height (gm) shifts to the side with more submerged volume. the τ = δ × gm × sin θ gz curve added mass rayleigh-plesset equation when a fluid speeds up, its pressure drops. p + ½ρv² + ρgh = constant analogy: where you see bernoulli's principle every day: the continuity equation a₁v₁ = a₂v₂ worked example — water in a narrowing pipe: 8 m/s 30,000 pa = 30 kpa venturi meter worked example — airplane wing lift: 1,920 pa 1,036,800 n ≈ 106 tonnes flow rate and the continuity equation: 24,000 m³/s steady, inviscid, incompressible lift — the full picture: kutta-joukowski theorem l' = ρv∞γ circulation kutta condition cfd (computational fluid dynamics) "thickness." temperature changes everything: drag viscous drag pressure drag square terminal velocity the drag equation: f_d = ½ρv²c_da worked example — drag on a car at different speeds: 93.0 worked example — terminal velocity of a raindrop: 6.5 m/s linear stokes' law: f_d = 6πμrv v_t = 2r²(ρ_s − ρ_f)g / (9μ) the drag crisis blasius solution law of the wall small piston large piston force multiplication = area of large piston / area of small piston everyday example — car brakes: master cylinder slave cylinders across india's construction sites worked example — hydraulic jack: 500 n worked example — car braking system: 400 n ~5,000 n 20,000 n why brake fluid matters: real-world hydraulic engineering hagen-poiseuille equation q = πr⁴δp / (8μl) 16× electrohydraulic servo valves micrometres hydraulics vs pneumatics: fluid-mechanics fluid mechanics engineering reynolds number archimedes navier-stokes the-little-boat ferrymans-riddle lotus-float siang-river pressure in fluids pressuredepthdiagram imagine standing at the bottom of a swimming pool. you feel a squeezing sensation on your eardrums. that squeeze is pressure — the force the water pushes on every square centimetre of your body.

 pressure = force / area, measured in pascals (pa), where 1 pa = 1 n/m².

 think of it like this: a woman in stiletto heels exerts more pressure on the floor than an elephant, because all her weight is concentrated on two tiny heel tips. the elephant spreads its weight across four huge feet.

 | scenario | force (n) | area (m²) | pressure (pa) |
 |----------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
 | elephant standing (4,000 kg) | 39,240 | 0.12 (4 feet) | 327,000 |
 | woman in stilettos (60 kg) | 589 | 0.0001 (2 heels) | 5,890,000 |
 | lying flat on ground (70 kg) | 687 | 0.5 | 1,374 |

 in a fluid at rest, pressure depends on depth: the deeper you go, the more fluid sits above you, and the heavier that column becomes. the formula is:

 > p = ρgh (density x gravity x depth)

 water has a density of about 1,000 kg/m³. at 10 metres depth: p = 1,000 x 9.81 x 10 = 98,100 pa — nearly one whole atmosphere of extra pressure on top of the air pressure above the surface. this is why your ears hurt when you dive to the bottom of a deep pool.

 pascal's principle says: pressure applied to an enclosed fluid transmits equally in all directions. squeeze a sealed water balloon at one point, and the pressure increases everywhere inside — not just where you squeezed. this is the foundation of every hydraulic system, from car brakes to the excavators building roads across assam.

 why does the brahmaputra's embankment need to be thickest at the base? because the water pressure at the bottom is far greater than at the top (h is largest there). a 5-metre-deep section pushes with 49,050 pa — five times more than the 1-metre level. the complete hydrostatic pressure formula includes atmospheric pressure:

 > p = patm + ρgh

 | depth in fresh water | gauge pressure (ρgh) | absolute pressure (patm + ρgh) | in atmospheres |
 |---------------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|----------------|
 | 0 m (surface) | 0 pa | 101,325 pa | 1.0 atm |
 | 5 m | 49,050 pa | 150,375 pa | 1.5 atm |
 | 10 m | 98,100 pa | 199,425 pa | 2.0 atm |
 | 30 m (recreational dive limit) | 294,300 pa | 395,625 pa | 3.9 atm |
 | 100 m | 981,000 pa | 1,082,325 pa | 10.7 atm |

 every 10 m of water adds roughly 1 atmosphere. scuba divers must equalise ear pressure because the eardrum has air on one side and water on the other — the pressure difference can rupture it.

 worked example — hydraulic press:

 a small piston has area a₁ = 5 cm² and a large piston has area a₂ = 200 cm². you push with 50 n on the small piston.

 | step | calculation | result |
 |------|------------|--------|
 | pressure created | p = f₁/a₁ = 50 / 5×10⁻⁴ | 100,000 pa |
 | force on large piston | f₂ = p × a₂ = 100,000 × 200×10⁻⁴ | 2,000 n |
 | force multiplication | f₂/f₁ = a₂/a₁ = 200/5 | 40× |
 | distance trade-off | d₁ = d₂ × (a₂/a₁) = 0.01 × 40 | 0.40 m |
 | work in | 50 × 0.40 | 20 j |
 | work out | 2,000 × 0.01 | 20 j ✓ |

 energy is conserved: you get 40× the force but must push 40× further. gauge pressure (p − patm) is what instruments read: a car tyre at "32 psi" means 32 psi above atmospheric (absolute = 32 + 14.7 = 46.7 psi). navier-stokes equations — the complete description of pressure in a moving fluid:

 > ρ(∂v/∂t + v·∇v) = −∇p + μ∇²v + ρg

 this combines momentum conservation with viscous forces. these equations have no general closed-form solution and are one of the millennium prize problems ($1 million for proving existence and smoothness of solutions in 3d).

 | term | physical meaning | contribution |
 |------|-----------------|-------------|
 | ρ(∂v/∂t) | local acceleration | unsteady flow changes |
 | ρ(v·∇v) | convective acceleration | fluid speeding up as it moves |
 | −∇p | pressure gradient force | fluid flows from high to low p |
 | μ∇²v | viscous diffusion | internal friction between layers |
 | ρg | body force | gravity pulling fluid down |

 the reynolds number re = ρvl/μ determines flow regime:

 | re range (pipe flow) | flow type | character |
 |---------------------|-----------|----------|
 | re < 2,300 | laminar | smooth, orderly layers |
 | 2,300 < re < 4,000 | transitional | intermittent turbulent bursts |
 | re > 4,000 | turbulent | chaotic, mixing eddies |

 turbulence remains one of the last great unsolved problems in classical physics — feynman called it "the most important unsolved problem of classical physics."

 hydrostatic paradox: the pressure at the bottom of a container depends only on depth, not on the total volume of fluid. a tall thin tube and a wide lake at the same depth have identical bottom pressure. at the bottom of the mariana trench (10,900 m), the pressure reaches 1,100 atm (110 mpa), compressing water to 5% greater density than at the surface. true-false water pressure at a given depth depends on the shape of the container. the hydrostatic paradox: pressure depends only on depth (p = ρgh), not on container shape or total water volume. at 10 m depth

searchText:

trophic-dynamic model (1942) derivation — maximum food chain length: worked example: keystone species keystone herbivore asian elephants ecosystem engineers trophic cascades — when predators control bioaccumulation biomagnification how it works step by step: worked example — mercury in a river food web: biomagnification factor (bmf) worked example — ddt in a grassland food web: trophic magnification factor (tmf) bioconcentration factor (bcf) pharmacokinetic modelling of bioaccumulation: worked example — methylmercury in a river system: the inverted pyramid explained: productivity standing stock phumdi comparing energy budgets — terrestrial vs. aquatic: worked example — wetland energy budget: the microbial loop: microbial loop odum's energy flow model (1957) — silver springs, florida: net ecosystem production (nep): nutrients cycle decomposition — the engine of recycling: the q10 rule and decomposition rates: worked example — climate warming and soil carbon: soil carbon feedback nutrient stoichiometry — the redfield ratio: redfield ratio decomposition kinetics — the exponential decay model: worked example — leaf litter decay: the humification index: humus food-webs-energy-flow food webs & energy flow who eats whom — how energy flows from sunlight through every living thing. kaziranga-grass elephant-corridor golden-hilsa producers, consumers, and decomposers grassrhinohabitatdiagram match each role to its definition producers (autotrophs) capture solar energy through photosynthesis — grasses, algae, phytoplankton primary consumers (herbivores) eat producers directly — rhinos, deer, buffalo secondary consumers (carnivores) eat herbivores — tigers, leopards, fishing cats tertiary consumers (apex predators) top of the chain — feed on other carnivores decomposers break down dead matter, recycling nutrients — fungi, bacteria, earthworms every ecosystem runs on a single energy source: sunlight. think of an ecosystem like a factory. the factory needs raw materials (sunlight, water, co2), workers at different levels, and a cleanup crew. without any one group, the whole operation shuts down.

 producers are the factory's first shift. green plants, algae, and cyanobacteria capture sunlight through photosynthesis and convert it into glucose — chemical energy that every other organism depends on. in a floodplain grassland, towering elephant grass (saccharum ravennae) converts monsoon sunlight and nutrient-rich river silt into biomass. aquatic algae and phytoplankton in wetlands form a parallel base of production underwater.

 | role | examples | energy source | trophic level |
 |------|----------|---------------|---------------|
 | producer (autotroph) | elephant grass, rice paddy, river algae | sunlight via photosynthesis | 1 |
 | primary consumer (herbivore) | one-horned rhino, swamp deer, hog deer | eating producers | 2 |
 | secondary consumer (carnivore) | bengal tiger, leopard, fishing cat | eating herbivores | 3 |
 | tertiary consumer (apex) | pallas's fish eagle, large crocodile | eating other carnivores | 4 |
 | decomposer | fungi, bacteria, dung beetles | dead organic matter | all levels |

 analogy: imagine a relay race. producers are the first runners — they receive the baton (energy) from the sun. they pass it to herbivores, who pass it to carnivores. decomposers are the cleanup crew that picks up dropped batons and returns them to the starting line (soil), so the next race (growing season) can begin.

 primary consumers eat producers directly. the indian one-horned rhinoceros can eat over 50 kg of grass per day. wild water buffalo, swamp deer, and hog deer graze the same grasslands. each species eats slightly different plants or plant parts, reducing direct competition — a pattern called resource partitioning.

 secondary consumers eat herbivores. bengal tigers prey on deer and young buffalo. fishing cats at deepor beel hunt fish and frogs. tertiary consumers sit at the top — large raptors that eat fish that ate insects that ate algae.

 | decomposer type | what it breaks down | how fast? | nutrients released |
 |-----------------|--------------------|-----------|-----------------|
 | bacteria | soft tissues, sugars, proteins | hours to days | nitrogen, phosphorus |
 | fungi | tough cellulose, lignin in wood | weeks to months | carbon, phosphorus |
 | earthworms | leaf litter, soil organic matter | days to weeks | nitrogen, calcium |
 | dung beetles | animal waste | hours to days | nitrogen, phosphorus |
 | termites | dead wood, fallen trees | months to years | carbon, nitrogen |

 decomposers are the unsung heroes. without fungi, bacteria, earthworms, and dung beetles, dead matter would pile up and nutrients would stay locked in corpses forever. in warm, humid floodplain environments, decomposition is fast: a fallen tree can become soil in just a few years, recycling carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus back into the ecosystem.

 check yourself: a patch of grassland produces 10,000 kg of plant biomass per year. if herbivores convert 10% to animal biomass, and carnivores convert 10% of that, how many kg of carnivore biomass can the patch support? (answer: 100 kg.) ecologists quantify food-web structure with precise metrics. these numbers tell you how complex and stable a web is.

 | metric | formula | what it measures | typical range |
 |--------|---------|------------------|---------------|
 | connectance (c) | c = l / s^2 | fraction of possible links that exist | 0.03 – 0.30 |
 | link density (ld) | ld = l / s | average links per species | 1.5 – 5.0 |
 | trophic level (tl) | tl = 1 + mean tl of prey | position in the web | 1.0 – 5.0 |
 | omnivory index | variance of prey tls | how broadly a species feeds | 0 – 2.0 |

 where l = number of feeding links, s = number of species.

 worked example — bengal tiger trophic level:

 a tiger eats swamp deer (tl 2.0), wild boar (tl 2.3, partly omnivorous), and hog deer (tl 2.0).

 tiger tl = 1 + mean(2.0, 2.3, 2.0) = 1 + 2.1 = 3.1

 if the tiger also occasionally eats a fishing cat (tl 3.0):

 tiger tl = 1 + mean(2.0, 2.3, 2.0, 3.0) = 1 + 2.325 = 3.325

 this confirms tigers sit at roughly tl 3.1–3.5 depending on diet composition.

 assimilation and production efficiencies differ dramatically by diet:

 | consumer type | assimilation efficiency | production efficiency | combined (ecological) |
 |----

' + '| volume (and mass) | l³ | halves this is why: 1/l worked example — scaling an ant: worked example — terminal velocity and survival: bone scaling: 7% of body mass kleiber's law metabolic rate ∝ m^0.75 0.75 (3/4) engineering implications: two motions happen simultaneously forward downward parabola kite festivals in assam: sweet spot key equations for projectile motion (no air resistance): r = v₀² sin 2θ / g h = v₀² sin²θ / (2g) t = 2v₀ sin θ / g worked example — cricket ball bowled at 140 km/h (38.9 m/s): 45° 154.4 38.6 5.6 θ = 45° worked example — how far can you throw from the top of a guwahati hill? air resistance with drag vs without drag (cricket ball at 40 m/s, 45°): 82 m the magnus effect — why balls curve: always equals conservation of momentum woodpeckers — masters of controlled collision: increase the collision time to reduce the peak force everyday collisions on ne india roads: for elastic collisions in 1d special cases: worked example — woodpecker impact force: worked example — newton's cradle (5 balls): momentum kinetic energy yes head injury criterion (hic) rigid hammer tiny brain mass 2d collisions — scattering: impact parameter rutherford scattering formula forces-and-motion forces & motion newton's laws, gravity, friction, and why woodpeckers don't get headaches. woodpeckers-drum the-little-boat kite-festival flying-squirrel lotus-float ferrymans-riddle s first law ───────────────────────────── { title: s first law — inertia imagine you are sitting on a ferry crossing the brahmaputra. the ferry is moving steadily. you don't feel any motion — you could close your eyes and think you were sitting on the bank. but when the ferry suddenly slows to dock, you lurch forward. why? because your body was moving with the ferry, and when the ferry stopped, your body wanted to keep going.

 this is newton's first law, also called the law of inertia: an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion keeps moving in a straight line at constant speed, unless a force acts on it.

 analogy: think of inertia as laziness — objects are "lazy" and resist any change. a parked bicycle doesn't start moving on its own. a rolling ball doesn't stop on its own (it's friction that stops it).

 | situation | what you observe | inertia explanation |
 |-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
 | bus brakes suddenly | you lurch forward | your body resists the change from moving to stopped |
 | tablecloth trick | plates stay on table | plates resist being set in motion |
 | shaking a wet umbrella | water droplets fly off | droplets resist the change in direction |
 | coin on cardboard over glass | coin drops into glass when card is flicked | coin resists horizontal motion, gravity pulls it down |

 inertia depends on mass: heavier objects resist changes more. a loaded truck needs far more braking force than a bicycle at the same speed.

 | object | mass | ease of stopping at 30 km/h |
 |--------|------|----------------------------|
 | cricket ball | 0.16 kg | very easy — catch it with one hand |
 | bicycle + rider | 80 kg | moderate — squeeze both brakes |
 | auto-rickshaw | 400 kg | hard — needs good brakes |
 | loaded truck | 20,000 kg | very hard — long stopping distance |

 in space, where there is no friction or air resistance, a spacecraft launched at a certain speed will coast at that speed forever unless something pushes or pulls it. the voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is still drifting at 61,000 km/h with its engines off — pure inertia. newton's first law is really a special case of the second law when net force is zero: if σf = 0, then a = 0, meaning velocity stays constant (or zero).

 worked example — balanced forces on a ferry:

 a brahmaputra ferry (mass 5,000 kg) cruises at constant 8 m/s. the engine provides 3,000 n forward thrust. water drag = 2,400 n backward. air resistance = 600 n backward.

 σf = 3,000 − 2,400 − 600 = 0 n

 net force is zero, so acceleration is zero — the ferry moves at constant speed. this is newton's first law in action.

 now the engine cuts out:

 σf = 0 − 2,400 − 600 = −3,000 n
 a = f/m = −3,000 / 5,000 = −0.6 m/s²

 the ferry decelerates at 0.6 m/s². time to stop: t = v/a = 8/0.6 = 13.3 seconds.

 inertial vs non-inertial frames:

 | frame type | example | newton's 1st law holds? |
 |------------|---------|------------------------|
 | inertial | ground (approximately), a train at constant speed | yes |
 | non-inertial | accelerating car, sp

' + '| most mutations are neutral. silent a famous harmful mutation — sickle cell disease: carrier sickle cell disease the twist: mutations = raw material of evolution. effects of point mutations on proteins: missense nonsense frameshift the sickle cell mutation in molecular detail: heterozygote advantage (overdominance): highest balancing selection mutation rates and mechanisms: crispr-cas9 gene editing — precision surgery on dna: guide rna (grna) cas9 nuclease pam sequence repair pathways after the cut: nhej hdr next-generation editing tools: base editors prime editing epigenome editors crispr in agriculture (ne india relevance): genetics-and-dna genetics & dna the code of life — how dna determines whether a seed becomes bamboo or a banyan. coconut-jackfruit orchid-colors takin-face dna structure dnadoublehelixdiagram watch the dna helix spin in the diagram above. those two twisted strands running around each other — that's a double helix, the shape of every gene ever expressed, every trait ever inherited, every bit of biological information on earth. the structure was unknown until 1953. james watson and francis crick worked it out in a burst of insight (using crystallography images produced by rosalind franklin, who never received the nobel prize she should have shared).

 here's what should astonish you: the dna in one of your cells is 2 metres long. and you have 37 trillion cells. if you unspooled every strand of dna in your body and laid them end to end, you'd have enough dna to reach the sun — 370 times. all of it, in you, right now, carrying instructions written in a 4-letter alphabet.

 dna is a twisted ladder. the rails are sugar and phosphate groups. the rungs are pairs of chemical bases:

 | base | always pairs with | number of hydrogen bonds |
 |------|-------------------|-------------------------|
 | adenine (a) | thymine (t) | 2 |
 | guanine (g) | cytosine (c) | 3 |

 this pairing rule is absolute — a never pairs with c or g. if you know one side of the ladder, you can predict the other.

 analogy: think of a-t and g-c like matching puzzle pieces. only the right shapes click together.

 how much dna is inside you? each cell contains about 2 metres of dna — crammed into a nucleus just 6 micrometres (0.006 mm) across. that is like stuffing 40 kilometres of thread into a tennis ball. the trick? dna wraps around protein spools called histones, then coils and loops into tighter and tighter bundles.

 quick check: if one strand of dna reads a-t-g-c-c-a, what does the opposite strand read?

 answer: t-a-c-g-g-t — each base pairs with its partner.

 the sequence of bases along a dna strand is a code. a gene is a segment of dna whose base sequence tells the cell how to make a particular protein. the human genome has roughly 20,000–25,000 genes, but they make up only 1.5% of total dna. the rest includes regulatory switches, structural dna, and sequences still being studied.

 | dna component | % of human genome | what it does |
 |---------------|-------------------|--------------|
 | protein-coding genes | 1.5% | instructions for building proteins |
 | regulatory sequences | 8% | "on/off switches" that control when genes are active |
 | repetitive sequences | 45% | structural roles, some unknown functions |
 | other non-coding | 45.5% | still being researched |
 precise dimensions of the double helix:

 | property | value |
 |----------|-------|
 | width | 2 nm |
 | base pairs per full turn | 10 |
 | rise per turn (pitch) | 3.4 nm |
 | distance between base pairs | 0.34 nm |
 | human genome size | 3.2 billion base pairs |
 | chromosomes | 23 pairs (46 total) |

 the two strands run antiparallel — one goes 5' → 3' and the other 3' → 5' (the numbers refer to carbon atoms on the deoxyribose sugar). this matters for replication and transcription because enzymes can only read dna in the 5' → 3' direction.

 dna packaging — from string to spool:

 | level | structure | size |
 |-------|-----------|------|
 | naked dna | double helix | 2 nm wide |
 | nucleosome | 147 bp wrapped around histone octamer (2× h2a, h2b, h3, h4) | 11 nm |
 | beads on a string | nucleosomes linked by 50 bp linker dna | 11 nm |
 | 30 nm fibre | nucleosomes coil into a solenoid | 30 nm |
 | loops | attached to protein scaffold | 300 nm |
 | chromosome | maximum compaction during cell division | 1,400 nm |
