 if 3.2 billion bp are spa

title: Geology & Earth Science
tagline: The ground under your feet is moving, mountains are growing, and rocks tell stories.
searchText:
geology & earth science the ground under your feet is moving, mountains are growing, and rocks tell stories. the earth is an egg tectonic plates — the ground is moving under your feet mountains are still growing volcanoes — pressure cookers underground earthquakes — when plates get stuck then snap rocks tell stories — three types of rock match each rock type to its formation process fossils — nature\ erosion — water is patient and powerful freeze-thaw weathering the shell = the crust. the egg white = the mantle. convection the yolk = the core. check yourself: seismology mohorovičić discontinuity (moho) gutenberg discontinuity inge lehmann d″ layer magnetic field paleomagnetism 15 major tectonic plates pangaea convection currents divergent boundary convergent boundary transform boundary try this: 1 centimetre per year erosion tectonic uplift isostatic adjustment did you know? analogy: magma lava viscosity low viscosity high viscosity seismic waves focus epicenter p-waves (primary) s-waves (secondary) richter scale 32 times igneous rocks granite basalt obsidian sedimentary rocks sandstone limestone shale metamorphic rocks marble slate quartzite rock cycle sedimentary rock replace petrification trace fossils coprolites 3.5 billion years old water ice frost wedging wind gravity biological erosion expands when it freezes freeze-thaw weathering 207 mpa pried apart freezing point tensile strength frequency water availability rock porosity crack density frozen fringe saturation coefficient geology-and-earth geology & earth science the ground under your feet is moving, mountains are growing, and rocks tell stories. the earth is an egg earthlayersdiagram if you could slice the earth in half like a boiled egg, you would see layers.

the shell = the crust. this is the thin, solid outer layer where we live. under the continents, it is 30-70 km thick (continental crust). under the oceans, it is only 5-10 km thick (oceanic crust). compared to the whole earth, the crust is thinner than an eggshell is to an egg.

the egg white = the mantle. this is a 2,900 km thick layer of hot, dense rock. it is not liquid, but it is not quite solid either — over millions of years, it flows like extremely thick honey. this slow churning (called convection) is what drives the movement of tectonic plates on the surface. the upper mantle reaches temperatures of about 1,000 degrees celsius; the lower mantle exceeds 3,000 degrees celsius.

the yolk = the core. the outer core (2,200 km thick) is liquid iron and nickel at 4,000-5,000 degrees celsius. it is this swirling liquid metal that generates earth's magnetic field — the invisible shield that deflects solar radiation and makes compasses work. the inner core (1,200 km radius) is solid iron at over 5,000 degrees celsius — as hot as the surface of the sun. it is solid despite the extreme temperature because the pressure at the center of the earth is over 3.6 million times atmospheric pressure, which forces the atoms to pack tightly into a solid.

check yourself: the inner core is as hot as the surface of the sun. why is it solid? (answer: the immense pressure — 3.6 million atmospheres — forces atoms together so tightly that they cannot flow as a liquid.) earth’s internal structure is probed by seismology. p-waves travel through both solids and liquids; s-waves only through solids. a seismometer on the far side of the earth from an earthquake detects p-waves but not s-waves in a "shadow zone" between 104° and 140° angular distance, proving the outer core is liquid. deeper analysis reveals velocity discontinuities: the mohorovičić discontinuity (moho) at 5–70 km depth marks the crust–mantle boundary (p-wave velocity jumps from 6.5 to 8 km/s); the gutenberg discontinuity at 2,900 km marks the mantle–core boundary (p-wave velocity drops from 13.7 to 8 km/s as the medium changes from solid to liquid). the inner core was discovered by inge lehmann in 1936 from faint p-wave reflections within the shadow zone. modern seismic tomography constructs 3d maps of earth’s interior velocity structure from thousands of earthquake recordings, revealing mantle plumes (rising columns of hot material) and subducted slabs (cold, dense remnants of oceanic crust sinking to the core–mantle boundary). the d″ layer at the base of the mantle shows ultra-low velocity zones (ulvzs) interpreted as partial melt or iron-enriched silicates. earth’s magnetic field is generated by the geodynamo — convection of liquid iron in the outer core, modelled by solving the magnetohydrodynamic (mhd) equations. the field reverses polarity at irregular intervals (averaging 300,000 years); the last reversal (brunhes–matuyama) was 780,000 years ago. paleomagnetism — the record of ancient field orientation locked into cooling lava — provided some of the strongest evidence for continental drift and sea-floor spreading. tectonic plates — the ground is moving under your feet plateboundarydiagram the earth's crust is not a single unbroken shell. it is cracked into about 15 major tectonic plates that float on the semi-liquid mantle below, like crackers floating on soup. these plates are in constant motion — typically 2-10 centimetres per year, roughly the speed your fingernails grow.

that sounds slow, but over millions of years it reshapes the world. 200 million years ago, all the continents were joined in a single supercontinent called pangaea. tectonic movement broke it apart and pushed the pieces to where they are today. south america and africa were once joined — which is why their coastlines fit together like puzzle pieces and why you find identical fossils on both continents.

plates move because of convection currents in the mantle. hot rock rises from near the core, flows sideways under the crust, cools, and sinks back down. this circulation drags the plates along.

where plates meet, three things can happen:
- divergent boundary — plates pull apart, magma rises to fill the gap (mid-ocean ridges, like the mid-atlantic ridge)
- convergent boundary — plates push together, one slides under the other (subduction), creating mountains and deep ocean trenches
- transform boundary — plates slide past each other sideways (like the san andreas fault in california)

try this: take two sheets of paper and lay them flat on a table touching edge to edge. now slowly push them together. watch how they buckle upward — that is exactly how mountains form at convergent boundaries.

check yourself: if india is moving north at about 5 cm per year, how far will it have traveled in 1 million years? (answer: 5 cm x 1,000,000 = 5,000,000 cm = 50 km — enough to significantly change the geography.) mountains are still growing geomountaingrowthdiagram the himalayas — the tallest mountain range on earth — are still growing. mount everest gains about 1 centimetre per year because the indian tectonic plate is still crashing into the eurasian plate at about 5 cm per year.

this collision started about 50 million years ago when the indian subcontinent, which had been drifting north after breaking away from africa, slammed into asia. the force was (and is) enormous — enough to crumple the earth's crust upward like a car bonnet in a head-on collision.

but mountains do not grow forever. erosion fights back. wind, water, ice, and gravity constantly wear down mountains. the appalachian mountains in eastern north america were once as tall as the himalayas — about 450 million years ago. after hundreds of millions of years of erosion with no new tectonic uplift, they have been worn down to gentle, rounded hills.

the height of a mountain at any moment is the balance between:
- tectonic uplift (pushing up) — active at convergent plate boundaries
- erosion (wearing down) — happening everywhere, all the time
- isostatic adjustment — the crust floats on the mantle like an iceberg in water. as erosion removes material from the top, the mountain rises slightly, like a ship rising as you unload c

title: Geometry Essentials
tagline: Shapes, angles, and spatial reasoning — from bamboo huts to bridge arches.
searchText:
geometry essentials shapes, angles, and spatial reasoning — from bamboo huts to bridge arches. types of angles triangles and the pythagorean theorem 3d shapes: volume and surface area try it — areas & volumes transformations what is an angle? vertex angle types by size: how to measure: key angle relationships: complementary supplementary vertical angles angle relationships with parallel lines: worked example: one known angle non-euclidean geometry changes the rules: spherical (elliptic) hyperbolic (saddle) gauss-bonnet theorem the simplest polygon: 180° classification by sides: classification by angles: the pythagorean theorem example: ladder against a wall 4 metres the converse: 3-4-5 triple pythagorean triples generating primitive triples: worked problem: 12 metres generalizations of the pythagorean theorem: law of cosines: n-dimensional distance: fermat's last theorem what is a circle? radius key measurements: circle vocabulary: chord tangent arc sector sector area: arc length and sector area (radians): 11.78 cm 88.36 cm² key theorems: inscribed angle theorem thales' theorem circle equation: apollonius circles isoperimetric inequality why 3d matters: key formulas: worked example: cylindrical water drum composite shapes: example: total 282.7 m³ 75 pi = 235.6 m² cavalieri's principle: curse of dimensionality 2 + b what is a transformation? rigid transformations symmetry line symmetry rotational symmetry japi hat coordinate descriptions: (−4, 3) similar symmetry groups: group 17 distinct symmetry groups 230 space groups geometry-essentials geometry essentials shapes, angles, and spatial reasoning — from bamboo huts to bridge arches. the-magic-japi-hat basket-weavers-song bridge-that-grew types of angles what is an angle?

 an angle forms where two rays meet at a common point called the vertex. angles are measured in degrees (°).

 angle types by size:

 | type | range | example |
 |------|-------|---------|
 | acute | 0° < angle < 90° | tip of a bamboo leaf |
 | right | exactly 90° | corner of a book |
 | obtuse | 90° < angle < 180° | open laptop lid |
 | straight | exactly 180° | a flat line |
 | reflex | 180° < angle < 360° | more than halfway around |

 how to measure: place a protractor's centre on the vertex, align one ray with 0°, and read where the other ray crosses.

 key angle relationships:

 - complementary — two angles that add to 90° (e.g. 30° + 60°)
 - supplementary — two angles that add to 180° (e.g. 110° + 70°)
 - vertical angles — opposite angles where two lines cross. always equal

 these relationships are the building blocks of every geometric proof. angle relationships with parallel lines:

 when a transversal crosses two parallel lines, several angle pairs form:

 | relationship | rule |
 |-------------|------|
 | vertical angles | always equal |
 | alternate interior angles | equal |
 | co-interior (same-side) angles | sum to 180° |
 | corresponding angles | equal |

 worked example: two parallel lines cut by a transversal, one angle = 65°:

 | angle type | value | why |
 |-----------|-------|-----|
 | alternate interior | 65° | equal to given |
 | co-interior | 115° | 180° − 65° |
 | corresponding | 65° | equal to given |

 with just one known angle, you can find every angle in the figure. non-euclidean geometry changes the rules:

 - spherical (elliptic) — triangle angles sum to more than 180°. a triangle with vertices at the north pole, equator at 0°, and equator at 90°e has three 90° angles, totaling 270°
 - hyperbolic (saddle) — triangle angles sum to less than 180°
 - the angular excess (or deficit) is proportional to the triangle's area — the gauss-bonnet theorem links local geometry to global topology

 gps satellites must account for non-euclidean spacetime geometry (general relativity) to achieve metre-level accuracy. angleexplorerdiagram triangles and the pythagorean theorem the simplest polygon: a triangle has three sides, three angles, always summing to exactly 180°.

 classification by sides:

 | type | sides | angles |
 |------|-------|--------|
 | equilateral | all 3 equal | all 60° |
 | isosceles | 2 equal | 2 equal base angles |
 | scalene | none equal | all different |

 classification by angles: acute (all < 90°), right (one = 90°), obtuse (one > 90°).

 the pythagorean theorem

 for any right triangle with legs a and b and hypotenuse c:

 a² + b² = c²

 example: ladder against a wall

 | step | operation | result |
 |------|-----------|--------|
 | given | ladder = 5 m, base = 3 m | |
 | formula | height = sqrt(5² − 3²) | |
 | compute | sqrt(25 − 9) = sqrt(16) | 4 metres |

 the converse: if three sides satisfy a² + b² = c², the triangle must be a right triangle. the classic 3-4-5 triple (9 + 16 = 25) has been used by builders for thousands of years to create perfect right angles. pythagorean triples are integer solutions to a² + b² = c²:

 | triple | check |
 |--------|-------|
 | (3, 4, 5) | 9 + 16 = 25 |
 | (5, 12, 13) | 25 + 144 = 169 |
 | (8, 15, 17) | 64 + 225 = 289 |
 | (7, 24, 25) | 49 + 576 = 625 |

 generating primitive triples: a = m²−n², b = 2mn, c = m²+n² where m > n > 0, gcd(m,n) = 1, and m−n is odd.

 - m=2, n=1 gives (3, 4, 5)
 - m=3, n=2 gives (5, 12, 13)

 worked problem: a 13 m ladder, base 5 m from wall. height = sqrt(13² − 5²) = sqrt(169 − 25) = sqrt(144) = 12 metres. generalizations of the pythagorean theorem:

 - law of cosines: c² = a² + b² − 2ab·cos c works for any triangle. when c = 90°, cos 90° = 0 and it reduces to the pythagorean theorem
 - n-dimensional distance: d² = sum of (xi − yi)² — the n-dimensional pythagorean theorem
 - fermat's last theorem (proved by andrew wiles in 1995 after 358 years): a^n + b^n = c^n has no positive integer solutions for n > 2

 the proof used deep connections between elliptic curves and modular forms — mathematics far beyond anything fermat himself could have known. triangleexplorerdiagram what is a circle?

 a circle is the set of all points equidistant from a centre point. that distance is the radius (r).

 key measurements:

 | property | formula | notes |
 |----------|---------|-------|
 | diameter | d = 2r | longest line through centre |
 | circumference | c = 2 pi r | distance around |
 | area | a = pi r² | space enclosed |

 the number pi is approximately 3.14159 — the ratio of any circle's circumference to its diameter.

 circle vocabulary:

 - chord — a line segment connecting two points on the circle (diameter = longest chord)
 - tangent — a line touching the circle at exactly one point, always perpendicular to the radius at that point
 - arc — a portion of the circumference
 - sector — the "pie slice" between two radii and the arc they enclose

 sector area: (angle/360) x pi r²

 these properties appear everywhere — wheel design, gear ratios, clock faces, satellite orbits, and the circular cross-sections of bamboo used in traditional assamese homes. arc length and sector area (radians):

 - arc length: s = r x theta
 - sector area: a = (1/2) r² theta

 worked example: a pizza slice with r = 15 cm and angle = 45° (= pi/4 radians):

 | quantity | calculation | result |
 |----------|------------|--------|
 | arc length | 15 x pi/4 | 11.78 cm |
 | sector area | (1/2)(225)(pi/4) | 88.36 cm² |

 key theorems:

 - inscribed angle theorem — an inscribed angle is half the central angle subtending the same arc
 - thales' theorem — an angle inscribed in a semicircle is always 90° circle equation: (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r² for centre (h, k).

 in the complex plane, a circle of radius r centred at z0 is |z − z0| = r.

 - apollonius circles — loci where the distance ratio to two fixed points is constant — appear in electromagnetic field theory and optics
 - isoperimetric inequality — among all closed curves of a given perimeter, the circle encloses maximum area: a <= p²/(4 pi), with equality only for a circle
\

title: Git & Version Control
tagline: Track every change, collaborate without chaos, and never lose your work again.
searchText:
git & version control track every change, collaborate without chaos, and never lose your work again. what is version control? git basics: init, add, commit, status, log match each git command to what it does branches: creating, switching, and merging remote repositories: push, pull, clone, and github collaboration workflow: pull requests and code review directed acyclic graph (dag) edit git add commit working directory staging area repository commits should be atomic git flow trunk-based development interactive rebase cherry-pick bisect submodules git hooks git lfs forks pull requests code review conventional commits semantic versioning monorepos git-version-control git & version control track every change, collaborate without chaos, and never lose your work again. what is version control? imagine you are writing an essay and you save a new copy every time you make major changes: essayv1.docx, essayv2.docx, essayfinal.docx, essayfinalfinal.docx. within a week you have a dozen files and no idea which one has the paragraph you deleted yesterday. version control solves this by keeping a single file but recording every change as a snapshot in time. you can scroll back through history, see exactly what changed, who changed it, and why.

 this matters enormously when multiple people work on the same project. without version control, two developers editing the same file will overwrite each other's work. with it, the system tracks both sets of changes and helps merge them together. every professional software team in the world uses version control — it is as fundamental to coding as saving a document is to writing. git is the most popular version control system by far, created in 2005 by linus torvalds (who also created linux). git tracks changes, not files. each commit stores a snapshot plus metadata (author, timestamp, message, parent). the sha-1 hash uniquely identifies each commit. key commands: git init, git add file.py, git commit -m "message", git log --oneline, git diff, git status. the staging area lets you commit selectively: change 5 files, stage 2, commit those 2 — the rest remain uncommitted. the .gitignore file prevents tracking generated files (nodemodules/, pycache/, .env). git's data model is a directed acyclic graph (dag) of commits. branches are lightweight pointers — creating one is o(1). merges create two-parent commits. rebasing replays commits onto a new base for linear history. the reflog records every head movement, enabling recovery of "lost" commits. git's content-addressable storage deduplicates identical files across commits. pack files compress similar objects together, making repositories 10-50× smaller than storing full copies. the three-tree architecture (head, index, working directory) enables git's powerful staging workflow. git basics: init, add, commit, status, log gitstagingdiagram try the three-zone diagram above. click edit on a file to modify it (it moves to working). click git add to stage it. type a message and commit. watch files flow working → staged → committed. this is what every git workflow comes down to: three zones and the commands that move files between them.

 git works in three stages. first, you make changes to files in your working directory — this is just your normal folder. second, you stage the changes you want to save using git add. staging is like placing items on a conveyor belt: you choose exactly which changes to include. third, you commit those staged changes with git commit -m "your message", which takes a permanent snapshot.

 the command git init creates a new repository (repo) in your folder — it tells git to start tracking changes here. git status shows you what has changed since the last commit: which files are modified, which are staged, and which are new. git log shows the history of all commits, newest first, with the author, date, and message for each. think of the log as a detailed diary of every save point in your project. these five commands — init, add, commit, status, and log — are the foundation of everything else in git. the three areas: working directory (your files), staging area (index — selected changes for next commit), repository (committed history). workflow: edit files → git add file.py (stage) → git commit -m "message" (save). git diff shows unstaged changes; git diff --staged shows staged changes. git log --oneline --graph shows branch history graphically. undo: git restore file.py discards working changes; git restore --staged file.py unstages. commits should be atomic — each commit represents one logical change with a clear message. git internals: a commit object contains a tree (snapshot of all files), parent pointer(s), author, and message. a tree object maps filenames to blob objects (file contents). blobs are content-addressed — identical file contents are stored once regardless of filename. git cat-file -p <hash> inspects any object. the .git directory contains: objects/ (blobs, trees, commits), refs/ (branch pointers), head (current branch pointer), index (staging area). understanding these internals helps debug unusual situations: detached head, lost commits, corrupted repositories. match each git command to what it does create a new repository and start tracking changes stage changes for the next commit git commit save a permanent snapshot of staged changes git status show which files have changed since the last commit display the history of all commits branches: creating, switching, and merging step through the branch history in the diagram above. a branch forks off main, gets its own commits, and eventually merges back. notice how a branch is just a pointer to a commit — not a full copy of files. that's why creating branches in git is instant.

 a branch is a parallel copy of your project where you can experiment without affecting the main version. imagine a tree: the trunk is your main code, and each branch grows in its own direction. you might create a branch called "add-dark-mode" to try a new feature. if it works out, you merge the branch back into the trunk. if it doesn't, you delete the branch and the main code is untouched.

 you create a branch with git branch feature-name and switch to it with git checkout feature-name (or the newer git switch feature-name). while on that branch, all your commits only affect that branch. when you are ready, you switch back to the main branch and run git merge feature-name to bring the changes in. the default branch is usually called main (older repos use master). branches are lightweight in git — they are just pointers to a commit, not full copies of files — so creating hundreds of branches costs almost nothing. professional teams create a new branch for every bug fix and every feature, keeping the main branch always stable and deployable. branch workflow: git branch feature-xyz (create), git switch feature-xyz (switch), make changes, commit, then git switch main; git merge feature-xyz. merge conflicts occur when both branches modify the same lines — manually edit, then git add and commit. git flow: main (production), develop (integration), feature/, release/, hotfix/. simpler: trunk-based development — short-lived branches merged frequently. github/gitlab pull requests enable code review before merging. advanced git: interactive rebase (git rebase -i head5) reorders, squashes, edits, or drops commits. cherry-pick (git cherry-pick abc123) applies a specific commit. bisect (git bisect start/bad/good) binary-searches through history to find bug-introducing commits in o(log n) steps. submodules manage repository dependencies. git hooks run scripts before/after events (pre-commit: lint code, pre-push: run tests). git lfs handles large binaries by storing pointers in the repo and actual files on a separate server. gitbranchdiagram remote repositories: push, pull, clone, and github so far, everything has been on your own computer. a remote repository is a copy of your repo stored on a server — most co

title: Gravity & Orbits
searchText:
gravity & orbits orbits and satellites weightlessness escape velocity the rocket equation hohmann transfer orbits 686 n g(h) = gm/(r+h)² 8.67 m/s² u = −gmm/r −4.37 × 10⁹ j general relativity g_μν = √(2gm/r) 11,186 m/s = 11.2 km/s 2,376 m/s = 2.4 km/s 0.414 × 7.9 = 3.3 km/s ke = ½mv² = gmm/r 6.25 × 10¹⁰ j ≈ 62.5 gj schwarzschild radius 2,953 m ≈ 3 km 8.87 mm tsiolkovsky rocket equation δv = v_e × ln(m₀ / m_f) mass ratio staging δv = v_e ln(m₀/m_f) = i_sp × g₀ × ln(m₀/m_f) worked example 4,740 m/s 9,316 m/s 14,056 m/s gravity loss drag loss thrust-to-weight ratio oberth effect nuclear thermal rockets fusion drives fuel-efficient hohmann transfer orbit ellipse circularize two short engine burns delta-v geostationary orbit 3.94 km/s 5.26 hours bi-elliptic transfer patched conic approximation gravity ' + 'assists gravity-and-orbits gravity & orbits kite-festival star-fell-deepor lotus-float newton's law of gravitation in 1687, isaac newton published his law of universal gravitation: every object with mass attracts every other object with mass. the force between two objects is given by f = gmm/r², where g is the gravitational constant (6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ n m²/kg²), m and m are the masses of the two objects, and r is the distance between their centres. this single equation explains everything from falling mangoes to orbiting planets. the force is always attractive, never repulsive, and it acts along the line connecting the two objects.

the inverse square law — the 1/r² in the equation — means that gravity weakens rapidly with distance. double the distance and the force drops to one-quarter. triple the distance and it falls to one-ninth. this is why the moon, despite being a quarter of earth's diameter, exerts only a tiny gravitational pull on you compared to the earth beneath your feet: the moon is 384,400 km away, while the ground is right under you. yet even at that distance, the moon's gravity is strong enough to raise the oceans in tides.

weight and mass are often confused but they are fundamentally different. mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in kilograms — it does not change regardless of location. weight is the gravitational force acting on that mass: w = mg, where g is the local gravitational acceleration. on earth, g is approximately 9.81 m/s². on the moon, g is only 1.62 m/s², so a person with a mass of 60 kg would weigh 589 n on earth but only 97 n on the moon. their mass remains 60 kg in both places. astronauts on the international space station are often described as "weightless," but this is not because gravity has disappeared — it is because they are in continuous freefall, as we will discuss shortly. worked example: what is the gravitational force between the earth (m = 5.97 × 10²⁴ kg) and a 70 kg person standing on the surface (r = 6.371 × 10⁶ m)? f = gmm/r² = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ × 70 / (6.371×10⁶)² = 686 n (which is their weight — consistent with w = mg = 70 × 9.81). the gravitational acceleration at any altitude: g(h) = gm/(r+h)². at the iss (h = 408 km): g = 6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ / (6.779×10⁶)² = 8.67 m/s² (89% of surface gravity — astronauts are not beyond gravity's reach). gravitational potential energy: u = −gmm/r (negative because work must be done against gravity to separate objects). at the surface: u = −6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ × 70 / 6.371×10⁶ = −4.37 × 10⁹ j (this is the energy needed to remove a 70 kg person to infinity). newton's gravitation is the weak-field, low-velocity limit of einstein's general relativity (gr, 1915), which describes gravity not as a force but as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass-energy. the einstein field equations gμν = (8πg/c⁴)tμν relate spacetime curvature (gμν) to the distribution of mass-energy (tμν). for a spherically symmetric mass, the schwarzschild solution gives the metric and predicts: (1) gravitational time dilation — clocks tick slower in stronger gravity (confirmed by pound-rebka experiment, 1959, and gps corrections); (2) gravitational lensing — light bends around massive objects (first observed during the 1919 solar eclipse, now used to map dark matter); (3) gravitational redshift — photons lose energy climbing out of a gravity well; (4) black holes — when mass is compressed within the schwarzschild radius rs = 2gm/c² (for the sun: rs = 2.95 km; for earth: rs = 8.9 mm). the detection of gravitational waves by ligo (2015, nobel prize 2017) confirmed gr's prediction of propagating spacetime distortions travelling at exactly c. gravitationalfielddiagram orbits and satellites an orbit is the curved path an object follows when it moves fast enough to keep falling towards a body without ever hitting it. imagine firing a cannonball horizontally from a very tall mountain. fire it slowly and it hits the ground nearby. fire it faster and it travels further before landing. fire it at just the right speed and the curvature of its fall matches the curvature of the earth — it falls continuously but the ground curves away at the same rate. this is an orbit. in a circular orbit, gravity provides exactly the centripetal force needed to maintain the circular path: gmm/r² = mv²/r, which gives the orbital velocity v = sqrt(gm/r).

satellites orbit at different altitudes depending on their purpose. low earth orbit (leo) ranges from about 200 to 2,000 km altitude. the international space station orbits at 408 km, completing one orbit every 90 minutes at a speed of 27,600 km/h. at this altitude, it passes over different parts of the earth on each orbit, making it ideal for earth observation and scientific experiments. medium earth orbit (meo), at 2,000 to 35,786 km, is used by navigation satellites like gps, which orbit at about 20,200 km with a 12-hour period.

geostationary earth orbit (geo) is a special orbit at exactly 35,786 km above the equator, where the orbital period matches earth's rotation: 24 hours. a satellite in geo appears to hover motionless over one spot on the equator. this makes geo ideal for communications satellites and weather monitoring — your satellite tv dish can point at a fixed spot in the sky because the satellite never moves relative to the ground. india's insat series of weather and communication satellites operates from geo, providing continuous coverage of the indian subcontinent. orbital velocity for a circular orbit: v = √(gm/r). for leo at 400 km (r = 6,771 km = 6.771×10⁶ m): v = √(6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴ / 6.771×10⁶) = 7,672 m/s = 27,620 km/h. orbital period: t = 2πr/v = 2π × 6.771×10⁶ / 7672 = 5,543 s = 92.4 minutes. for geo (t = 86,400 s): solving t = 2π√(r³/gm) gives r = (gmt²/4π²)^(1/3) = 42,164 km from earth's centre, or 35,786 km above the surface. kepler's third law: t² = (4π²/gm)r³. for any earth orbit: t² = 9.89 × 10⁻¹⁴ × r³ (with t in seconds, r in metres). gps satellites at r = 26,560 km: t = 2π√(2.656×10⁷)³/(6.674×10⁻¹¹ × 5.97×10²⁴)) = 43,077 s ≈ 11.97 hours. the total energy of an orbiting body: e = −gmm/(2r) — negative means bound. more energy (less negative) → higher orbit. real orbits are ellipses (kepler's first law), described by semi-major axis a and eccentricity e (0 = circle, 0-1 = ellipse, 1 = parabola, >1 = hyperbola). the vis-viva equation v² = gm(2/r − 1/a) gives the speed at any point in an elliptical orbit. at periapsis (closest approach): vp = √(gm(1+e)/(a(1−e))). to transfer between orbits, the hohmann transfer uses

' + '| |
' + '| nitrification requires oxygen, denitrification requires its absence first-order kinetics model habitats-and-biomes habitats & biomes from kaziranga grasslands to himalayan alpine meadows — where life thrives and why. kaziranga-grass snow-leopards-promise cloud-weaver-of-tawang dancing-deer-of-loktak-lake what defines a habitat grassrhinohabitatdiagram match each habitat factor to its category temperature, rainfall, soil type abiotic (non-living) factors predators, food species, competitors biotic (living) factors floodplain grasslands with wallowing pools indian rhinoceros habitat floating vegetation mats over a lake sangai deer habitat dense canopy with fruiting trees hoolock gibbon habitat think of a habitat as an organism's home address — not just the geographic location, but everything that makes it liveable: food, water, shelter, temperature, neighbours. move a rhino from tall floodplain grassland into dry scrubland, and it's like dropping a fish onto a pavement. the address is wrong.

 every habitat is shaped by two categories of factors:

 | factor type | examples | think of it as... |
 |-------------|---------|--------------------|
 | abiotic (non-living) | temperature, rainfall, soil type, sunlight, altitude, water chemistry | the house itself — walls, roof, plumbing |
 | biotic (living) | food species, predators, competitors, parasites, symbiotic partners | the neighbours — helpful or hostile |

 change any factor significantly and the habitat may become unliveable. when a barrage raised water levels in loktak lake, manipur, it thinned the floating phumdi mats that sangai deer depend on — their habitat literally sank beneath them, pushing the species toward extinction.

 explore the diagram above — it shows how the alluvial floodplain grasslands of the brahmaputra valley provide everything the indian one-horned rhinoceros needs: tall grass for cover, low-lying pools for wallowing, nutrient-rich soil renewed by annual floods.

 habitat destruction is the single greatest threat to biodiversity worldwide — responsible for more species declines than pollution, hunting, or climate change combined. when a forest is cleared for agriculture, the habitat doesn't just shrink. it fragments into isolated patches too small to sustain viable populations, with roads and settlements creating impassable barriers between them.

 | habitat feature | why it matters | what happens when lost |
 |----------------|---------------|----------------------|
 | tall alluvial grass | cover from predators, food source | rhinos exposed, starve |
 | wallowing pools | thermoregulation, parasite control | overheating, skin disease |
 | flood cycle | renews soil nutrients, prevents forest takeover | grassland converts to woodland |
 | corridors between patches | gene flow, seasonal migration | inbreeding, local extinction |

 quick check: why is fragmentation worse than simple shrinkage? because 10 tiny patches don't equal one large patch. edge effects (more wind, light, predators) penetrate deep into small fragments, and animals can't cross hostile terrain between them. species distribution models (sdms) predict where a species could live by matching occurrence records against environmental variables.

 | sdm method | input data | strengths | limitations |
 |-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
 | maxent | presence-only | works with incomplete surveys | cannot distinguish absence from non-detection |
 | glm / gam | presence + absence | statistically rigorous | requires confirmed absence data |
 | random forest | presence + background | handles complex interactions | black-box; hard to interpret |
 | ensemble | multiple models averaged | reduces individual model bias | computationally expensive |

 for the indian rhinoceros, sdms identify the brahmaputra floodplain, terai grasslands, and parts of the duars as high-suitability zones — matching observed populations almost exactly.

 habitat fragmentation metrics:

 | metric | what it measures | conservation meaning |
 |--------|-----------------|---------------------|
 | patch size | area of each fragment | larger patches sustain more species |
 | edge-to-area ratio | shape complexity | more edge = more degradation from wind, light, invasives |
 | nearest-neighbour distance | isolation between patches | greater distance = less dispersal |
 | connectivity index | functional linkage | high connectivity = gene flow maintained |

 the species–area relationship s = ca^z (s = species count, a = area, c and z are constants, z ≈ 0.15–0.35) predicts that halving habitat area reduces species by 20–30%. this is not a guess — it has b

dangerous why assam summers feel worse than delhi summers: traditional cooling technology: matka the energy cost of evaporation: q = ṁ × l_v worked example — sweating: 673 w humidity and the psychrometric chart: worked example — matka cooling: 34 w the penman equation e = (δ·r_n + ρ_a·c_p·(e_s − e_a)/r_a) / (δ + γ(1 + r_s/r_a)) the clausius-clapeyron relation and climate change: radiative sky cooling heat-and-thermodynamics heat & thermodynamics engineering specific heat conduction convection grandmothers-pitha holi-tea-gardens little-chef monsoon-home temperature vs heat molecularmotiondiagram temperature and heat are related but fundamentally different — and confusing them is one of the most common mistakes in physics.

 temperature = a measure of how fast the particles in a substance are moving on average.
 heat = the transfer of thermal energy from a hotter object to a cooler one.

 think of it this way: temperature is like the speed of cars on a highway, while heat is like the flow of traffic between two roads.

 | concept | what it measures | units | analogy |
 |---------|-----------------|-------|---------|
 | temperature | average kinetic energy of particles | °c, k, °f | speed of individual cars |
 | heat | energy transferred between objects | joules (j) | traffic flowing between roads |
 | thermal energy | total kinetic energy of all particles | joules (j) | total cars on the road |

 key insight: a large object at a low temperature can hold more total thermal energy than a small object at a high temperature. a bathtub of warm water at 40°c contains far more thermal energy than a red-hot spark at 3,000°c — because the bathtub has trillions of times more molecules.

 temperature scales:

 | scale | freezing point of water | boiling point of water | absolute zero |
 |-------|------------------------|----------------------|---------------|
 | celsius (°c) | 0°c | 100°c | −273.15°c |
 | kelvin (k) | 273.15 k | 373.15 k | 0 k |
 | fahrenheit (°f) | 32°f | 212°f | −459.67°f |

 the kelvin scale starts at absolute zero — the theoretical temperature where all particle motion ceases. one kelvin degree equals one celsius degree, so converting is simple: k = °c + 273.15.

 try the diagram above — watch how molecules move faster as temperature increases and nearly freeze at very low temperatures.

 when you pour a fresh cup of assam tea, the tea is at 90°c and the surrounding air is at 25°c. heat flows from the tea into the air — never the reverse. the tea cools, the air warms (slightly). this one-way flow is a fundamental law of nature. the relationship between heat transferred and temperature change is the most important equation in this chapter:

 > q = mcδt

 | symbol | meaning | units |
 |--------|---------|-------|
 | q | heat energy transferred | joules (j) |
 | m | mass of the substance | kilograms (kg) |
 | c | specific heat capacity | j/(kg·°c) |
 | δt | temperature change (tfinal − tinitial) | °c or k |

 worked example — heating water for tea:

 you want to heat 0.5 kg of water from 20°c to 100°c in a kettle (cwater = 4,186 j/(kg·°c)).

 q = mcδt = 0.5 × 4,186 × (100 − 20) = 0.5 × 4,186 × 80
 q = 167,440 j ≈ 167 kj

 if the electric kettle is rated at 2,000 w:

 t = q / p = 167,440 / 2,000 = 84 seconds ≈ 1.4 minutes

 the kinetic theory connects temperature to molecular motion:

 | quantity | formula | at 300 k (room temp) |
 |----------|---------|----------------------|
 | average ke per molecule | ⟨ke⟩ = (3/2)kt | 6.21 × 10⁻²¹ j |
 | rms speed of n₂ | vrms = √(3kt/m) | 517 m/s — faster than sound! |
 | rms speed of o₂ | vrms = √(3kt/m) | 483 m/s |
 | rms speed of h₂ | vrms = √(3kt/m) | 1,920 m/s |

 here k = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ j/k is boltzmann's constant — the bridge between the microscopic world (individual molecules) and the macroscopic world (temperature you can feel). temperature in statistical mechanics is defined via the boltzmann distribution: the probability of a system being in a state with energy e is p(e) ∝ exp(−e/kt). formally:

 > 1/t = ∂s/∂e

 where s is entropy and e is internal energy. this definition allows negative absolute temperatures — not "colder than zero" but "hotter than infinity."

 | temperature regime | meaning | example |
 |-------------------|---------|--------|
 | t > 0 | normal — higher energy states less populated | everything around you |
 | t → ∞ | all states equally populated | extremely hot plasma |
 | t < 0 | population inversion — higher energy states more populated | lasers, spin systems |

 a negative-temperature system spontaneously transfers energy to any positive-temperature system — it is "hotter tha

title: HTML / CSS / JavaScript
tagline: The three languages every website is built with
searchText:
html / css / javascript the three languages every website is built with what the web actually is match each web technology to its role html — the structure css — the style javascript — the behavior how the three work together match the file type to what it contains the dom — your page as a tree responsive design — one page, every screen a complete html page styling with css interactive javascript putting it all together: a mini app building a search feature the dom — selecting & changing elements try dom manipulation events — responding to user actions try events forms — collecting user input flexbox — layout made simple try flexbox css grid — two-dimensional layout try css grid typescript + dom — type-safe web pages try typescript + dom types https dns lookup tcp handshake tls handshake http request sent server processes response streams back browser parses render tree the modern web pushes every step: http/2 multiplexes http/3 service workers preload/prefetch hints input → state → render dom (document object model) selecting elements: changing elements: creating elements: check yourself: event adding event listeners: common events: the event object `event.preventdefault()` basic form: input types: reading form values: `e.preventdefault()` validation: key properties on the container (parent): key properties on the children: css grid basic grid: named areas: when to use flexbox vs grid: flexbox grid the problem with plain javascript: typescript catches the issue: common dom types: typed event handlers: form data with typescript: html-css-js html / css / javascript the three languages every website is built with boy-who-built-a-library what the web actually is when you visit a website, your browser (chrome, firefox, safari) sends a request to a server somewhere in the world. the server sends back files — html, css, and javascript. your browser reads these files and renders the page you see. every website you have ever visited — google, youtube, wikipedia — is built from these same three languages. the browser is like a kitchen: html is the ingredients list, css is the plating instructions, and javascript is the cooking process that transforms everything into the final dish. the http request/response cycle: your browser sends get /index.html http/1.1 → the server responds with http/1.1 200 ok and the html content. status codes: 200=ok, 301=moved permanently, 404=not found, 500=server error. chrome devtools (f12) → network tab shows every request, its size, and load time. a typical web page makes 50-100 requests (html, css, js, images, fonts, analytics). https encrypts this traffic using tls — the padlock icon means a man-in-the-middle cannot read your data. http/2 multiplexes multiple requests over a single connection, and http/3 uses quic (over udp) for even faster connections. what actually happens between typing a url and seeing the page? a 100+ step journey in under a second: 1. dns lookup — your computer asks "what ip address is google.com?" (often cached, 20ms) 2. tcp handshake — three packets exchanged to establish a reliable connection (50ms) 3. tls handshake — if https, encryption keys negotiated (100ms) 4. http request sent — your browser shouts "get /" 5. server processes — database queries, template rendering, response assembly 6. response streams back — html arrives in chunks 7. browser parses html → builds dom, fetches css → builds cssom, fetches js → runs it 8. render tree computed from dom + cssom, layout calculated, pixels painted, composited to screen the modern web pushes every step: cdns (cloudflare, fastly) cache content at 300 edge locations globally so step 6 takes 20ms instead of 200ms. http/2 multiplexes many requests over one connection. http/3 ditches tcp entirely for quic (over udp) — no head-of-line blocking on packet loss. service workers cache responses locally, enabling offline-first web apps. preload/prefetch hints () tell the browser "fetch this now, i'll need it soon." all of it aimed at one goal: shrink the journey from url-typed to pixels-painted. match each web technology to its role defines the structure and content of the page controls colors, fonts, spacing, and layout javascript makes the page interactive and dynamic reads html/css/js files and renders the page stores and sends website files when requested html — the structure htmltagsdiagram click tags in the diagram above. each one is a labeled slot:

marks a heading,

marks a paragraph, places an image. html is just text wrapped in labels the browser knows how to render. html (hypertext markup language) defines what is on the page. it uses tags — words in angle brackets — to label content.

says "this is a main heading."

says "this is a paragraph." places an image. creates a clickable link. tags usually come in pairs:

hello

— the opening tag, the content, and the closing tag. the browser reads these tags from top to bottom and lays out the page accordingly. html does not control colors, fonts, or animations — that is css's job. html only says "there is a heading here, a paragraph here, an image here." think of it as the blueprint of a house: it shows where the rooms and doors are, but not what color the walls are. semantic html elements convey meaning:
,
,
,
,
,
,
. screen readers rely on semantic elements to navigate. forms:

title: Human Body Systems
tagline: How your heart pumps, lungs breathe, and brain thinks — the engineering inside you.
searchText:
human body systems how your heart pumps, lungs breathe, and brain thinks — the engineering inside you. the circulatory system match each heart structure to its role the respiratory system the digestive system match each digestive organ to its primary function the nervous system skeleton and muscles 3 billion times two pumps, fused together. right atrium right ventricle left atrium left ventricle here's a number that should astonish you: 100,000 kilometres capillaries lub dub once every minute cardiac output — how much blood your heart pumps: cardiac output 5.04 l/min 30.6 l/min worked example: your entire blood supply completes a full circuit once per minute blood pressure the heart's electrical system: ecg p wave qrs complex t wave why is the left ventricle ~3× thicker than the right? the frank-starling law at the cellular level heart failure modern intervention: you have 300 million of these sacs. nose trachea bronchi → bronchioles alveoli one cell thick haemoglobin high concentration to low concentration 150 litres of air per minute gas exchange follows fick's law of diffusion: 64 mmhg 6 mmhg why does co₂ still leave efficiently with only 6 mmhg difference? lung volumes — measured by spirometry: vital capacity total lung capacity worked example — minute ventilation: 7,000 ml/min = 7 l/min 70 l/min haemoglobin's oxygen dissociation curve — a masterpiece of molecular design: cooperative sigmoidal (s-shaped) curve hill coefficient factors that shift the curve: high-altitude populations 24-48 hours 9 metres villi microvilli 250 square metres mouth esophagus stomach small intestine 90% of nutrients absorbed here large intestine behind the scenes — organs that don't touch the food but do most of the work: pancreas liver bile gallbladder ulcer enzyme specificity — the lock-and-key model: worked example — michaelis-menten kinetics: daily digestive secretions: total ~7 l lactose intolerance the gut microbiome — your "virtual organ": short-chain fatty acids (scfas): the gut-brain axis: faecal microbiota transplantation (fmt) action potential 85 billion of these at up to 120 metres per second. myelin nodes of ranvier jumps from gap to gap dendrites soma (cell body) axon myelin sheath synapse neurotransmitters this is how drugs work. your brain in numbers: the fastest trick: reflex arcs. before the pain signal even reaches your brain the action potential — step by step: all-or-nothing principle firing frequency saltatory conduction 120 m/s synapse mechanics: botulinum toxin (botox) long-term potentiation (ltp) — how synapses learn: early ltp late ltp the nmda receptor as a coincidence detector: camkii optogenetics clinical link: muscles can only pull. they cannot push. antagonistic pairs. bones are not dead scaffolding. strength-to-weight ratio better than steel support protection movement blood cell factory 200 billion blood cells per day mineral bank why are babies floppy? 270 bones 206 bones ball-and-socket hinge pivot gliding 20 muscles bone — a remarkable composite material: strength-to-weight ratio better than steel or concrete wolff's law muscle contraction — the sliding filament model: motor units hill's force-velocity equation: muscle fibre types: titin — the largest known protein: passive tension human-body-systems human body systems how your heart pumps, lungs breathe, and brain thinks — the engineering inside you. tigers-whisker honey-hunters-lesson red-panda-mask the circulatory system heartdiagram right now, while you're reading this, your heart is pumping. it will do this 3 billion times over your life without a single break — no maintenance, no battery change, no pause for sleep. press two fingers against the side of your neck. feel that? every thump is the most reliable machine in your body firing again.

 watch the diagram above. see that red blob pumping in rhythm, and the particles flowing in and out? that's your heart — roughly the size of your fist, but not one pump. two pumps, fused together. the right side (your left as you look at it) sends "used" blood to the lungs to pick up oxygen. the left side (stronger, thicker-walled) pumps that refreshed blood to everything else — toes, fingertips, brain, kidneys.

 | chamber | what it does | think of it as... |
 |---------|--------------|-------------------|
 | right atrium | catches used blood coming back from the body | inbox |
 | right ventricle | pumps it to the lungs for cleaning | short-haul truck |
 | left atrium | catches clean blood returning from the lungs | returns tray |
 | left ventricle | pumps it out to the entire body | long-haul truck (3× thicker walls) |

 here's a number that should astonish you: if you laid every blood vessel in your body end to end, they'd stretch 100,000 kilometres — enough to wrap around the earth 2.5 times. most of that length is in capillaries so narrow that red blood cells have to squeeze through single file. that's where the real work happens: oxygen and nutrients slip out to your tissues, waste seeps in.

 every heartbeat sends a pressure wave down your arteries — that's your pulse. and the "lub-dub" you hear through a stethoscope? that's the sound of valves snapping shut. lub when the valves between atria and ventricles close; dub when the exit valves snap. they're one-way doors making sure blood never flows backwards.

 your entire blood supply (about 5 litres) completes a full circuit through your body once every minute at rest. during a sprint, that rate jumps six-fold. cardiac output — how much blood your heart pumps:

 | parameter | symbol | at rest | during exercise |
 |-----------|--------|---------|------------------|
 | heart rate | hr | 72 bpm | 180 bpm |
 | stroke volume | sv | 70 ml | 170 ml |
 | cardiac output | co = hr × sv | 5.04 l/min | 30.6 l/min |

 worked example: at rest, your heart pumps 5 litres per minute. your body contains about 5 litres of blood, so your entire blood supply completes a full circuit once per minute. during a sprint, cardiac output rises 6×.

 blood pressure = cardiac output × total peripheral resistance (tpr)

 | measurement | value | what it means |
 |-------------|-------|---------------|
 | systolic | 120 mmhg | pressure during a heartbeat (squeeze) |
 | diastolic | 80 mmhg | pressure between heartbeats (rest) |
 | written as | 120/80 | normal, healthy blood pressure |

 the heart's electrical system:

 | structure | role | timing |
 |-----------|------|--------|
 | sa node (pacemaker) | generates the impulse | sets rhythm at 72/min |
 | av node | delays signal briefly | 0.1 s — lets atria finish emptying |
 | bundle of his | carries signal down the septum | directs to ventricles |
 | purkinje fibres | spread signal across ventricles | triggers ventricular contraction |

 an ecg records this sequence: the p wave = atrial depolarisation, qrs complex = ventricular depolarisation, t wave = ventricular repolarisation.

 why is the left ventricle 3× thicker than the right? the left side must generate 120 mmhg to push blood through the entire body. the right side only needs 25 mmhg for the short trip to the lungs. the frank-starling law — the heart's built-in self-regulation:

 when more blood fills the ventricle (increased preload), the cardiac muscle fibres stretch further, optimising actin-myosin overlap and generating a stronger contraction. this means the heart automatically matches its output to venous return — no neural input needed.

 | factor | effect on stroke volume | mechanism |
 |--------|------------------------|------------|
 | ↑ preload (venous return) | ↑ sv | frank-starling — stretch → stronger contraction |
 | ↑ afterload (arterial resistance) | ↓ sv | heart must push against higher pressure |
 | ↑ contractility (sympathetic) | ↑ sv | noradrenaline → more ca²⁺ release → stronger contraction |

 at the cellular level, cardiac muscle has unique features:

 | feature | description | why it matters |
 |---------|-------

' + '| (deep sleep) | 20–40 min | physical repair, growth hormone release, |
' + '| every ~90 minutes hover over each stage in the diagram above the glymphatic system — your brain's cleaning crew: amyloid-β 60% more efficient during sleep how much sleep do you need? the study-sleep irony: why are teenagers always sleepy in the morning? circadian clock melatonin and blue light: blue light (460–480 nm) sleep architecture across the night: first half: second half: the adenosine story: adenosine caffeine the two-process model of sleep regulation (borbely, 1982): process s process c the molecular clock — transcriptional oscillation: inhibit clock/bmal1 depression and sleep — neurocircuitry: default mode network dorsolateral prefrontal cortex neuroplasticity human-health-nutrition human health & nutrition health-medicine what your body needs, how diseases spread, and why sleep matters more than you think. grandmothers-pitha little-chef holi-tea-gardens your body's three fuels macronutrientplatediagram imagine your body as a machine. every machine needs fuel. your body runs on three types of fuel — and each one does a completely different job:

 | fuel | what it does | energy per gram | think of it as... |
 |------|-------------|-----------------|--------------------|
 | carbohydrates | instant and sustained energy | 4 kcal/g | petrol in a car |
 | proteins | build and repair | 4 kcal/g | bricks and cement |
 | fats | long-term storage, insulation, hormones | 9 kcal/g | a backup battery |

 your body cannot substitute one for another. you can't build muscle out of rice, and you can't run on protein alone. each fuel has a role.

 try the diagram above — switch between an assamese meal, a balanced meal, and an athlete's meal to see how the proportions change.

 quick check: if fats give 9 kcal per gram but carbs only give 4, why doesn't your body just burn fat all the time?

 because fat is harder to break down. carbohydrates are fast fuel — ready in minutes. fat is slow fuel — your body saves it for when carbs run out. how much of each do you need?

 the indian council of medical research (icmr) recommends:

 | macronutrient | % of daily calories | for a 2,000 kcal diet |
 |---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
 | carbohydrates | 55–65% | 275–325 g |
 | proteins | 10–15% | 50–75 g |
 | fats | 20–30% | 44–67 g |

 a typical assamese bhaat-dail-maas (rice-dal-fish) meal:

 | food | serving | carbs | protein | fat | calories |
 |------|---------|-------|---------|-----|----------|
 | steamed rice | 300 g | 78 g | 6 g | 1 g | 345 |
 | masoor dal | 150 g | 15 g | 12 g | 1 g | 117 |
 | rohu fish curry | 100 g | 2 g | 20 g | 5 g | 133 |
 | mustard oil | 1 tbsp | 0 g | 0 g | 14 g | 126 |
 | total | | 95 g | 38 g | 21 g | 721 |

 this single meal provides 36% of daily calories. the carb:protein:fat ratio is roughly 53:21:26 — close to balanced, though carb-heavy (typical for rice-based diets). why 4-4-9? the energy content of macronutrients comes from their chemical bonds.

 | macronutrient | bond type | oxidation pathway | atp yield |
 |---------------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
 | glucose (c₆h₁₂o₆) | c-h, c-o | glycolysis → krebs → etc | 36-38 atp |
 | palmitate (c₁₆h₃₂o₂) | many c-h | β-oxidation → krebs → etc | 129 atp |
 | alanine (amino acid) | c-h, c-n, c-o | deamination → krebs | 15 atp |

 fats yield more atp per gram because they have more c-h bonds (high hydrogen:oxygen ratio). each c-h bond releases energy when oxidised. glucose is already partially oxidised (it contains oxygen atoms), so it yields less.

 the atwater factors (4-4-9) are averages. real values vary: alcohol yields 7 kcal/g, soluble fiber 2 kcal/g (fermented by gut bacteria into short-chain fatty acids). match each macronutrient to its primary role carbohydrates primary energy source — rice, potatoes, bread (4 kcal/g) building and repair — muscles, enzymes, antibodies (4 kcal/g) energy storage, insulation, cell membranes, hormones (9 kcal/g) indigestible carbohydrate — supports digestion and gut bacteria carbohydrates: quick vs slow energy glycaemicindexdiagram not all carbohydrates are equal. some hit your bloodstream like a fire hose. others trickle in steadily.

 simple carbohydrates (sugars) are small molecules — glucose, fructose, sucrose. your body absorbs them almost immediately. eating a spoonful of sugar is like throwing paper on a fire: intense flame, quick burnout.

 complex carbohydrates (starches) are long chains of sugar molecules linked together. your body must break them down link by link before absorbing them. eating rice is like feeding logs to a fire: steady heat over hours.

 the glycaemic index (gi) measures this speed on a scale of 0–100:

 | food | gi | speed | what happens in your blood |
 |------|-----|-------|---------------------------|
 | pure glucose | 100 | ⚡ instant | sharp spike, then crash |
 | white rice | 73 | 🔥 fast | high spike, drops within 2 hours |
 | brown rice | 50 | 🕐 medium | moderate rise, gradual decli

title: Hydrology & Water Science
tagline: The same water dinosaurs drank is falling from your tap right now.
searchText:
hydrology & water science the same water dinosaurs drank is falling from your tap right now. the water cycle — the same water dinosaurs drank groundwater — an invisible ocean under your feet aquifers — underground sponges why wells work — water table and pressure rivers — how water carves landscapes floods and droughts — too much and too little water purification — making dirty water safe desalination — turning ocean into drinking water water cycle evaporation transpiration condensation clouds precipitation rain collection solar energy gravity check yourself: water budgets penman–monteith equation residence time variable infiltration capacity (vic) swat isotope hydrology remote sensing infiltration groundwater 30% of all freshwater on earth 2 billion people the problem: analogy: aquifer porosity permeability impermeable layers unconfined confined artesian well water table unsaturated zone saturated zone artesian wells in india: step-wells erosion transportation dissolved suspended saltation traction deposition deltas meander oxbow lake floods droughts 1. screening 2. coagulation and flocculation 3. sedimentation 4. filtration 5. disinfection in emergencies boiling solar disinfection (sodis) household chlorination desalination reverse osmosis (ro) reverse distillation energy brine disposal scale hydrology-and-water hydrology & water science marine-science the same water dinosaurs drank is falling from your tap right now. the water cycle — the same water dinosaurs drank watercyclediagram here is a mind-bending fact: the water on earth today is essentially the same water that has been here for 4 billion years. the water you drank this morning may have once been part of a jurassic ocean, fallen as rain on a roman battlefield, or frozen in an ice age glacier. no new water is being created — it just keeps cycling.

the water cycle (hydrological cycle) has four main stages:

1. evaporation — the sun heats surface water (oceans, lakes, rivers), converting liquid water into water vapor (gas). about 86% of evaporation happens from the oceans. plants also release water vapor through their leaves in a process called transpiration — a single large tree can release 400 litres of water per day.

2. condensation — as water vapor rises into the atmosphere, it cools. cool air holds less moisture, so the vapor condenses into tiny water droplets that form clouds. each cloud droplet is about 10 micrometres (0.01 mm) — too small to fall.

3. precipitation — cloud droplets collide and merge until they are heavy enough to fall. this is rain (or snow, sleet, hail depending on temperature). a typical raindrop is about 2 mm — roughly 200 times larger than a cloud droplet, containing about 1 million merged droplets.

4. collection — water flows downhill through rivers and streams (surface runoff), soaks into the ground (infiltration), or collects in lakes and oceans. eventually, it evaporates again, and the cycle repeats.

the entire cycle is powered by just two things: solar energy (drives evaporation) and gravity (drives precipitation and runoff).

check yourself: why does it rain more over mountains than flat areas? (answer: mountains force air upward. rising air cools, causing water vapor to condense into clouds and rain. this is called orographic precipitation.) the water cycle is quantified by water budgets: global annual precipitation over land ≈ 110,000 km³, of which 40% becomes runoff (rivers) and 60% returns to the atmosphere via evapotranspiration. the penman–monteith equation estimates evapotranspiration from solar radiation, temperature, humidity, and wind speed — the standard method used by the fao for irrigation planning. residence time (volume/flow rate) reveals how long water stays in each reservoir: atmosphere 9 days, rivers 2 weeks, soil moisture 1–2 months, shallow groundwater 100–200 years, deep groundwater 10,000 years, and ocean 3,000 years. the brahmaputra’s annual discharge of 510 km³ represents about 30% of india’s total surface water resources, underscoring ne india’s hydrological significance. hydrological modelling uses physically based frameworks like the variable infiltration capacity (vic) model or swat (soil and water assessment tool), which discretise a watershed into grid cells and simulate water and energy balances including snowmelt, soil infiltration (richards’ equation), groundwater flow (darcy’s law: q = −ka·dh/dl), and river routing (saint-venant equations). isotope hydrology uses ratios of ¹⁸o/¹⁶o and ²h/¹h (deuterium) in water to trace origins: monsoon rain in ne india has a distinct isotopic signature (δ¹⁸o ≈ −6 to −10‰) that differs from himalayan snowmelt (δ¹⁸o ≈ −12 to −18‰), enabling separation of flow contributions during floods. remote sensing (grace satellite gravimetry) measures monthly changes in total water storage across the brahmaputra basin to ±2 cm equivalent water height, revealing groundwater depletion trends invisible to surface observations. groundwater — an invisible ocean under your feet hydrogroundwaterdiagram when it rains, not all the water flows into rivers. a large fraction soaks into the ground — this is infiltration. the water trickles downward through soil and rock, filling the tiny spaces between grains of sand, pebbles, and cracks in rock. this underground water is called groundwater, and there is a staggering amount of it.

groundwater accounts for about 30% of all freshwater on earth — far more than all the world's rivers and lakes combined (which hold less than 1%). only the ice caps and glaciers hold more freshwater. if you could pump out all the groundwater and spread it evenly over earth's land surface, it would be about 55 metres deep.

groundwater moves, but extremely slowly — typically a few metres per year, compared to the kilometres per day that river water travels. it follows gravity, flowing from higher elevations to lower ones, and eventually seeps out into rivers, lakes, springs, or the ocean.

about 2 billion people — roughly one quarter of the world's population — depend on groundwater as their primary drinking water source. in india, groundwater supplies about 85% of rural drinking water and 60% of irrigation.

the problem: in many places, we are pumping groundwater out faster than rain can replenish it. the water table (the upper surface of the groundwater) is dropping — in parts of northern india, by over 1 metre per year. wells that worked for decades are going dry. this is one of the most serious and least visible environmental crises in the world.

check yourself: why is groundwater generally cleaner than surface water from rivers? (answer: as water percolates down through layers of soil and rock, the grains act as a natural filter, trapping bacteria, sediment, and many pollutants. it is nature's own purification system.) aquifers — underground sponges hydroaquiferdiagram analogy: imagine soaking a kitchen sponge with water. the sponge is solid, but it is full of tiny holes (pores) that hold water. squeeze the sponge and water flows out. an aquifer is a layer of underground rock or gravel that works exactly the same way.

two properties determine how good a rock is at being an aquifer:

porosity — the percentage of the rock that is empty space. sandstone has high porosity (about 20-30% air space). granite has almost zero porosity. higher porosity means more water can be stored.

permeability — how easily water can flow through the connected spaces. a rock can be porous but not permeable if the spaces are not connected (like a sponge wrapped in plastic). gravel is highly permeable — water flows through it easily. clay is porous (it holds water) but has very low permeability — the spaces are too small for water to flow through quickly. this is why clay layers act as barriers.

an aquifer typically sits between two impermeable layers (like clay or solid granite). the water table is the upper surface of the saturated zone within the aquifer.
