redundant example — leucine has 6 codons: third position the code is (nearly) universal: common ancestor wobble base pairing — why fewer trnas than codons are needed: wobble hypothesis ~45 different trna anticodon types the code minimises mutation damage — and we can prove it: 1 million randomly generated codes >99.99% silent stop codon nicknames exceptions to the "universal" code: genetic drift expanding the genetic code — synthetic biology frontier: compressed genome non-canonical amino acids (ncaas) worked example — counting synonymous vs non-synonymous mutations: 3 synonymous 33% of single-nucleotide mutations in gcu are silent exactly the same dna different pages. this is what differentiation is. epigenetics 20-30% two levels of gene control: level 1 — transcription factors (the switches): activators repressors level 2 — epigenetics (the dimmer knobs): without altering the dna sequence itself silences tightly wound loosely wound environment multi-level gene regulation: no lactose present: lactose present: glucose also present: histone code — the language of chemical tags: activation silencing dna methylation and cpg islands: 70% of human gene promoters dnmt1 epigenetic reprogramming — erasing and rewriting the marks: tet enzyme pathway: genomic imprinting — parent-of-origin gene expression: transgenerational epigenetic inheritance: despite never being conditioned themselves worked example — predicting gene silencing from epigenetic marks: silenced epigenetic cancer therapy single-cell epigenomics: molecular-biology molecular biology & protein synthesis transcription translation amino acid protein folding from gene to protein — how cells read dna instructions and build the machinery of life. why-the-muga-silk-is-golden dna replication watch the animation above. that yellow blob at the fork? it's helicase — a molecular motor that unzips the dna double helix at 1,000 base pairs per second. right behind it, dna polymerase (the red proteins) reads each exposed strand and builds a new complementary strand, one base at a time. the result: two identical double helices from one.

 this is happening in every dividing cell in your body right now, with near-perfect accuracy. the error rate is about 1 wrong base per billion — like typing out war and peace ten times and making a single letter mistake. how? polymerase has a built-in proofreading mechanism: if it puts down the wrong base, it feels the mismatch, reverses, and corrects. then a second team of repair enzymes sweeps behind looking for anything polymerase missed. then another team catches damage from uv light and chemicals. three layers of quality control to keep your dna intact.

 this process happens billions of times in your life. every single cell you have — every gut lining cell, every skin cell, every immune cell that just divided — started with a helicase unzipping a helix and two polymerases getting to work.

 the four bases and their pairing rules:

 | base | pairs with | memory trick |
 |------|-----------|-------------|
 | adenine (a) | thymine (t) | apple-tree |
 | thymine (t) | adenine (a) | tree-apple |
 | cytosine (c) | guanine (g) | car-garage |
 | guanine (g) | cytosine (c) | garage-car |

 replication works like unzipping a jacket and making a mirror copy of each half:

 1. helicase (the unzipper) breaks the bonds between base pairs and pries the two strands apart
 2. primase lays down a short rna starter (primer) — dna polymerase needs a "running start"
 3. dna polymerase reads each exposed strand and builds a new matching strand, base by base
 4. dna ligase (the glue) seals any gaps

 the result: two identical dna molecules, each containing one original strand and one new strand. this is called semiconservative replication — each copy conserves half of the original.

 how accurate is the copy?

 | stage | error rate |
 |-------|----------|
 | initial base selection by polymerase | 1 error per 100,000 bases |
 | after proofreading (polymerase checks its own work) | 1 error per 10,000,000 bases |
 | after mismatch repair enzymes patrol | 1 error per 1,000,000,000 bases |

 that final error rate means roughly 3 mistakes across the entire 3.2 billion base-pair human genome per cell division — like copying 800 copies of the mahabharata and making only 3 typos total. replication fork machinery in detail:

 the replication fork is a y-shaped structure where the dna is being actively unwound and copied. dna polymerase can only build new dna in the 5' to 3' direction, which creates an asymmetry:

 | strand | direction | how it's made | named after |
 |--------|----------|--------------|------------|
 | leading strand | same as fork movement | continuous synthesis | leads the way |
 | lagging strand | opposite to fork movement | short fragments joined later | lags behind |

 the lagging strand is built in okazaki fragments (100-200 nucleotides in eukaryotes, 1,000-2,000 in bacteria), each starting from its own rna primer. dna ligase then seals the fragments into a continuous strand.

tagline: Pitch, rhythm, harmony, and the science hiding inside every song.

functions tabla syahi finite element analysis music-theory-physics music theory & the physics of sound music-arts pitch, rhythm, harmony, and the science hiding inside every song. basket-weavers-song music-dimasa bamboo-flute-of-nagaland what is sound? musicalwavesdiagram sound is a mechanical wave — a vibration that travels through a medium (air, water, wood, metal) by causing molecules to bump into each other in a chain reaction. unlike light, sound cannot travel through a vacuum because there are no molecules to transmit the vibration. when a drum skin vibrates, it pushes the air molecules in front of it together (a compression), and when it moves back, it creates a region of lower pressure (a rarefaction). these alternating compressions and rarefactions propagate outward as a sound wave.

try this: put your hand on your throat and hum. feel the vibrations? those vibrations push on the air, creating the sound that reaches someone else's ears. now hum a higher note — feel how the vibrations get faster? that's frequency changing in real time, right under your fingertips.

the frequency of a sound wave — the number of complete vibrations per second, measured in hertz (hz) — determines its pitch. a high-frequency vibration (say, 4,000 hz) sounds high-pitched, like a bird's chirp. a low-frequency vibration (say, 80 hz) sounds low-pitched, like a bass drum. the human ear can detect frequencies from roughly 20 hz to 20,000 hz, though this range narrows with age. middle c on a piano vibrates at about 262 hz, meaning the string oscillates back and forth 262 times every second.

the amplitude of the wave — the height of the compression peaks — determines volume (loudness). a louder sound has bigger amplitude, meaning the molecules are displaced farther from their resting position.

the speed of sound depends on the medium. in air at room temperature, sound travels at about 343 metres per second (roughly 1,235 km/h). in water it travels about 4.3 times faster (1,480 m/s), and in steel about 15 times faster (5,960 m/s). this is because molecules in denser, stiffer materials transmit vibrations more quickly.

check yourself: if you see lightning and count 3 seconds before hearing thunder, how far away was the lightning strike? (answer: about 1 km — sound travels 343 m/s, so 343 x 3 = roughly 1,029 metres.) the relationship v = fλ lets you calculate wavelength for any pitch. middle c (262 hz) in air: λ = 343/262 = 1.31 m. the lowest piano note a0 (27.5 hz): λ = 343/27.5 = 12.5 m — the wave is literally longer than a room! the highest piano note c8 (4,186 hz): λ = 343/4,186 = 0.082 m (8.2 cm). sound intensity in watts per square metre: a conversation at 1 m is roughly 10⁻⁶ w/m², while a rock concert hits 1 w/m² — a million times more power. the inverse square law means doubling your distance cuts intensity to 1/4: at 2 m from a speaker, you get 1/4 the intensity compared to 1 m. in decibels: 10 × log₁₀(1/4) = −6 db per doubling of distance. sound propagation is governed by the linear wave equation: ∂²p/∂x² = (1/c²)∂²p/∂t², where p is pressure deviation and c is sound speed. the general solution is p(x,t) = f(x − ct) + g(x + ct) — two waves traveling in opposite directions. in enclosed spaces, room acoustics uses the sabine equation for reverberation time: t₆₀ = 0.161v/a, where v is room volume (m³) and a = σαᵢsᵢ is total absorption (absorption coefficient × surface area). a concert hall (v = 10,000 m³) with average α = 0.3 and surface 3,000 m²: t₆₀ = 0.161 × 10,000 / 900 = 1.79 s — ideal for orchestral music. psychoacoustics research shows that the just-noticeable-difference (jnd) for pitch is about 5 cents (1/20 of a semitone) for trained listeners, corresponding to a frequency change of 0.3%. the brain processes pitch in the auditory cortex using tonotopic maps — neurons arranged by their preferred frequency, mirroring the cochlea's spatial frequency decomposition. tone-player harmonics — why a guitar sounds different from a flute musicharmonicsdiagram here is a mystery: a guitar and a flute can both play middle c (262 hz). the pitch is identical. yet you can tell them apart instantly, even with your eyes closed. why?

the answer is harmonics (also called overtones). when a guitar string vibrates at 262 hz, it does not vibrate only at that frequency. it simultaneously vibrates at 2x (524 hz), 3x (786 hz), 4x (1,048 hz), 5x, 6x, and beyond — all at the same time. the 262 hz vibration is the fundamental (the pitch you perceive), and the higher multiples are the harmonics. each harmonic has a different loudness relative to the fundamental, and this mix is what gives each instrument its unique timbre (tone color).

a guitar has strong harmonics all the way up to the 6th or higher — the string's stiffness, the resonating wooden body, and the way you pluck or strum all contribute upper harmonics. the resulting waveform is complex and jagged when you add all these sine waves together.

a flute, by contrast, produces a nearly pure tone — the fundamental is very strong, the 2nd harmonic is weak, and higher harmonics are almost absent. this is because the smooth, cylindrical air column and the way air is blown across the embouchure produce very clean vibrations. the resulting waveform looks almost like a textbook sine wave.

the key insight: the same fundamental pitch + different harmonic recipes = different timbres. this is why orchestras sound rich — every instrument adds its own harmonic fingerprint to the mix.

predict before you read on: would a square wave (like old 8-bit video game music) sound more like a flute or more like a buzzy synth? think about what shape implies about harmonics. (answer: a square wave contains all odd harmonics at high amplitudes — 3rd, 5th, 7th, etc. — making it sound buzzy and electronic, not smooth like a flute.) the harmonic series of a note at fundamental frequency f₁: f₁, 2f₁, 3f₁, 4f₁, 5f₁... for a2 (110 hz): harmonics are 220, 330, 440, 550, 660, 770 hz... these co

title: Natural Resources & Sustainability
tagline: Forests, water, minerals, and energy — using Earth\
searchText:
natural resources & sustainability forests, water, minerals, and energy — using earth\ renewable vs non-renewable classify each resource as renewable or non-renewable water resources forest resources energy resources sustainability reserves-to-production (r/p) ratio hubbert curve energy return on energy invested (eroei) hotelling’s rule natural capital accounting payment for ecosystem services (pes) life cycle assessment (lca) natural-resources natural resources & sustainability forests, water, minerals, and energy — using earth's gifts without using them up. girl-grew-forest kaziranga-grass golden-hilsa honey-hunters-lesson renewable vs non-renewable resourcerenewablediagram classify each resource as renewable or non-renewable solar energy, wind, hydropower renewable — replenished naturally within a human lifetime coal, petroleum, natural gas non-renewable — took millions of years to form, finite supply timber and bamboo (managed sustainably) renewable — regrows if forests are allowed to recover metal ores (iron, copper, aluminium) non-renewable — mined from finite geological deposits natural resources are materials and energy sources that humans extract from the environment to meet their needs. they fall into two broad categories. renewable resources can be replenished naturally within a human lifetime — solar energy, wind energy, hydropower, timber (if forests are managed sustainably), fish (if harvesting does not exceed the reproduction rate), and fresh water (recycled through the water cycle). non-renewable resources exist in finite quantities and take millions of years to form — fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, natural gas), metal ores (iron, copper, aluminium), and mineral deposits. once consumed, they cannot be replaced on any timescale meaningful to human civilisation.

northeast india is endowed with both categories in abundance. the non-renewable resources include significant petroleum and natural gas reserves in the brahmaputra valley — digboi in upper assam was the site of india's first commercial oil refinery, established in 1901, and the assam-arakan basin continues to produce oil and gas today, though at declining rates compared to peak production. meghalaya has coal, limestone, and uranium deposits. on the renewable side, ne india has enormous hydroelectric potential (estimated at 63,000 mw — about 40% of india's total hydropower potential), abundant rainfall for water resources, vast forests, and increasingly viable solar and wind energy. the region also has rich biodiversity resources that support livelihoods — fisheries in the brahmaputra system, bamboo forests (the region produces about 60% of india's bamboo), and non-timber forest products like honey, medicinal plants, and wild foods.

the critical distinction is that "renewable" does not mean "infinite" or "impossible to destroy." forests are renewable only if they are allowed to regrow; if deforestation outpaces regeneration, the resource is effectively depleted. fish stocks are renewable only if harvesting is sustainable; overfishing collapses populations. even water, the most renewable of resources, can be locally depleted if extraction (from groundwater, for instance) exceeds recharge. the sustainability of a resource depends entirely on how it is managed. this is the central challenge of resource geography: understanding not just where resources are and how much exists, but how to use them at rates and in ways that allow continued availability for future generations. resource depletion is quantified by the reserves-to-production (r/p) ratio — known reserves divided by annual extraction. global r/p ratios as of 2023: oil ≈ 50 years, natural gas ≈ 49 years, coal ≈ 139 years. but these are moving targets — new discoveries and extraction technologies (like hydraulic fracturing) shift reserves, while demand growth accelerates production. the hubbert curve models peak production of a finite resource as a bell-shaped curve; global conventional oil likely peaked around 2005–2008. energy return on energy invested (eroei) measures efficiency: conventional oil yields 20:1 (20 units of energy per 1 invested in extraction), solar pv 10:1, and tar sands 3:1. resources with eroei below 5:1 struggle to sustain industrial civilisation. ne india’s petroleum reserves have declining eroei as fields mature, making the shift to renewables both ecologically and economically necessary. resource economics uses hotelling’s rule: the price of an exhaustible resource in a competitive market rises at the rate of interest, because owners must earn the same return from leaving the resource in the ground as from extracting and investing the proceeds. natural capital accounting (following the un system of environmental-economic accounting, seea) assigns monetary values to ecosystem services: kaziranga’s flood attenuation, water purification, and carbon sequestration are worth an estimated ₹2,000–3,000 crore per year. payment for ecosystem services (pes) programmes pay landholders to maintain forests, wetlands, or biodiversity — sikkim’s organic farming certification and meghalaya’s community forestry are nascent pes models. life cycle assessment (lca) evaluates a product’s total environmental footprint from raw material extraction through disposal, revealing hidden costs — a cotton t-shirt requires 2,700 litres of water to produce. water resources water covers 71% of the earth's surface, but the vast majority — about 97.5% — is saltwater in the oceans. of the 2.5% that is freshwater, about 69% is locked in glaciers and ice caps, about 30% is groundwater, and less than 1% is in accessible surface water (rivers, lakes, wetlands, and soil moisture). this means that only about 0.007% of earth's total water is readily available for human use — a sobering fraction for a resource that every person, farm, factory, and ecosystem depends on. the water cycle — evaporation from oceans and lakes, transportation as water vapour by winds, condensation into clouds, precipitation as rain or snow, collection in rivers and groundwater — continuously recycles this limited supply. but the distribution is uneven: some regions have far more water than they can use, while others face chronic water scarcity.

the brahmaputra is one of the largest rivers in the world by water volume, carrying an average discharge of about 19,800 cubic metres per second — surpassed only by the amazon, congo, and ganges-brahmaputra combined at the delta. it drains a basin of 580,000 square kilometres across tibet, bhutan, india, and bangladesh. for ne india, the brahmaputra and its tributaries (subansiri, manas, teesta, barak, and dozens of others) are the lifeline — providing water for agriculture, fisheries, transportation, drinking water, and hydroelectric power. the annual floods, while devastating, deposit nutrient-rich silt that maintains the fertility of the valley's agricultural land. wetlands (beels) in the floodplain serve as natural water reservoirs, fish nurseries, and biodiversity hotspots.

despite this apparent abundance, water challenges in ne india are real and growing. groundwater levels in parts of the brahmaputra valley are declining due to increased extraction for irrigation and urban water supply. water quality is threatened by industrial effluents, coal mining runoff (particularly in meghalaya's jaintia hills, where acid mine drainage has rendered streams lifeless), agricultural chemicals, and untreated urban sewage. the brahmaputra itself is a transboundary river — china's plans for hydroelectric dams on the yarlung tsangpo in tibet raise concerns about downstream impacts on water flow and sediment load, which could affect agriculture and flood patterns in assam and bangladesh. climate change adds another layer of uncertainty: himalayan glacial retreat threatens the river's dry-season flow, while increasingly erratic monsoons make water availability less predictable. watercyclediagram forest resources resour

bold signal 7-tesla ultra-high-field mri magnetoencephalography (meg) two-photon microscopy neuropixels brain initiative short-term (working) memory ram 7 items long-term memory hard drive 2.5 petabytes sleep hippocampus episodic semantic procedural george miller 4 chunks phonological loop rapid pattern separator sharp-wave ripples thalamocortical spindles engram cells optogenetics reconsolidation memory palaces try this with a friend: 0.25 seconds light hitting your retina → signal to brain: brain processing and decision: brain sending signal to arm muscles: muscles contracting: finger movement: age alertness practice type of stimulus d = ½gt² t = √(2d/g) 0.175 s = 175 ms choice reaction time hick's law cognitive stages erp (event-related potential) components lateralised readiness potential (lrp) race model drift-diffusion models artificial neural network artificial neurons connections between nodes weights learning 86 billion neurons 100 trillion connections megawatts backpropagation output = f(σ(wᵢxᵢ) + b) sigmoid relu softmax wᵢ ← wᵢ − η × ∂loss/∂wᵢ convolutional neural network (cnn) recurrent, sparse, and asynchronous feedforward, dense, and synchronous spiking neural networks (snns) transformer architecture self-attention neuroscience-and-brain neuroscience & the brain neuroscience your brain is an electrical storm — 86 billion neurons firing at lightning speed. your brain is an electrical storm brainneurondiagram right now, as you read this sentence, approximately 86 billion neurons in your brain are firing electrical signals. each neuron is a tiny cell — thinner than a hair — that works like a miniature wire with a switch.

imagine a stadium of 86,000 people doing "the wave." one section stands up, the section next to them sees it and stands up too, and the wave ripples around the stadium. that is essentially how your brain works, except the "wave" is an electrical pulse called an action potential, it travels at up to 120 metres per second (430 km/h), and instead of 86,000 people, you have 86 billion neurons — more neurons than there are stars in the milky way.

each neuron has three main parts:
- dendrites — branch-like extensions that receive signals from other neurons (the "ears")
- cell body (soma) — processes the incoming signals and decides whether to fire (the "brain of the brain cell")
- axon — a long cable that carries the signal to the next neuron (the "voice")

the axon is wrapped in a fatty coating called myelin that works like the rubber insulation around an electrical wire — it speeds up signal transmission and prevents the signal from leaking away. diseases that damage myelin (like multiple sclerosis) cause signals to slow down or stop, leading to movement and coordination problems.

check yourself: if a single neuron fires and passes its signal to 1,000 connected neurons, and each of those fires and passes to 1,000 more, how many neurons are involved after just 3 steps? (answer: 1 + 1,000 + 1,000,000 + 1,000,000,000 = over a billion — showing how quickly information spreads through the brain.) the human brain weighs 1.4 kg (2% of body mass) but consumes 20% of the body's oxygen and glucose — about 20 watts of power. it contains 86 billion neurons and roughly an equal number of glial cells (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglia). neurons vary enormously: purkinje cells in the cerebellum have 200,000 dendritic spines each; granule cells in the dentate gyrus are tiny with only 4–5 dendrites. axon diameters range from 0.1 μm (unmyelinated c-fibres carrying pain signals at 0.5–2 m/s) to 20 μm (myelinated motor neurons at 120 m/s). the total length of myelinated axons in a human brain is 150,000 km — roughly the distance from earth to the moon and halfway back. saltatory conduction between nodes of ranvier (spaced 1 mm apart) increases speed 50× compared to continuous conduction and reduces energy costs by 5,000× (only the nodes need ion channel activity). the brain's wiring follows small-world network topology: most connections are local (within cortical columns), but a small fraction of long-range connections create shortcuts that reduce the average path length between any two neurons to 4–6 synaptic steps. this architecture balances segregation (specialised processing in local circuits) and integration (global information sharing). the connectome — complete map of neural connections — has been fully mapped for c. elegans (302 neurons, 7,000 synapses). the human connectome project uses diffusion tensor imaging (dti) to map white matter tracts and resting-state fmri to map functional connectivity. at the micro level, serial electron microscopy now reconstructs cubic-millimetre volumes at synaptic resolution — the microns project mapped 75,000 neurons and 523 million synapses in 1 mm³ of mouse visual cortex (2024), revealing connectivity rules: excitatory neurons preferentially connect to neurons with similar orientation tuning, while inhibitory connections are less selective. how neurons talk — the synapse brainsynapsediagram neurons do not physically touch each other. between the axon terminal of one neuron and the dendrite of the next, there is a tiny gap — about 20 nanometres wide (a nanometre is a billionth of a metre). this gap is called the synapse.

analogy: passing a note in class. you cannot reach the person behind you, so you write a note and toss it across the gap. they pick it up, read it, and decide whether to pass it along.

here is what happens at a synapse:
1. an electrical signal arrives at the axon terminal
2. this triggers the release of tiny chemical messengers called neurotransmitters — they are stored in little bubbles called vesicles
3. the neurotransmitters float across the synaptic gap
4. they land on receptors on the next neuron — like a key fitting into a lock
5. if enough neurotransmitters bind to enough receptors, the next neuron fires its own electrical signal

different neurotransmitters do different things:
- dopamine — reward and motivation

surface-area-to-volume ratio sphere smallest the scaling law: critical radius neutron reflectors quantifying criticality k_eff = 1 surface-area-to-volume ratio for a sphere: sa/v = 3/r worked example — critical mass of u-235: ~49 kg ~52 kg reflector effect — beryllium (10 cm shell): comparison of fissile materials: neutron diffusion theory material buckling: geometric buckling for different shapes: weapons physics: implosion design weapons-grade chain reaction subcritical critical supercritical how reactors maintain k = 1: fuel rods moderator control rods coolant containment generation counting: the six-factor formula k_eff product ~1.000 the reactor period delayed neutrons save the day: ~0.65% point kinetics equations prompt criticality inherent safety mechanisms: positive nuclear-physics nuclear physics s nucleus — fission, fusion, and the energy that powers stars.", relatedstories: [ ], understand: [ // ── section 1: the nucleus ────────────────────────────────── { title: , diagram: , beginnercontent: s nehru stadium, the nucleus would be a pea on the centre spot — yet that pea would contain more than 99.9% of the stadium's mass. the nucleus is made of two particle types:

 | particle | charge | mass (u) | role in the nucleus |
 |----------|--------|----------|---------------------|
 | proton | +1 | 1.0073 | determines the element (z = atomic number) |
 | neutron | 0 | 1.0087 | adds mass, provides nuclear "glue" |

 together, protons and neutrons are called nucleons. the total number of nucleons is the mass number a (a = z + n).

 the puzzle: protons all carry positive charge and should fly apart due to electromagnetic repulsion. what holds them together? the strong nuclear force — the most powerful force in nature. it acts only over a femtometre (10⁻¹⁵ m) but within that range it overpowers electromagnetic repulsion by a factor of 100.

 | force | relative strength | range | what it does in the nucleus |
 |-------|-------------------|-------|----------------------------|
 | strong nuclear | 1 (strongest) | 1 fm | holds nucleons together |
 | electromagnetic | 1/137 | infinite | pushes protons apart |
 | weak nuclear | 10⁻⁶ | 0.001 fm | causes beta decay |
 | gravity | 10⁻³⁹ | infinite | negligible at nuclear scale |

 neutrons are the peacemakers — they add to the strong force without adding repulsion. heavier elements need more neutrons than protons to stay stable. lead-208, for instance, has 82 protons but 126 neutrons.

 binding energy is the energy needed to pull all nucleons apart. a higher binding energy per nucleon = a more tightly bound (more stable) nucleus. iron-56 sits at the peak — the most stable nucleus of all. lighter elements can release energy by fusing toward iron; heavier elements release energy by splitting away from it. this single fact explains why stars fuse hydrogen and power plants split uranium. nuclear size and density

 the nuclear radius follows: r = r₀ a^(1/3), where r₀ ≈ 1.2 fm.

 | nucleus | a | r (fm) | notes |
 |---------|---|--------|-------|
 | helium-4 | 4 | 1.9 | alpha particle |
 | carbon-12 | 12 | 2.7 | life's backbone |
 | iron-56 | 56 | 4.6 | most stable nucleus |
 | uranium-238 | 238 | 7.4 | heaviest natural element |

 worked example — iron-56 radius:
 r = 1.2 × 56^(1/3) = 1.2 × 3.83 = 4.59 fm

 nuclear density is roughly constant at ρ ≈ 2.3 × 10¹⁷ kg/m³ — a teaspoon would weigh 6 billion tonnes. this constancy means nuclear matter is essentially incompressible, like a liquid (hence the "liquid drop" model).

 binding energy per nucleon (b/a):

 | nucleus | b (mev) | b/a (mev) | significance |
 |---------|---------|-----------|---------------|
 | ²h (deuterium) | 2.22 | 1.11 | weakly bound — easy to break |
 | ⁴he (helium) | 28.3 | 7.07 | extremely stable (doubly magic) |
 | ⁵⁶fe (iron) | 492 | 8.79 | peak stability |
 | ²³⁵u (uranium) | 1,784 | 7.59 | can release energy by fission |
 | ²³⁸u (uranium) | 1,802 | 7.57 | most common uranium isotope |

 the semi-empirical mass formula (bethe-weizsacker) estimates b:

 b = av a − as a^(2/3) − ac z(z−1)a^(-1/3) − aa (n−z)²/a + δ(a,z)

 | term | name | physics it captures |
 |------|------|---------------------|
 | av a | volume | bulk attraction — each nucleon attracts neighbours |
 | −as a^(2/3) | surface | surface nucleons have fewer neighbours |
 | −ac z(z−1)a^(-1/3) | coulomb | proton-proton repulsion |
 | −aa (n−z)²/a | asymmetry | penalty for unequal n and z |
 | δ(a,z) | pairing | even-even nuclei are more stable | the strong nuclear force is mediated by gluons exchanged between quarks inside nucleons. at the nuclear level, this manifests as the residual strong force (nuclear force), analogous to how the van der waals force is a residual electromagnetic effect.

 yukawa potential:

 v(r) = −g² exp(−r/r₀) / r

 where r₀ ≈ 1.4 fm, corresponding to the pion mass via r₀ = h/(mπ c). the pion (π) is the exchange particle at nuclear distances.

 nuclear shell model (nobel prize 1963, goeppert mayer/jensen) explains magic numbers:

 | magic number | nucleon count | doubly-magic examples |
 |-------------|---------------|----------------------|
 | 2 | 2p or 2n | ⁴he (2p, 2n) |
 | 8 | 8p or 8n | ¹⁶o (8p, 8n) |
 | 20 | 20p or 20n | ⁴⁰ca (20p, 20n) |
 | 28 | 28p or 28n | ⁴⁸ca (20p, 28n) |
 | 50 | 50p or 50n | — |
 | 82 | 82p or 82n | ²⁰⁸pb (82p, 126n) |
 | 126 | 126n | ²⁰⁸pb (82p, 126n) |

 magic numbers arise from spin-orbit coupling splitting energy levels, creating large gaps in the nuclear energy spectrum.

' + 'the pattern repeats every 6 steps (this is : a^(p−1) ≡ 1 (mod p) for prime p when gcd(a, p) = 1).

' + ' — reduce 100 mod 6. 100 = 16 × 6 + 4, so 100 mod 6 = 4. therefore 3^100 ≡ 3⁴ ≡ rsa encryption — primes and modular arithmetic, hand in glove. euler's theorem why it is secure. the quantum threat. shor's algorithm post-quantum cryptography number-theory-and-sequences number theory & sequences primes, factors, and the hidden patterns inside numbers themselves. boy-counted-butterflies bamboo-grows-fast factors and multiples tara has 24 chocolates and a question. can she share them equally among her friends? it depends on how many friends. with 6 friends, each gets 4. with 4 friends, each gets 6. with 5 friends — uh oh — 24 doesn't split evenly into 5.

 

 the numbers that do split 24 evenly are 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, and 24. we call them the factors of 24.

 two key words.

 | word | definition | example |
 |------|-----------|---------|
 | factor of n | a number that divides n with no remainder | 6 is a factor of 24 because 24 ÷ 6 = 4 |
 | multiple of n | a number you get by multiplying n by a positive integer | 14 is a multiple of 7 (because 7 × 2 = 14) |

 note the symmetry: if a is a factor of b, then b is a multiple of a. the pair always travels together.

 the dna of every number — prime factorisation. take any number bigger than 1 and break it down into the smallest pieces that still multiply to give the original. those pieces are prime numbers (more on these in the next section). the breakdown is unique: there is exactly one way to do it. that fact is so important it has a name — the fundamental theorem of arithmetic.

 walked example — factorise 360.

 | step | what to do | result so far |
 |------|-----------|---------------|
 | 1 | 360 is even, divide by 2 | 360 = 2 × 180 |
 | 2 | 180 is even, divide by 2 | 360 = 2 × 2 × 90 |
 | 3 | 90 is even, divide by 2 | 360 = 2³ × 45 |
 | 4 | 45 is not even. divisible by 3? (4+5=9, yes) | 360 = 2³ × 3 × 15 |
 | 5 | 15 is divisible by 3 | 360 = 2³ × 3² × 5 |
 | 6 | 5 is prime — done | 360 = 2³ × 3² × 5 |

 counting the factors of a number — without listing them. if the prime factorisation is n = p₁^a · p₂^b · p₃^c, the number of factors is (a+1)(b+1)(c+1). for 360 = 2³ · 3² · 5¹, that is (3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 24 factors. try listing them — yes, 24 of them: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 15, 18, 20, 24, 30, 36, 40, 45, 60, 72, 90, 120, 180, 360.

 check yourself. without listing, how many factors does 1000 = 2³ × 5³ have? (answer: (3+1)(3+1) = 16 factors.)

 where this shows up.

 | setting | the factor question |
 |---------|---------------------|
 | sharing 120 students into equal groups | what are the factors of 120? |
 | filling 750-ml bottles from a 15-litre tin of mustard oil | does 15000 ÷ 750 leave no remainder? yes — 20 bottles. |
 | cutting a 36 m × 24 m field into equal square plots, biggest possible | common factor of 36 and 24 — 12 m × 12 m squares |
 | tiles of size 8 cm × 12 cm covering a 96 × 84 floor with no cutting | does 8 divide 96? 12 divide 84? yes to both. | the euclidean algorithm — finding gcd without listing factors. listing every factor of two big numbers is slow. the euclidean algorithm is over 2,300 years old and still the way computers do it.

 idea. gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a mod b). replace the bigger number with the remainder, repeat. the last non-zero remainder is the gcd.

 worked example — gcd(252, 198).

 | step | division | remainder |
 |------|----------|-----------|
 | 1 | 252 = 1 × 198 + 54 | 54 |
 | 2 | 198 = 3 × 54 + 36 | 36 |
 | 3 | 54 = 1 × 36 + 18 | 18 |
 | 4 | 36 = 2 × 18 + 0 | 0 — stop |

 last non-zero remainder is gcd(252, 198) = 18.

 why it terminates fast. lamé's theorem (1844) says the number of steps never exceeds 5 times the number of digits of the smaller input. so gcd on two 100-digit numbers takes at most 500 steps — milliseconds, not lifetimes.

 sum of factors — same trick. for 360 = 2³ × 3² × 5, the sum of all 24 factors is

 (1 + 2 + 4 + 8) × (1 + 3 + 9) × (1 + 5) = 15 × 13 × 6 = 1170

 each factor appears in the expanded product exactly once. the prime number theorem. sound the trumpet. among the first n positive integers, the number of primes is approximately

 π(n) ≈ n / ln n

 for n = 1,000,000 the prediction is 72,382 primes. the actual count is 78,498 — a relative error of 8%, and the error keeps shrinking as n grows. riemann's 1859 paper conjectured a much sharper formula whose error term depends on the location of zeros of the riemann zeta function ζ(s) = σ 1/nˢ.

 the riemann hypothesis. all non-trivial zeros of ζ(s) lie on the line re(s) = 1/2. if true, it pins down the error in the prime-counting function precisely. it has been verified numerically for the first ten trillion zeros — but no proof. carries a $1,000,000 millenn

title: NumPy
tagline: Fast math on arrays of numbers
searchText:
numpy fast math on arrays of numbers linear algebra matrix vector eigenvalue dot product broadcasting gaussian why arrays matter the speed difference — lists vs arrays creating arrays — from lists and from scratch math on entire arrays — no loops needed filtering data — boolean indexing real-world data shapes match the numpy function to what it does creating arrays vectorized math — no loops statistics on arrays boolean indexing — filtering data reshaping and slicing real-world example: analyzing sensor data describe the array you want, don't build it one element at a time. 50-100× faster linear algebra in numpy — the operations behind ml: memory layout matters for speed: practical rule: advanced indexing: the performance stack under numpy: the speed difference is staggering: ~500,000× debugging slow numpy code: views vs copies — the most common numpy bug: view rules of thumb: copy memory-mapped files — when data exceeds ram: 2 for x in my_list]` ' + 'with `my_array broadcasting rules — how numpy handles mismatched shapes: the two rules: step-by-step example: outer product via broadcasting: einstein summation 4280 linear regression with numpy — the math behind "best fit line": β = (xᵀx)⁻¹ xᵀy worked example in numpy: image processing — images are just arrays: the data pipeline — how numpy connects to everything: tabular data pipeline: image pipeline: audio pipeline: satellite/gis pipeline: correlation ≠ causation spearman vs pearson — choosing the right correlation: when they differ: how spearman works: partial correlation — removing confounders: data-science linear algebra eigenvalue dot product broadcasting fast math on arrays of numbers girl-who-spoke-to-elephants bamboo-flute-of-nagaland how-majuli-island-was-born why-the-muga-silk-is-golden why arrays matter play with the ruler in the diagram above. toggle linspace vs arange. slide the control. watch how numpy creates entire arrays of evenly-spaced numbers from just a handful of arguments. that's the numpy mindset: describe the array you want, don't build it one element at a time.

 imagine you have 1,000 temperature readings and you want to convert all of them from celsius to fahrenheit. with a plain python list, you would write a loop that visits each value one by one — 1,000 trips. numpy does the same conversion in a single operation on the whole array at once, often 50-100x faster. the secret is that numpy stores numbers in a tightly packed block of memory (like seats in a cinema row), so the processor can blast through them without stopping to look up each one. this speed difference does not matter for 10 numbers, but it is essential for millions — which is what real data science, machine learning, and image processing involve. numpy arrays vs python lists: np.arange(1000000).sum() runs 50-100× faster than sum(range(1000000)) because numpy uses compiled c loops on contiguous memory. array creation: np.zeros((3,4)), np.ones(5), np.linspace(0, 2np.pi, 100), np.random.randn(1000). array attributes: a.shape, a.dtype, a.ndim, a.size. reshaping: np.arange(12).reshape(3,4). broadcasting: a 2 doubles every element; a + np.array([1,2,3,4]) adds to every row. linear algebra in numpy — the operations behind ml:

 | operation | code | what it does |
 |---|---|---|
 | matrix multiply | a @ b | rows of a dot columns of b — core of neural networks |
 | inverse | np.linalg.inv(a) | a⁻¹ such that a @ a⁻¹ = i — used in least-squares regression |
 | eigenvalues | np.linalg.eig(a) | finds directions where a only scales (no rotation) — pca |
 | svd | np.linalg.svd(a) | decomposes any matrix into rotation × stretch × rotation — image compression, recommendation systems |
 | fft | np.fft.fft(signal) | converts time-domain signal to frequency spectrum |

 memory layout matters for speed:
 a 2d array can be stored row-by-row (c order, default) or column-by-column (fortran order). cpus load data in chunks (cache lines). if you iterate along the contiguous axis, each cache load gets you 8 useful values. if you iterate the wrong way, each cache load gets 1 useful value and 7 wasted ones → 3-10× slower.

 practical rule: if your array is c-order (default), iterate over rows (axis=1) for fast inner loops. if operations are slow, check with a.flags — if ccontiguous is false, call np.ascontiguousarray(a) to fix it.

 advanced indexing:
 - boolean mask: a[a > threshold] — select elements matching a condition
 - fancy indexing: a[[0, 3, 7]] — select specific indices
 - np.where(condition, x, y) — element-wise if/else: np.where(temps > 30, "hot", "ok") numpyrulerdiagram the speed difference — lists vs arrays a python list is like a drawer of labeled envelopes — each envelope can hold any type of object, so the computer has to open each one individually. a numpy array is like a sheet of graph paper where every cell holds the same type of number. because the computer knows exactly where each number is and that they are all the same type, it can process them in bulk using special cpu instructions (simd). for 1 million additions, a python list loop might take 200 milliseconds; numpy does it in 2 milliseconds. this is not a minor optimization — it is the difference between a program that feels instant and one that makes you wait. numpy achieves 50-100× speed over python lists through: (1) contiguous memory layout (no pointer chasing), (2) compiled c/fortran inner loops (no python interpreter overhead), (3) simd instructions (single instruction multiple data — process 4-8 numbers simultaneously). benchmark: squaring 1 million numbers — python list: 150ms, numpy: 1.5ms. the speedup increases with array size because the fixed overhead of calling numpy becomes negligible. memory efficiency: a python list of 1m integers uses 28 mb (each int is a 28-byte object), while a numpy int64 array uses 8 mb (raw 8-byte values in contiguous memory). the performance stack under numpy:

 when you write a @ b for 1000×1000 matrices, numpy doesn't use python at all. it calls down through layers of optimization:

 your code: a @ b (python)
 → numpy c code: selects the right blas routine
 → blas library (openblas or intel mkl): dgemm
 → cache-blocked loops (process 64×64 tiles that fit in l1 cache)
 → simd instructions (process 4-8 multiplications simultaneously)
 → multiple cpu cores (if mkl/openblas is multithreaded)
 the speed difference is staggering:
 | method | 1000×1000 matrix multiply | why |
 | python triple loop | 3 hours | interpreter overhead per operation |
 | numpy (openblas) | 20 ms | compiled c with simd + cache blocking |
 | speedup | 500,000× | |

 debugging slow numpy code:
 if your numpy code is slow, you almost certainly have an accidental python loop. common culprits:
 - for i in range(len(a)): result[i] = a[i] b[i] → replace with result = a b
 - appending to a list in a loop, then converting → pre-allocate with np.empty() and fill
 - calling a python function per element → use np.vectorize() (still slow) or rewrite as array operations true-false a numpy array stores all elements as the same data type, which makes it faster than a python list. uniform type means the cpu can process elements in bulk using simd instructions, avoiding per-element type checks. for 10 numbers, numpy is significantly faster than a regular python list. numpy has startup overhead. for tiny arrays the speed difference is negligible. the gains appear with thousands or millions of elements. creating arrays — from lists and from scratch you can create a numpy array from any python list: np.array([1, 2, 3]). but numpy also has helper functions to generate common arrays without typing every value. np.zeros(100) gives you 100 zeros. np.ones((3, 4)) gives a 3-by-4 grid of ones. np.arange(0, 10, 0.5) counts from 0 to 10 in steps of 0.5 — like python's range() but for decimals. np.linspace(0, 1, 50) gives 50 evenly spaced points from 0 to 1, which is perfect for plotting smooth curves. np.random.randn(1000) gives 1,00

505,000 km³ isotope tracing: rayleigh distillation worked example — monthly water balance (thornthwaite method): land-surface energy balance and climate feedbacks: latent heat flux bowen ratio budyko framework atmospheric rivers tracer hydrology the fast (biological) loop photosynthesis respiration decomposition ocean exchange the slow (geological) loop earth's carbon reservoirs — where carbon lives: worked example — carbon flux calculation: 5,060 t co₂/yr the ocean's carbon pump: solubility pump biological pump modelling the carbon cycle — box models and feedbacks: net accumulation +5.4 airborne fraction key climate-carbon feedbacks: keeling curve math: the five key steps: 1. fixation 2. nitrification 3. assimilation 4. ammonification 5. denitrification global nitrogen budget — natural vs human: haber-bosch process ~150 industrial: n₂ + 3h₂ → 2nh₃ (high t, high p) total human ~220 exceeds all natural fixation worked example — nitrogen budget for a rice paddy: total 100% 120 nitrogen use efficiency (nue): nitrogenase — the enzyme that breaks the triple bond: leghemoglobin nitrogen cascade: planetary boundary where does a forest store its carbon? the surprise: quick check: the nutrient recycling engine — litter decomposition: c:n ratio worked example — litter turnover: soil carbon dynamics — the two-pool model: active/labile slow/passive temperature sensitivity: mycorrhizal networks: soil forms in layers called horizons: | bedrock | unweathered solid rock | below c |

' + ' why topsoil matters for nutrient cycling: the soil nutrient triangle — n, p, k: nitrogen (n) phosphorus (p) potassium (k) worked example — fertiliser calculation: cation exchange capacity (cec): soil biogeochemistry — redox chemistry in waterlogged soils: denitrification methane alternate wetting and drying (awd) phosphorus — the non-cycling nutrient: the three big disruptions at a glance: carbon nitrogen water carbon disruption: nitrogen disruption: water disruption: tracking the damage — planetary boundaries: worked example — eutrophication from fertiliser runoff: carbon maths — the remaining budget: coupled biogeochemical modelling — why cycles interact: 20–30% less carbon uptake by land ecosystems stoichiometric constraints — the redfield ratio extended to land: the integrated solution: carbon: nitrogen: water: nutrient-cycles nutrient cycles carbon, nitrogen, water — the atoms that cycle endlessly through earth's living systems. girl-grew-forest first-rice kaziranga-grass the water cycle watercyclediagram imagine a single drop of water. over thousands of years, that same drop has been part of a glacier, a cloud, a river, a dinosaur's blood, and the cup of tea you drank this morning. water is never created or destroyed — it just keeps moving.

 the water cycle (hydrological cycle) has four main stages, each driven by solar energy and gravity:

 | stage | what happens | key numbers |
 |-------|-------------|-------------|
 | evaporation | liquid water becomes vapor (oceans, lakes, rivers) | oceans supply 86% of atmospheric vapor |
 | transpiration | plants release water through leaf pores (stomata) | a large tree releases 200–400 litres/day |
 | condensation | vapor cools at altitude, forms cloud droplets around particles (dust, pollen, sea salt) | clouds contain 0.001% of earth's water |
 | precipitation | droplets coalesce and fall as rain, snow, sleet, or hail | global average: 1,000 mm/year over land |

 together, evaporation and transpiration are called evapotranspiration — the combined water loss from a landscape.

 once precipitation reaches the ground, it follows three paths:

 | path | description | timescale |
 |------|------------|----------|
 | surface runoff | flows across land into streams, rivers, and lakes | hours to weeks |
 | infiltration | seeps into soil to become groundwater | days to thousands of years |
 | plant uptake | absorbed by roots, returned to air via transpiration | hours to days |

 some of the most extreme rainfall on earth occurs in meghalaya, where moisture-laden monsoon winds from the bay of bengal slam into the khasi hills. cherrapunji and mawsynram receive 10,000–12,000 mm per year — orographic lifting forces the air up, it cools, and water dumps out. rain falling on kaziranga's grasslands may have evaporated from the bay of bengal a week earlier and from a rice paddy in thailand a month before that.

 analogy: think of the water cycle as a giant distillery. the sun heats ocean water (the "still"), vapor rises and cools (the "condenser"), and pure water collects as rain (the "distillate"). dissolved salts stay behind — which is why rain is fresh but the ocean is salty. global water budget — where is all the water?

 | reservoir | volume (km³) | % of total | avg. residence time |
 |-----------|-------------|-----------|--------------------|
 | oceans | 1,335,000,000 | 96.5% | 3,000 years |
 | glaciers & ice caps | 26,350,000 | 1.74% | 10,000 years |
 | groundwater (fresh) | 10,530,000 | 0.76% | days–10,000 years |
 | freshwater lakes | 91,000 | 0.007% | 17 years |
 | soil moisture | 16,500 | 0.001% | 1–2 months |
 | atmosphere | 12,900 | 0.001% | 10 days |
 | rivers | 2,120 | 0.0002% | 2 weeks |

 the global cycle moves about 505,000 km³ of water per year through evaporation and precipitation.

 isotope tracing: hydrologists use ratios of heavy oxygen (¹⁸o) and heavy hydrogen (²h, deuterium) to trace water's journey. as air masses move poleward or upslope and cool, heavy isotopes preferentially condense out — a process called rayleigh distillation. this produces a characteristic relationship:

 δ²h = 8 × δ¹⁸o + 10 (the global meteoric water line)

 deviations from this line reveal whether rain came from recycled continental moisture (transpiration adds vapor without isotopic fractionation) or direct oceanic evaporation.

 worked example — monthly water balance (thornthwaite method):

 for guwahati, july: precipitation (p) = 320 mm, potential evapotranspiration (pet) = 140 mm.

 water surplus = p − pet = 320 − 140 = 180 mm

 this 180 mm surplus recharges groundwater and feeds river flow. in contrast, february: p = 15 mm, pet = 60 mm → deficit of 45 mm. plants draw on stored soil moisture, and farmers may need irrigation. the brahmaputra basin runs surplus june–october (monsoon) and deficit december–april, driving the seasonal rhythm of agriculture and flooding. land-surface energy balance and climate feedbacks:

 the water cycle is tightly coupled to the energy budget of earth's surface. the energy used to evaporate water is called latent heat flux (λe), and it represents a major pathway for cooling land surfaces.

 | component | formula | typical value (tropical forest) |
 |-----------|---------|--------------------------------|
 | net radiation | rn = (1−α)·sw↓ + lw↓ − lw↑ | 160 w/m² |
 | latent heat (evaporation) | λe | 100 w/m² (62%) |
 | sensible heat (warming air) | h | 40 w/m² (25%) |
 | ground heat flux | g | 20 w/m² (13%) |

 the bowen ratio (β = h / λe) characterizes a surface: forests have β ≈ 0.3–0.5 (most energy goes to evaporation), while deserts have β > 5 (most goes to heating air). deforestation increases β — less evaporation, hotter surfaces, altered cloud formation.

 the budyko framework relates a watershed's long-term water balance to the aridity index (pet/p). as climate warms, evaporative dema

' + ' worked example -- biconvex lens: 20 cm +5 d chromatic aberration and dispersion: abbe number achromatic doublet apochromatic the five seidel aberrations: gradient-index (grin) lenses the eye is a biological camera: how focusing works (accommodation): rods vs cones: common vision defects: the eye's optical system in numbers: corrective lens calculations: example 1 -- myopia: -2 d example 2 -- hyperopia: +3 d diffraction limit of the eye: 1.34 x 10^-4 rad = 0.46 arcminutes modulation transfer function (mtf): peak wavefront aberrometry lasik surgery: retinal neural preprocessing: lateral inhibition centre-surround motion-detection compression ratio: 

' + ' magnification = f_objective / f_eyepiece = 1000/10 = 100x reflecting telescope (concave mirror + eyepiece): compound microscope (2 convex lenses, reversed): 400x resolution limits -- why magnification alone is not enough: individual atoms telescope performance metrics: 816x 7.3 magnitudes 0.69 arcseconds microscope resolution: numerical aperture 1.4 240 nm james webb space telescope (jwst) -- engineering at the limit: 25 nm rms 0.063 arcseconds super-resolution microscopy (beyond the diffraction limit): electron microscopy -- why it works: 0.0025 nm when does light get trapped inside a material? critical angle total internal reflection (tir) critical angles for common materials: why diamonds sparkle: fibre optic cables: snell's window: calculating the critical angle: worked examples: 41.8 degrees 48.8 degrees 24.4 degrees numerical aperture (na) of an optical fibre: worked example: 0.56 34.1 degrees types of optical fibre: modal dispersion the evanescent wave: evanescent wave applications of the evanescent wave: frustrated tir the goos-hanchen effect (1947): 2 - n_clad optics-and-lenses optics & lenses how lenses bend light to let us see the very small and the very far. kingfisher-blue the-girl-who-painted-rain stars-ziro-valley dragonfly-and-the-paddy-field reflection -- mirrors and images the law of reflection is beautifully simple: the angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection, both measured from the normal (the line perpendicular to the mirror surface).

 > think of bouncing a ball off a wall -- it rebounds at the same angle it arrived. light does the same thing.

 three types of mirrors:

 | mirror type | shape | image | real or virtual? | everyday use |
 |------------|-------|-------|-----------------|-------------|
 | plane (flat) | flat surface | same size, upright, laterally inverted | virtual | bathroom mirror, dressing mirror |
 | concave (curved inward) | like inside of a spoon | varies with object distance | real or virtual | shaving mirror, torch reflector, solar cooker |
 | convex (curved outward) | like back of a spoon | smaller, upright, wide field of view | virtual | vehicle side mirrors, shop security mirrors |

 key terms:

 | term | meaning |
 |------|---------|
 | virtual image | light rays only appear to come from behind the mirror -- cannot be projected on a screen |
 | real image | light rays actually converge -- can be projected on a screen |
 | focal point (f) | where parallel rays converge (concave) or appear to diverge from (convex) |
 | focal length (f) | distance from mirror to focal point; f = r/2 where r is radius of curvature |
 | laterally inverted | left and right swapped (your left hand looks like right in a plane mirror) |

 concave mirrors converge parallel rays to a focal point. place an object beyond f and you get a real, inverted image that can be projected onto a screen -- this is how reflecting telescopes and solar concentrators work. place an object closer than f and the mirror produces an enlarged virtual image -- which is why shaving mirrors and makeup mirrors are concave.

 convex mirrors diverge light rays, producing a smaller, upright, virtual image that covers a wider field of view. vehicle side mirrors carry the warning "objects in mirror are closer than they appear" because the image is always smaller than reality.

 the parabolic solar cookers used by the indian army at high-altitude posts in arunachal pradesh use concave reflectors to focus sunlight onto a cooking vessel -- the same optics principle as a satellite dish, just applied to heat instead of radio signals. the mirror equation:

 1/f = 1/v + 1/u

 where f = focal length, v = image distance, u = object distance (sign convention: distances in front of mirror are negative for the object).

 worked example 1 -- concave mirror:

 f = 20 cm, object at u = 60 cm.

 | step | calculation |
 |------|------------|
 | apply mirror equation | 1/v = 1/f - 1/u = 1/20 - 1/60 |
 | find common denominator | = 3/60 - 1/60 = 2/60 |
 | solve for v | v = 30 cm (real image, in front of mirror) |
 | magnification | m = -v/u = -30/60 = -0.5 (inverted, half-size) |

 worked example 2 -- convex mirror:

 f = -15 cm (negative for convex), object at u = 30 cm.

 | apply mirror equation | 1/v = 1/(-15) - 1/30 = -2/30 - 1/30 = -3/30 |
 | solve for v | v = -10 cm (virtual, behind mirror) |
 | magnification | m = -(-10)/30 = +0.33 (upright, one-third size) |

 image summary for concave mirrors:

 | object position | image position | image nature | size |
 |----------------|---------------|-------------|------|
 | at infinity | at f | real, inverted | highly diminished |
 | beyond c | between f and c | real, inverted | diminished |
 | at c | at c | real, inverted | same size |
 | between f and c | beyond c | real, inverted | magnified |
 | at f | at infinity | -- | -- |
 | between f and p | behind mirror | virtual, upright | magnified |

 the radius of curvature r relates to f by f = r/2. parabolic mirrors eliminate spherical aberration (the imperfect focusing of rays far from the principal axis) that plagues spherical mirrors in telescopes. ray transfer matrices (abcd matrices):

 real optical systems are analysed by representing each element as a 2x2 matrix. a curved mirror with radius r:

 m = [[1, 0], [-2/r, 1]]

 cascading optical elements is done by matrix multiplication. for a cassegrain telescope with primary mirror m1 (f1 = 10 m) and secondary m2 (f2 = 0.5 m), the system matrix gives the effective focal length and magnification.

 adaptive optics:

 | parameter | typical value |
 |-----------|-------------|
 | deformable mirror actuators | 100-1000+ |
 | correction frequency | 1 khz |
 | wavefront accuracy | lambda/20 |
 | angular resolution achieved | 0.02 arcseconds (keck) |

 atmospheric turbulence distorts incoming starlight. adaptive optics systems measure the distortion using a guide star (real or laser-generated), then deform the mirror surface in real time to cancel the error -- achieving diffraction-limited resolution from the ground.

 x-rays penetrate most surfaces at normal incidence. wolter telescopes use grazing-incidence reflection at angles < 2 degrees from the surface, arranged in
