delocalised resonance energy markovnikov's rule worked example — propene + hbr: aromaticity — beyond benzene: 4n+2 pi electrons electrophilic aromatic substitution (eas) — the mechanism: directing effects — substituents control where the next group goes: learn the groups, and you can predict how any organic molecule will react. alcohol carboxylic acid amine aldehyde ketone ester jackfruit aroma ether amide ne india in your nose: jackfruit esters khar assam tea the polarity pattern: polar functional group transformations — one group becomes another: oxidation ladder — primary alcohols go through three stages: ethanol acetaldehyde acetic acid worked example — esterification of salicylic acid: aspirin infrared (ir) spectroscopy — identifying functional groups by their vibrations: worked example — identify the unknown compound: nucleophilic addition to carbonyls: tea chemistry — assam's liquid gold: catechins theaflavins thearubigins exact same pieces isomers structural isomers of c₄h₁₀ (butane): the isomer explosion: types of isomers — the family tree: structural geometric optical life chooses sides: l-form d-form muga silk fibroin geometric (cis-trans) isomerism — why it requires a double bond: cannot rotate e/z nomenclature optical isomerism — molecules as hands: four different groups chiral centre different worked example — identify chiral centres in alanine: chirality in drug design — when mirrors matter: thalidomide ibuprofen l-dopa naproxen determining configuration — cahn-ingold-prelog (cip) rules: , anticlockwise = | |

' + ' monomers synthetic polymers — the plastics in your life: two ways to make polymers: condensation nature is the original polymer chemist: cellulose starch silk fibroin rubber dna muga silk — chemistry makes it golden: beta-sheet carotenoid pigment what determines a polymer's properties? worked example — why is hdpe harder than ldpe? condensation polymerisation — nylon as a worked example: polymer crystallography — understanding silk at the molecular level: why gly-ala repeats? polymer degradation — the plastic crisis: biodegradable alternatives: organic-chemistry-intro organic chemistry intro s four bonds build the molecules of life — from methane to silk protein.", relatedstories: [ ], understand: [ // ── section 1: why carbon is special ────────────────────────── { title: , diagram: , beginnercontent: + s golden textile |

 the fibroin protein in muga silk contains carbon chains thousands of atoms long, folded into beta-sheet structures that give the silk its extraordinary strength and golden luster. from a single methane molecule to a silk fibre — carbon's four bonds make it all possible.

 quick check: why can carbon form such diverse compounds while most other elements cannot?

 because carbon has exactly four bonding directions arranged tetrahedrally, plus strong and stable c-c bonds. this lets it build chains, branches, and rings of unlimited length — something no other element can do reliably. orbital hybridisation — the source of carbon's flexibility:

 carbon's 2s and 2p orbitals can mix ("hybridise") in three different ways, each producing a different geometry:

 | hybridisation | orbitals mixed | shape | bond angle | example |
 |--------------|---------------|-------|------------|--------|
 | sp³ | 1s + 3p → 4 equivalent | tetrahedral | 109.5° | methane (ch₄), ethane |
 | sp² | 1s + 2p → 3 equivalent + 1 unhybridised p | trigonal planar | 120° | ethene (c₂h₄), benzene |
 | sp | 1s + 1p → 2 equivalent + 2 unhybridised p | linear | 180° | ethyne (c₂h₂) |

 what determines the hybridisation? count the regions of electron density (bonds + lone pairs) around the carbon:

 | regions | hybridisation | pi bonds present |
 |---------|--------------|------------------|
 | 4 | sp³ | 0 |
 | 3 | sp² | 1 (in the double bond) |
 | 2 | sp | 2 (in the triple bond) |

 electronegativity (2.55 on the pauling scale) is moderate — carbon forms stable bonds with both electropositive elements (h, metals) and electronegative ones (o, n, halogens). this versatility means carbon can be the backbone while other atoms provide chemical personality.

 worked example — determine the hybridisation of each carbon in acetic acid (ch₃cooh):

 - carbon 1 (ch₃): bonded to 3h + 1c = 4 regions → sp³ (tetrahedral)
 - carbon 2 (c=o, -oh): bonded to =o + -oh + ch₃ = 3 regions → sp² (trigonal planar)

 acetic acid is the key component of vinegar — and every bottle of vinegar has both sp³ and sp² carbons. retrosynthetic analysis — thinking backwards to build forwards:

 e. j. corey (nobel prize 1990) formalised the idea of working backward from a target molecule to simpler precursors. you identify disconnection points — bonds that can be formed by known reactions — and trace back to commercially available starting materials.

 | strategy | notation | what it means |
 |----------|---------|---------------|
 | disconnection | ⟹ | "can be made from..." |
 | synthon | charged fragment | idealised reactive piece |
 | reagent | real chemical | what you actually use |
 | fgi | functional group interconversion | change one group to another |

 key c-c bond-forming reactions (nobel prize 2010 — suzuki, heck, negishi):

 | reaction | what it does | catalyst | conditions |
 |----------|-------------|----------|------------|
 | suzuki coupling | ar-b(oh)₂ + ar-x → ar-ar | pd(pph₃)₄ | base, aqueous |
 | heck reaction | ar-x + alkene → ar-alkene | pd(oac)₂ | base, heat |
 | grignard | r-mgbr + c=o → r-c-oh | none (stoichiometric) | dry ether |
 | aldol condensation | enolate + aldehyde → β-hydroxy carbonyl | base/acid | mild |

 asymmetric catalysis produces single enantiomers — critical for pharmaceuticals where one mirror-image form is therapeutic and the other is toxic (thalidomide's tragedy).

 carbon allotropes — same element, vastly different properties:

 | allotrope | hybridisation | structure | key property |
 |-----------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
 | diamond | sp³ | 3d network | hardest natural material |
 | graphite | sp² | stacked sheets | conductor, lubricant |
 | fullerene (c₆₀) | sp² | buckyball cage | drug delivery research |
 | graphene | sp² | single sheet | 200× stronger than steel |
 | carbon nanotube | sp² | rolled graphene | tensile strength 100× steel |

 muga silk's fibroin is studied by polymer chemists for its beta-sheet nanocrystals — models for designing synthetic fibres with silk-like toughness. the interplay of sp³ and sp² carbon in biological polymers is where organic chemistry meets materials science. match each hybridisation to its geometry and example sp³ hybridisation tetrahedral (109.5°) — methane, ethane, diamond sp² hybridisation trigonal planar (120°) — ethene, benzene, graphene sp hyb

title: Patterns in Nature
tagline: Fibonacci spirals, tessellations, fractals, and the hidden math in flowers and honeycombs.
searchText:
patterns in nature fibonacci spirals, tessellations, fractals, and the hidden math in flowers and honeycombs. mathematics is the language of nature the fibonacci sequence the golden ratio and spirals tessellations — tiling without gaps fractals — infinite patterns at every scale symmetry in biology voronoi patterns and efficiency why patterns matter match each pattern type to its example in nature fish migration — navigating rivers and oceans homing navigation — how animals find their way home tara picks up a sunflower from the garden. fibonacci numbers why is the universe so mathematical? check yourself. the honeycomb conjecture — proved in 1999. thomas hales (1999) d'arcy thompson (1917) — on growth and form. reaction-diffusion (turing patterns, 1952). activator inhibitor tara walks through the garden, looking at flowers. the rule that builds the sequence. why does nature pick fibonacci? golden angle where fibonacci shows up beyond petals. 144 the ratio approaches φ. golden ratio the golden angle and phyllotaxis. binet's formula — closed form for fibonacci. why φ is the "most irrational" number. pisano period. zeckendorf's theorem. tara is at the chalkboard. golden spiral the golden ratio. the golden spiral grows by φ every quarter turn. a note of caution. logarithmic spirals. self-similarity. why other organisms grow in this shape. φ in continued fractions — the slowest convergent. penrose tilings and quasicrystals. daniel shechtman 2011 nobel prize in chemistry bipin holds up a small chunk of honeycomb from his uncle's apiary. it turns out only why hexagons win for honeycomb. the pattern is everywhere because the optimum is everywhere. where you have seen tessellation. semi-regular (archimedean) tessellations 8 distinct semi-regular tessellations three-dimensional tessellations — space-filling. weaire and phelan the pentagon problem. 97 years complete penrose tilings (1974). the new aperiodic monotile (2023). tara picks up a fern. a fractal is a pattern that repeats at every scale. self-similarity why are fractals everywhere in nature? the amazing thing. the fractal dimension d. examples. 1.262 1.585 0.631 britain's coastline has d ≈ 1.25. the mandelbrot set. fractal dimension exactly 2 julia set real-world fractal applications. heisenberg uncertainty meets fractals. tara holds up a butterfly with paper wings she folded last week. two main kinds of symmetry in living things. bilateral radial why bilateral for moving creatures? why radial for sit-and-wait creatures? at the molecular and atomic level, symmetry rules. symmetry as a mathematical group. chirality — the broken symmetry of life. left-handed right-handed noether's theorem (1918) — symmetry is conservation. the standard model symmetry group. su(3) × su(2) × u(1) symmetry breaking. look at the cracks in dried mud. voronoi patterns the recipe for a voronoi diagram. why nature finds voronoi patterns. a historical case. john snow constructing a voronoi diagram. the delaunay triangulation — voronoi's twin. fortune's sweep-line algorithm (1986). generalised voronoi diagrams voronoi in robotics. voronoi in cosmology. patterns turning into engineering. biomimetics why the same patterns recur — physics, not magic. solar phyllotaxis — sunflower-inspired layouts. fractal antennas — multi-band reception. eastgate centre, harare — termite-mound architecture. macrotermes michaelseni sharkskin riblets — drag reduction. hilsa smell earth's magnetic field water temperature gradients ocean currents thrust vs drag kármán vortex streets true bicoordinate magnetic maps inclination intensity magnetoreception sun compass star compass olfactory maps landmarks magnetic map hypothesis total intensity inclination angle magnetite-based radical pair mechanism patterns-in-nature patterns in nature fibonacci spirals, tessellations, fractals, and the hidden math in flowers and honeycombs. basket-weavers-song the-magic-japi-hat mathematics is the language of nature tara picks up a sunflower from the garden. "look closely," her grandmother had said, "and you will see something strange." sure enough — the seeds in the dark centre form spirals. and not just one set of spirals: two interlocking families, one curving clockwise, one anticlockwise.

 

 tara counts. twenty-one going one way. thirty-four going the other. both come from the same magical sequence — the fibonacci numbers (we'll meet them in the next section). she didn't plan to find this. the flower didn't plan to grow this way. so why is it there?

 why is the universe so mathematical? look around: snail shells, pinecones, snowflakes, honeycombs, river deltas, lightning bolts, lung branches, galaxy spirals — all follow precise mathematical patterns. some highlights:

 | pattern in nature | mathematical structure |
 |-------------------|------------------------|
 | sunflower spirals, pinecones | fibonacci numbers, golden angle |
 | honeycomb cells | hexagonal tessellation |
 | snail and nautilus shells | logarithmic (golden) spiral |
 | snowflakes | six-fold rotational symmetry |
 | fern leaves, river networks | fractals (self-similar at every scale) |
 | butterfly wings | bilateral (mirror) symmetry |
 | mud flat cracks, dragonfly wing veins | voronoi diagrams |

 the reason isn't magic — it's efficiency. nature doesn't "know" maths. but the laws of physics and chemistry penalise waste. a bee that builds with hexagons saves wax. a plant that arranges leaves at the golden angle catches more sunlight per area. a coastline carved by waves repeats the same shape at every scale because the same erosion processes act at every scale. the patterns we see are the survivors of countless processes that select for whatever solves the problem with the least material, time, or energy.

 check yourself. why is a soap bubble round, not square? (surface tension minimises area for a given volume; a sphere is the unique shape that does this. the bubble doesn't pick — physics does.) the honeycomb conjecture — proved in 1999. for thousands of years, people noticed bees built hexagons. pappus of alexandria conjectured around 300 ce that hexagons were the most efficient way to partition a plane into equal-area cells. the full proof did not arrive until thomas hales (1999).

 statement. for a region divided into equal-area cells, the regular hexagonal partition has the shortest total perimeter of any partition.

 numerical comparison for area a.

 | cell shape | perimeter for area a | excess vs hexagon |
 |-----------|---------------------|--------------------|
 | regular hexagon | 3.722 √a | 0% |
 | square | 4.000 √a | +7.4% |
 | equilateral triangle | 4.559 √a | +22.5% |
 | circle (impossible to tile, listed for reference) | 3.545 √a | -4.7% — but circles leave gaps |

 why bees converge: 7% less perimeter means 7% less wax. across thousands of cells in a hive, that's significant survival advantage. d'arcy thompson (1917) — on growth and form. a landmark book arguing biological shapes are the products of physical forces, not just heredity. bones grow in trajectories of stress. cell walls form along surfaces of constant curvature. organisms are not designed; they emerge from the same equations that govern fluid dynamics, heat flow, and surface tension.

 reaction-diffusion (turing patterns, 1952). alan turing showed that two interacting chemicals — one activator, one inhibitor — diffusing at different rates can spontaneously form spots, stripes, and spirals. the minimal equations are:

 ∂u/∂t = du ∇²u + f(u, v)
∂v/∂t = dv ∇²v + g(u, v)

 when dv > du (the inhibitor diffuses faster than the activator), uniform states become unstable; the system settles into striped or spotted patterns. real examples:

 - spots on a leopard, stripes on a zebra, lines on an angelfish — different boundary conditions, same equations.
 - the belousov-zhabotinsky reaction in a petri dish produces chemical spirals indistinguishable from those on a s

title: The Periodic Table
tagline: The map of all matter — 118 elements organized by their deepest properties.
searchText:
the periodic table the map of all matter — 118 elements organized by their deepest properties. how the table is organized reading an element box match each symbol to its element and origin metals, nonmetals, and metalloids periodic trends the alkali metals and halogens match each element to its group behaviour the grid at a glance: periods groups valence electrons s-block p-block d-block f-block how many electrons can each shell hold? why does this matter? the mendeleev story: why the filling order isn't simply 1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 3d: aufbau principle effective nuclear charge worked example — sodium vs magnesium: 2.5 3.5 superheavy elements and relativistic effects: relativistic quantum chemistry g-block what's in the box: iron 55.845 why are some symbols "weird"? worked example — decoding iron (fe): 91.7% period 4 group 8 calculating atomic mass from isotope data: worked example — iron: 55.848 amu electron configurations — the shorthand: mass spectrometry — how we measure isotope abundances: from the radius equation: applications beyond chemistry: the three families: appearance conductivity semiconductors malleability ductility electron tendency bonding type count think of it this way: the metalloids — why they matter: silicon (si) germanium (ge) boron (b) arsenic (as) carbon steel silicon dioxide (sio₂) arsenic muga silk why metals conduct electricity — the electron sea model: 63.0 59.6 41.0 37.7 10.0 semiconductors — the magic in between: covalent crystal lattice n-type p-type diode band theory — why metals, semiconductors, and insulators differ: bands alloy design — mixing metals for purpose: hume-rothery rules trend 1: atomic radius (atom size) smaller larger trend 2: electronegativity (electron-grabbing power) analogy: nonpolar covalent polar covalent ionic trend 3: ionisation energy (how hard it is to remove an electron) the two competing forces: successive ionisation energies — proof of electron shells: 9× jump! massive jump between ie₁ and ie₂ electron affinity — the mirror trend: anomalies in the trends: quantifying trends — slater's rules for z_eff: worked example — oxygen's 2p electron (z = 8): total σ 3.45 z_eff 4.55 relativistic corrections to trends: group 1 — the alkali metals shatters the container why does reactivity increase down the group? group 17 — the halogens liquid halogen reactivity decreases down the group alkali metals + halogens → ionic compounds: 1,037 604 born-landé equation flame test colours — a practical identification tool: bright yellow halogen displacement reactions — the activity series: reduction potentials +2.87 interhalogen compounds periodic-table the periodic table the map of all matter — 118 elements organized by their deepest properties. why-the-muga-silk-is-golden little-potter how the table is organized periodictableoverviewdiagram imagine a library where every book is shelved by its author's birthday and its genre — so you can find any book by date or by type. the periodic table does the same thing for elements: it arranges all 118 known elements in order of atomic number (like birthday) and groups those with similar chemical behaviour into columns (like genre).

 the grid at a glance:

 | feature | what it is | how many | key idea |
 |---------|-----------|---------|----------|
 | periods (rows) | horizontal rows, numbered 1–7 | 7 | each period fills the next electron shell |
 | groups (columns) | vertical columns, numbered 1–18 | 18 | same group = same number of valence electrons = similar chemistry |
 | s-block | groups 1–2 (left side) | 2 columns | filling s orbitals |
 | p-block | groups 13–18 (right side) | 6 columns | filling p orbitals |
 | d-block | groups 3–12 (centre) | 10 columns | transition metals — filling d orbitals |
 | f-block | lanthanides + actinides (below) | 14 columns | filling f orbitals |

 how many electrons can each shell hold?

 | shell (n) | max electrons (2n²) | period | elements in that period |
 |-----------|---------------------|--------|------------------------|
 | 1 | 2 | period 1 | h, he (2 elements) |
 | 2 | 8 | period 2 | li → ne (8 elements) |
 | 3 | 8 | period 3 | na → ar (8 elements) |
 | 4 | 18 | period 4 | k → kr (18 elements, d-block starts) |

 period 3 fills only 8 because the 3d subshell fills after 4s, not immediately after 3p.

 why does this matter? because the column an element sits in tells you how it behaves. all group 1 elements are soft, explosive metals. all group 18 elements are inert gases. you don't need to memorise 118 separate personalities — just understand the table's logic.

 the mendeleev story: in 1869, the russian chemist dmitri mendeleev arranged the 63 known elements by atomic mass and noticed a repeating pattern of properties. his genius was leaving gaps for elements not yet discovered. he predicted three missing elements — eka-boron, eka-aluminium, and eka-silicon — describing their density, melting point, and oxide formulas. when gallium was discovered in 1875 and matched his eka-aluminium predictions almost exactly, the scientific world was convinced. mendeleev didn't just organise what was known — he predicted what was unknown. why the filling order isn't simply 1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 3d:

 the periodic table's shape follows the aufbau principle — electrons fill orbitals from lowest to highest energy. but energy depends on both shell number (n) and subshell type (l). the actual filling order:

 1s → 2s → 2p → 3s → 3p → 4s → 3d → 4p → 5s → 4d → 5p → 6s → 4f → 5d → 6p → 7s → 5f → 6d → 7p

 notice: 4s fills before 3d, and 4f fills after 6s. this is why the d-block appears in period 4 (not period 3) and the f-block in period 6.

 effective nuclear charge explains periodic trends:

 zeff = z − σ

 | symbol | meaning | effect |
 |--------|---------|--------|
 | z | atomic number (proton count) | pulls electrons in |
 | σ | shielding constant (from slater's rules) | inner electrons block the pull |
 | zeff | net charge felt by outer electron | determines atom size and reactivity |

 worked example — sodium vs magnesium:

 | property | na (z = 11) | mg (z = 12) |
 |----------|------------|-------------|
 | electron config | [ne] 3s¹ | [ne] 3s² |
 | core electrons | 10 | 10 |
 | approx. σ | 10 × 0.85 = 8.5 | 10 × 0.85 = 8.5 |
 | zeff | 11 − 8.5 = 2.5 | 12 − 8.5 = 3.5 |

 magnesium's outer electron feels a stronger pull → smaller atom, higher ionisation energy. this is why trends within a period exist — same shell, but increasing nuclear charge. superheavy elements and relativistic effects:

 elements 113–118 (nihonium through oganesson) were confirmed between 2002 and 2016, created by smashing accelerated ions into heavy-element targets. oganesson (z = 118) exists for less than a millisecond before alpha decay.

 | element | z | discovery | half-life | method |
 |---------|---|-----------|-----------|--------|
 | nihonium (nh) | 113 | riken, 2003 | 20 s | ⁷⁰zn + ²⁰⁹bi → ²⁷⁸nh + n |
 | flerovium (fl) | 114 | jinr, 1999 | 2.7 s | ⁴⁸ca + ²⁴²pu → ²⁸⁹fl + n |
 | oganesson (og) | 118 | jinr, 2002 | 0.7 ms | ⁴⁸ca + ²⁴⁹cf → ²⁹⁴og + 3n |

 relativistic quantum chemistry becomes critical for heavy atoms. inner electrons (especially 1s) travel at appreciable fractions of c, causing their mass to increase and orbitals to contract. consequences:

 | effect | explanation | example |
 |--------|------------|--------|
 | s/p orbital contraction | relativistic mass increase → tighter orbit | gold's colour: 5d→6s transition shifts into visible range |
 | d/f orbital expansion | better shielding by contracted s/p → d/f expand | lanthanide contraction is 15% relativistic |
 | mercury is liquid | 6s² electrons tightly held → weak metallic bonding | only metal liquid at room temperature |
 | lead is inert | 6s² "inert pair" too stable to bond easily | pb²⁺ more stable than pb⁴⁺ |

determines range: animal dispersal — gut passage effects: metapopulation theory island biogeography ne india biogeography: dispersal corridor what part of the plant is a potato? stem layers of modified leaves berry leaves fruit flower buds roots flower seed fruit classification: drupe pome aggregate multiple capsule seed structure: embryo seed coat (testa) cotyledon(s) fruit development — hormonal control: gibberellin parthenocarpy the abc model of flower development a alone a + b b + c c alone e (sepallata) plant-biology plant biology photosynthesis chlorophyll transpiration how plants make food from sunlight, grow toward the sky, and reproduce. old-banyan-trees-stories tejimola-the-girl-who-became-a-plant orchid-colors pitcher-plant bamboo-grows-fast girl-grew-forest photosynthesis — the engine of life photosynthesisdiagram right now, as you read this, every leaf outside your window is doing something no human factory has ever managed: splitting water molecules apart using nothing but sunlight. we've spent billions trying to copy this trick. we can't. a leaf does it before breakfast.

 watch the diagram above. those blue dots climbing up? water, pulled from the soil through hair-thin tubes. the gray dots drifting in from the left? carbon dioxide, slipping through tiny pores called stomata that open and close like mouths. and those golden sparks raining down from the sun? photons — packets of pure energy.

 inside the leaf, in tiny green discs called chloroplasts, something astonishing happens. light energy rips water molecules apart. the hydrogen atoms get welded onto co₂ to build glucose — sugar, the plant's food. the leftover oxygen? tossed out as waste. your next breath is a plant's trash.

 the whole thing in one line:

 > 6co₂ + 6h₂o + light → c₆h₁₂o₆ + 6o₂

 here's a number that should bother you: leaves are only about 1% efficient at converting sunlight to sugar. terrible, right? except that 1% feeds every animal on earth, including you. your breakfast this morning — sunlight, processed through a plant, processed through a kitchen. two steps removed from a star.

 | what goes in | what comes out | where you can see it |
 |-------------|---------------|---------------------|
 | co₂ from the air | glucose — stored in roots, fruits, seeds | a mango is concentrated sunlight |
 | h₂o from roots | oxygen — released through stomata | every breath you take |
 | sunlight | chemical energy locked in sugar bonds | wood burns because it's releasing stored sunlight |

 bamboo grows up to 91 cm in a single day. it photosynthesises so fast that you can almost watch it converting sunlight into structure. a rice paddy fixes 15 tonnes of co₂ per hectare per year. a tea leaf is green because it's packed with chlorophyll — the green pigment that catches photons. the darker the leaf, the harder it's working.

 think about this: plants need co₂ and release o₂. animals need o₂ and release co₂. neither invented this arrangement. it just emerged — a four-billion-year-old partnership between breathers and growers, each keeping the other alive. photosynthesis has two stages that work like a relay race — one captures energy, the other uses it.

 stage 1: light-dependent reactions (thylakoid membranes)

 chlorophyll absorbs photons and uses that energy to split water (photolysis): 2h₂o → 4h⁺ + 4e⁻ + o₂. the electrons travel along an electron transport chain:

 | step | component | what happens |
 |------|----------|-------------|
 | 1 | photosystem ii (p680) | absorbs light, splits water, releases electrons |
 | 2 | plastoquinone | shuttles electrons to next complex |
 | 3 | cytochrome b6f | pumps h⁺ into thylakoid lumen (builds gradient) |
 | 4 | photosystem i (p700) | re-energises electrons with another photon |
 | 5 | ferredoxin → nadp⁺ reductase | produces nadph |
 | 6 | atp synthase | h⁺ gradient drives atp production (chemiosmosis) |

 stage 2: calvin cycle (stroma — light-independent)

 the enzyme rubisco fixes co₂ onto ribulose bisphosphate (rubp), producing 3-phosphoglycerate (3-pga). atp and nadph then reduce 3-pga to glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate (g3p).

 | per turn of the calvin cycle | amount |
 |-----------------------------|--------|
 | co₂ fixed | 1 molecule |
 | atp consumed | 3 |
 | nadph consumed | 2 |
 | g3p produced | 1 (but 5 of every 6 are recycled to regenerate rubp) |
 | turns needed for 1 glucose | 6 (requiring 18 atp + 12 nadph total) | rubisco is the most abundant protein on earth (500 million tonnes globally) yet remarkably slow — fixing only 3 co₂/s versus typical enzyme rates of 1,000/s. worse, it catalyses a wasteful side reaction with o₂ called photorespiration, reducing efficiency by 25-30%.

 evolutionary solutions to photorespiration:

 | pathway | mechanism | examples | efficiency gain |
 |---------|-----------|---------|----------------|
 | c₃ (default) | rubisco fixes co₂ directly | rice, wheat, tea | baseline |
 | c₄ | pep carboxylase pre-fixes co₂ in mesophyll → shuttles to bundle-sheath → high [co₂] around rubisco | maize, sugarcane, millet | +30-40% in hot climates |
 | cam | co₂ fixed at night (stomata open), calvin cycle by day (stomata closed) | cacti, pineapple, orchids | massive water savings |

 many epiphytic orchids of meghalaya and arunachal pradesh use cam photosynthesis — critical for survival in tree canopies where water is scarce between mon

title: Plate Tectonics & Earthquakes
tagline: The ground beneath your feet is moving — how continents drift and earthquakes shake.
searchText:
plate tectonics & earthquakes the ground beneath your feet is moving — how continents drift and earthquakes shake. tectonic plates plate boundaries earthquakes seismic wave analysis preliminary reference earth model (prem) isostasy rayleigh number whole-mantle layered seismic anisotropy plate-tectonics plate tectonics & earthquakes the ground beneath your feet is moving — how continents drift and earthquakes shake. mountain-echoes siang-river turtle-mountain earth's layers the earth is not a uniform ball of rock. it is structured in concentric layers, each with distinct composition, temperature, and physical behaviour. the outermost layer, the crust, is the thin shell we live on — only about 5–70 kilometres thick. oceanic crust (under the oceans) is thinner and denser, made primarily of basalt, averaging just 7 kilometres thick. continental crust is thicker but lighter, composed mostly of granite, reaching up to 70 kilometres under major mountain ranges like the himalayas.

beneath the crust lies the mantle, a 2,900-kilometre-thick layer of silicate rock that makes up about 84% of earth's volume. the upper mantle is partially molten and behaves like a very thick fluid over geological timescales — this zone, called the asthenosphere, is the key to plate tectonics because the rigid plates above it can slide over it. deeper in the mantle, immense pressure keeps the rock solid despite temperatures exceeding 3,000°c. convection currents in the mantle — driven by heat from the core — slowly circulate material upward and downward, providing the engine that moves tectonic plates.

at the centre of the earth sits the core, divided into two parts. the outer core is a 2,200-kilometre-thick layer of liquid iron and nickel, reaching temperatures of 4,500–5,500°c. its swirling motion generates earth's magnetic field, which shields us from harmful solar radiation. the inner core, despite being even hotter (up to 6,000°c — as hot as the surface of the sun), is solid because of the crushing pressure of all the material above it. this solid iron-nickel sphere is about 1,220 kilometres in radius, roughly the size of the moon. earth’s layered structure is revealed by seismic wave analysis. p-wave velocity increases from 6 km/s in the upper crust to 13.7 km/s at the base of the mantle, then drops sharply to 8 km/s in the outer core (the gutenberg discontinuity at 2,891 km depth). s-waves do not penetrate the outer core, proving it is liquid. the preliminary reference earth model (prem) provides a standard 1d velocity, density, and pressure profile: density increases from 2.7 g/cm³ (upper crust) to 5.5 g/cm³ (lower mantle) to 13 g/cm³ (inner core), with total pressure reaching 364 gpa at the centre. isostasy (airy model) explains why continental crust stands higher than oceanic crust: less-dense continental crust "floats" higher on the mantle, with deep roots compensating for mountain topography, just as a tall iceberg has a deep keel. mantle convection is modelled by solving the equations of mass, momentum, and energy conservation for a viscous fluid heated from below and within (radioactive decay of u, th, and k provides about 50% of earth’s internal heat). rayleigh number ra = ρgαδt·d³ / (κη) determines convection vigour; for the mantle, ra ≈ 10⁷, far above the critical value of 10³, ensuring vigorous convection. debate continues over whether convection is whole-mantle (plumes rising from the core-mantle boundary through the entire 2,900 km mantle) or layered (separate upper- and lower-mantle cells). seismic anisotropy — directional variation in wave speed caused by preferred mineral alignment — maps mantle flow patterns: olivine crystals in the upper mantle align with flow direction, producing measurable anisotropy detected by splitting of sks waves. for ne india, tomographic studies reveal a high-velocity anomaly beneath the indo-burman range, interpreted as the subducting indian plate slab. earthlayersdiagram tectonic plates the outermost rigid layer of the earth — the lithosphere — is not a single unbroken shell. it is cracked into about 15 major pieces and several smaller ones called tectonic plates. these plates include both the crust and the uppermost part of the mantle, and they range in thickness from about 15 kilometres (under mid-ocean ridges) to over 200 kilometres (under old continental interiors). each plate floats on the semi-fluid asthenosphere below, moving at speeds of 1–15 centimetres per year — roughly the rate your fingernails grow. this may seem insignificant, but over millions of years, it reshapes the entire surface of the planet.

the major plates include the pacific plate (the largest, mostly oceanic), the north american plate, the eurasian plate, the african plate, the antarctic plate, the south american plate, and the indo-australian plate (sometimes divided into the indian plate and the australian plate). india sits on the indian plate, which was once part of an ancient supercontinent called gondwana. about 140 million years ago, india broke away from gondwana and began a northward journey across the tethys sea, travelling roughly 15 centimetres per year — remarkably fast in geological terms.

around 50 million years ago, the indian plate collided with the eurasian plate. neither plate could subduct easily beneath the other because both carried thick, buoyant continental crust. instead, the collision crumpled, folded, and thrust the crust upward, creating the himalayan mountain range — the tallest on earth. this collision is still happening today; india continues to push northward into eurasia at about 4–5 centimetres per year, and the himalayas are still rising by roughly 5 millimetres per year. the immense forces of this ongoing collision are why northeast india experiences frequent earthquakes. tectonicplatesdiagram plate boundaries the most geologically active places on earth are where tectonic plates meet. there are three main types of plate boundary, each producing different geological features. at convergent boundaries, two plates move toward each other. when oceanic crust meets continental crust, the denser oceanic plate dives beneath the lighter continental plate in a process called subduction, creating deep ocean trenches, volcanic arcs, and earthquakes. when two continental plates converge — as with the indian and eurasian plates — neither subducts easily, so the crust buckles and folds upward, building enormous mountain ranges. the himalayas, the alps, and the zagros mountains were all formed by continent-continent convergence.

at divergent boundaries, two plates move apart. magma rises from the mantle to fill the gap, creating new crust. the most dramatic example is the mid-ocean ridge system — a 65,000-kilometre underwater mountain chain that snakes through every ocean basin. iceland sits directly on the mid-atlantic ridge, where the north american and eurasian plates are pulling apart at about 2.5 centimetres per year, and volcanic activity is constant. on continents, divergent boundaries create rift valleys — the east african rift is slowly splitting africa in two, and in millions of years it will become a new ocean basin.

at transform boundaries, two plates slide horizontally past each other. no crust is created or destroyed, but the friction between the plates causes frequent earthquakes. the san andreas fault in california is the most famous transform boundary, where the pacific plate slides northwest past the north american plate. transform faults also occur along mid-ocean ridges, offsetting the ridge segments. the earthquakes at transform boundaries can be devastating because they occur at shallow depths close to the surface, which amplifies the shaking. plateboundarydiagram earthquakes earthquakes occur when stress builds up along fault lines — fractures in the earth's crust where blocks of rock are trying to move past each other but are locked by friction. when the accumulated stress exceeds the f

title: Population & Urbanization
tagline: How populations grow, migrate, and build cities — from Guwahati\
searchText:
population & urbanization how populations grow, migrate, and build cities — from guwahati\ population growth population distribution urbanization sustainable development exponential growth doubling time logistic model total fertility rate (tfr) dependency ratio cohort-component models life tables demographic dividend agent-based models (abms) nighttime lights data population-and-urbanization population & urbanization how populations grow, migrate, and build cities — from guwahati's boom to ziro's quiet valleys. elephant-corridor guwahati-name market-day-tura population growth population growth is determined by the balance between births and deaths (and, locally, migration). the crude birth rate is the number of live births per 1,000 people per year; the crude death rate is the number of deaths per 1,000 people per year. the difference between them — the rate of natural increase — determines how fast a population grows (ignoring migration). a country with a birth rate of 20 and a death rate of 8 has a natural increase rate of 12 per 1,000, or 1.2% per year. at 1.2% growth, a population doubles in about 58 years. india's current natural increase rate is about 0.9% per year, down from over 2% in the 1970s, reflecting a dramatic decline in birth rates as education, healthcare, and urbanization have spread.

the demographic transition model (dtm) describes how populations change as countries develop, and it has four (sometimes five) stages. in stage 1 (pre-industrial), both birth rates and death rates are high, so population grows slowly — most of human history was in this stage. in stage 2 (early industrial), death rates fall sharply due to improved sanitation, medicine, and food supply, but birth rates remain high, causing rapid population growth — india was in this stage from about 1920 to 1970. in stage 3, birth rates begin to fall as education (especially of women), urbanization, access to contraception, and changing cultural norms reduce family size — india has been in this stage since the 1970s. in stage 4, both birth rates and death rates are low, and population growth is very slow or zero — japan, germany, and most of western europe are here. some demographers identify a stage 5 where birth rates fall below death rates and the population shrinks — japan and south korea are entering this territory.

northeast india presents an interesting demographic picture. the region's overall population density (about 170 people per square kilometre) is much lower than the national average (about 420 per square kilometre), largely because of the hilly, forested terrain that limits settlement. however, the brahmaputra valley — flat, fertile, and well-connected — is densely populated, comparable to the gangetic plains. birth rates in ne india vary by state: meghalaya and nagaland have relatively higher fertility rates, while sikkim and tripura have rates closer to the national average. urbanization is accelerating, with guwahati growing rapidly as the region's economic hub. the demographic trajectory of ne india is important for planning education, healthcare, infrastructure, and environmental conservation in one of the world's most biodiverse regions. population growth is modelled mathematically. exponential growth n(t) = n₀ · e^(rt) applies when resources are unlimited (r = birth rate − death rate). the doubling time td = ln(2)/r: at india’s current r ≈ 0.009, td ≈ 77 years. in reality, growth slows as resources become scarce, following the logistic model n(t) = k / (1 + ((k−n₀)/n₀) · e^(−rt)), where k is the carrying capacity. the total fertility rate (tfr) — the average number of children per woman — must be about 2.1 for replacement in developed countries (accounting for child mortality). india’s tfr dropped from 5.9 in 1960 to 2.0 in 2023, below replacement level. the dependency ratio (population under 15 + over 65, divided by working-age 15–64) measures economic pressure; ne india’s young population means its ratio will fall as youth enter the workforce, creating a "demographic dividend" window. demographers use cohort-component models that track age-sex groups (cohorts) through time, applying age-specific fertility, mortality, and migration rates. life tables tabulate the probability of surviving from one age to the next, yielding life expectancy at birth (india: 70 years in 2023) and age-specific death rates. the demographic dividend is maximised when the working-age share peaks relative to dependents; for ne india, this window opens around 2025–2045. agent-based models (abms) simulate individual households making decisions about migration, fertility, and land use, capturing emergent patterns that aggregate models miss. remote sensing and nighttime lights data from viirs satellites now map urbanisation in real time, showing guwahati’s radial expansion and the emergence of peri-urban corridors along national highway 37 toward kaziranga. demographictransitiondiagram population distribution popdistributiondiagram people do not spread evenly across the earth's surface. population distribution is influenced by physical factors (climate, terrain, water availability, soil fertility) and human factors (economic opportunities, infrastructure, political stability, historical settlement patterns). globally, most people live in lowland areas below 500 metres elevation, near coasts or major rivers, in temperate or tropical climates. the great river valleys of the world — the ganges, nile, yangtze, mississippi — have supported dense populations for thousands of years because of fertile alluvial soil, reliable water supply, and easy transportation. harsh environments — deserts, tundra, dense tropical forests, high mountains — support sparse populations because they are difficult to farm and build in.

in northeast india, the brahmaputra valley is the population heartland. the flat, fertile floodplain supports intensive rice cultivation and has been settled for millennia. the major cities — guwahati, jorhat, tezpur, dibrugarh, silchar — are all in valley locations. the surrounding hills of meghalaya, nagaland, manipur, mizoram, and arunachal pradesh are much more sparsely populated. arunachal pradesh, india's largest northeastern state by area (83,743 sq km), has a population of only about 1.4 million — a density of just 17 people per square kilometre. migration has been a significant force in ne india's demography: waves of migration from bengal, bihar, and nepal into assam's tea plantations and farmland during the colonial and post-colonial periods have shaped the region's population composition. push factors (poverty, lack of opportunity in origin areas) and pull factors (tea plantation jobs, available farmland, urban employment in guwahati) drove these migrations. today, rural-to-urban migration within the region, and out-migration of educated youth to cities like delhi, bangalore, and pune for employment, are the dominant migration patterns. urbanization popurbanizationdiagram urbanization is the process by which an increasing proportion of a country's population lives in cities and towns. globally, more than 55% of people now live in urban areas, up from just 30% in 1950, and the un projects this will reach 68% by 2050. urbanization is driven by rural-to-urban migration (people moving to cities for jobs, education, and services) and the natural growth of urban populations (more births than deaths in cities). in india, about 35% of the population is urban — lower than the global average but rising steadily. by 2050, india is expected to add over 400 million urban residents, more than any other country.

guwahati is the poster child of urbanization in ne india. its population has grown from about 300,000 in 1991 to approximately 1.1 million in 2021 (metropolitan area), making it one of the fastest-growing cities in india. this growth has brought economic opportunities — it companies, educational institutions, healthcare facilities, shopping ce

title: Probability & Combinatorics
tagline: The mathematics of chance — from coin flips to predicting monsoon rainfall.
searchText:
probability & combinatorics the mathematics of chance — from coin flips to predicting monsoon rainfall. what is probability? try it — dice probability permutations and combinations independent vs dependent events conditional probability expected value the basics: (impossible) and examples: sample space key rules: complement: addition rules: mutually exclusive: non-exclusive: example: total | |

' + ' with repetition: 10,000 the binomial theorem — combinations as algebra. c(3,1) = 3. worked example: 720x³ from counting to certainty — the pigeonhole principle. 13 people 367 people 

' + ' dependent events: example: drawing aces without replacement both (4/52)(3/51) = 1/221 multiplication rule for many events: independent: dependent (chain rule): 1 in 5,525 three or more pairwise independence is not the same as mutual independence. = "first coin is heads"
