' + '- jointly not equal. why conditional probability is the honest way to handle dependence. chain rule a longer worked example — drawing 3 aces from a deck without replacement: the question: 2/3 bayes' theorem — reversing the condition: famous example: medical testing if you test positive, what is the chance you actually have it? ~9% bayes' theorem in odds form: applying bayes' theorem iteratively — updating beliefs with new evidence: 0.09 0.907 step by step for round 2: 0.907 (91%) this is bayesian updating: why this matters beyond medicine: spam filters: weather forecasting: criminal trials: expected value what is expected value? example: fair die law of large numbers should you play this game? | | |

' + 'ev = 0 means the game is variance and standard deviation: 1.71 chebyshev inequality: the law of large numbers — seeing it work: must linearity of expectation — the most useful property: always. harder example: probability-and-combinatorics probability & combinatorics the mathematics of chance — from coin flips to predicting monsoon rainfall. fishermans-daughter-storm golden-hilsa mishing-fish what is probability? roll the dice in the diagram above. run a few hundred trials. watch the empirical frequencies settle toward the theoretical probabilities — 1/6 for each face. that convergence isn't magic; it's the simple-but-deep rule the whole subject rests on.

 the basics:

 probability measures how likely an event is, as a number between 0 (impossible) and 1 (certain).

 p(event) = favourable outcomes / total outcomes

 examples:

 | experiment | favourable | total | probability |
 |-----------|-----------|-------|-------------|
 | coin flip: heads | 1 | 2 | 1/2 = 0.5 |
 | die roll: a 4 | 1 | 6 | 1/6 = 0.167 |
 | die roll: even | 3 | 6 | 1/2 = 0.5 |

 the set of all possible outcomes is the sample space — for a coin it is {h, t}, for a die it is {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.

 key rules:

 - probabilities can be expressed as fractions, decimals, or percentages
 - complement: p(not event) = 1 − p(event). if p(rain) = 0.3, then p(no rain) = 0.7
 - all probabilities in a sample space sum to exactly 1

 this framework underpins weather forecasting, medical diagnosis, insurance pricing, and every machine learning algorithm. addition rules:

 - mutually exclusive: p(a or b) = p(a) + p(b)
 - non-exclusive: p(a or b) = p(a) + p(b) - p(a and b)

 example: drawing from a standard deck, p(heart or queen):

 | part | count | probability |
 |------|-------|-------------|
 | hearts | 13 | 13/52 |
 | queens | 4 | 4/52 |
 | queen of hearts (overlap) | 1 | -1/52 |
 | total | 16 | 16/52 = 4/13 |

 complementary counting trick: p(at least one heads in 3 flips) = 1 - p(no heads) = 1 - (1/2)³ = 7/8. often easier than computing the event directly. the addition rule from intermediate — p(a or b) with overlap — is useful. but in practice, most interesting probability questions aren't about "a or b" at all. they're about "at least one", which lives at the edge of where direct counting breaks down.

 the complement flip — the single most useful trick in probability. what's the probability of rolling at least one 6 in four rolls of a die?

 try it the direct way: p(exactly one 6) + p(exactly two 6s) + p(three 6s) + p(four 6s). four separate cases, each with its own multiplication. painful.

 now flip it. p(at least one 6) = 1 − p(zero 6s). "zero 6s" is one clean case — every roll is a non-6, and the rolls are independent, so just multiply:

 | step | work |
 |------|------|
 | p(not 6 on one roll) | 5/6 |
 | p(not 6 on four rolls) | (5/6)⁴ = 625/1296 ≈ 0.482 |
 | p(at least one 6) | 1 − 0.482 = 0.518 |

 just over half — slightly surprising on its own. but the real power of the complement shows up on a problem that looks impossibly hard.

 the birthday problem — everyone guesses wrong. in a room of 23 people, what's the probability at least two share a birthday?

 most people guess near 5%. the actual answer: just over 50%.

 flip to the complement. p(all different) is a straight product: first person can be any of 365, second must avoid 1 (so 364/365), third avoids 2 (so 363/365), and so on:

 p(all different) = (365/365) × (364/365) × (363/365) × ... × (343/365)

 | people | p(all different) | p(at least one match) |
 |--------|-----------------|----------------------|
 | 10 | 0.883 | 11.7% |
 | 23 | 0.493 | 50.7% |
 | 30 | 0.294 | 70.6% |
 | 50 | 0.030 | 97.0% |

 why does intuition fail so badly? because people mentally substitute a different question: "what's the chance someone shares my birthday?" that's the question that needs 253 people for 50-50. the actual question — "do any two people share any birthday?" — involves c(23, 2) = 253 possible pairs of people, which is why 23 turns out to be enough.

 once you've seen the birthday problem, you start noticing that most surprising probability results — quality control statistics, hash collisions, password cracking, why your phone keeps offering the same "random" song — all hinge on the same idea: when you have many chances, "something unusual happens" becomes almost certain. and the complement flip is almost always how you actually calculate it. probabilitydicediagram python-playground import random

 simulate rolling two dice 10,000 times
trials = 10000
countseven = 0
for in range(trials):
 d1 = random.randint(1, 6)
 d2 = random.randint(1, 6)
 if d1 + d2 == 7:
 countseven += 1

theory = 6/36 6 ways to make 7 out of 36
print(f"p(sum = 7) theory: {theory:.4f}")
print(f"p(sum = 7) simulated: {countseven/trials:.4f}")
print(f"trials: {trials}") try it — dice probability permutations and combinations try the counter in the diagram above. move n and r. toggle between "order matters" (permutations) and "order doesn't" (combinations). for small n and r, every arrangement or group is actually listed out — so you can see why p(5,3) = 60 and c(5,3) = 10. once the visual lands, the formulas stop being mysterious.

 building the counting formulas from a simple question.

 you have 5 friends: anu, bina, charu, deepa, eka. you need to pick 3 to form a team.

 first question: does the order matter?

 if order matters (say, picking captain, vice-captain, treasurer):

 - for captain: 5 choices
 - for vice-captain: 4 re

title: Python
tagline: The friendly language that reads like English
searchText:
python the friendly language that reads like english what is a program? how computers follow instructions variables are labeled boxes making decisions (if / else) repeating things (loops) match the programming concept to its everyday analogy how a program runs — line by line debugging — finding and fixing mistakes hello world and print() variables and types try it — variables try it — lists if / elif / else try it — conditionals try it — loops try it — functions dictionaries list comprehensions — one-line loops working with files error handling — try / except the bisect module — binary search built in the itertools module — power tools for iteration tuples & sets classes — building your own types scope — where variables live string formatting — f-strings and beyond math & numbers — beyond arithmetic data processing — filter, transform, aggregate input → processing → output machine code fetch-decode-execute cycle billions of times per second bytecode python virtual machine (pvm) just-in-time (jit) compilers it runs top to bottom, one line at a time, exactly as written `x` is now 16. but `y` is still 8. name that points to an object in memory reference semantics mutable immutable dynamic typing `snake_case` `upper_case` `pascalcase` annotate interns identity equality use `==` for values, use `is` only for ' + '`none`, `true`, `false` garbage collector one chained comparisons: `and` `or` `not` short-circuit evaluation ternary expression truthiness structural pattern matching destructure guard clauses accumulator counter search transform filter iterator protocol sequential execution order matters in every program. call stack traceback read tracebacks bottom-to-top concurrency 1. `threading` global interpreter lock (gil) 2. `multiprocessing` 3. `asyncio` the rule of thumb: logging unit testing core types: type conversion: the `math` module slicing key operations: operators: combine: chained: key patterns: key concepts: default parameters multiple return values type hints docstrings isinstance() generators 2 + dy that's all a dictionary is: a set of labeled slots. keys values key methods: collections module add a filter: generator expression: dict/set comprehensions: common errors: best practice: use cases: key functions: tuple set tuples tuple unpacking named tuples sets set operations when to use what: + create instance class instance instance variables methods inheritance when to use classes vs dicts: scope legb rule ocal (inside the current function) → nclosing (inside any outer function) → lobal (module level) → uilt-in (python's own names like `print`, `len`).

' + 'a why avoid global variables: function factories f-strings format specs multiline f-strings `.format()` when to use which: rounding family: powers & roots: the `statistics` module integer division: raw data → filter → transform → aggregate → result filtering transforming aggregating grouping chaining the friendly language that reads like english girl-who-spoke-to-elephants dragonfly-and-the-paddy-field why-the-muga-silk-is-golden boy-who-talked-to-clouds what is a program? a program is a set of instructions that tells a computer what to do, step by step. think of it like a recipe: ingredients (data) and steps (instructions), and the computer follows them in order. a simple recipe has three moves: take something in (eat the list of ingredients), do something with it (chop, mix, cook), then give back a result (the finished dish). every program, from a calculator to a self-driving car, does the same three things: input → processing → output. that's it. everything else is detail. zoom in on those three moves. consider a program to calculate elephant bmi:

 python
weight = float(input("weight in kg: ")) input
height = float(input("height in m: ")) input
bmi = weight / height2 processing
print(f"bmi: {bmi:.1f}") output


 four lines. two inputs captured into weight and height. one line of processing (the division). one line of output. every program you'll ever write is this pattern, scaled up. a web browser? input is keystrokes and mouse clicks; processing is rendering html; output is pixels on the screen. a neural network? input is training data; processing is millions of matrix multiplications; output is a prediction. the variables between steps (like weight, height, bmi) are the program's temporary memory — the cook's bowls and plates between recipe steps. beneath your recipe-like python code, the computer doesn't see words. it sees binary: sequences of 1s and 0s called machine code, loaded into memory, one instruction at a time. the cpu executes these with the fetch-decode-execute cycle: fetch the next instruction from memory, decode what it means (add? move? compare?), execute it. your quad-core laptop runs this cycle billions of times per second, per core.

 python adds two layers of translation. when you run python script.py: (1) the python interpreter compiles your source into bytecode (stored in .pyc files — portable, lower-level but not machine code yet); (2) the python virtual machine (pvm) reads that bytecode one instruction at a time and runs it. this interpretation step is why python is slower than compiled languages like c or rust — for every single operation in your code, the pvm is doing extra lookup work. but it's also why python feels so fast to develop in: you skip the multi-minute compile step and run instantly. just-in-time (jit) compilers like pypy get the best of both: watch the bytecode, notice which functions run often, compile those to machine code on the fly. 10-100× speedups on compute-heavy workloads. how computers follow instructions computers are ridiculously fast but completely literal. tell a human "go buy milk" and they figure out the details — which shop, how much, which brand. a computer can't do that. it needs every micro-step: stand up, turn left, walk 12 steps, open the door, look for the word "milk"... a program is just that list, written down. python is special because you can write these instructions in words that look almost like english — if temperature > 35: warn("hot") — and python handles translating it into the zillions of tiny steps the computer needs. you focus on what you want; python handles how. so what does "literal" actually look like when python runs your code? it runs top to bottom, one line at a time, exactly as written — no jumping ahead, no guessing what you meant. walk through this:

 python
x = 5 1
y = x + 3 2
x = y 2 3
print(x, y) 4


 line 1: x is 5. line 2: python looks up x (finds 5), adds 3, stores the result in y. now y is 8. line 3: python looks up y (finds 8), multiplies by 2, stores in x. x is now 16. but y is still 8. line 4 prints: 16 8.

 a common confusion: "but didn't i change x in line 3? doesn't that update y, which depended on x?" no. python doesn't remember where values came from. line 2 grabbed the value 5 out of x and used it once. after that, y has no connection to x. assignments always evaluate the right side completely first, then assign to the left. this is the rule — there's no magic "oh i see what you meant." if python follows your instructions literally, what is a variable to python? not a box containing a value — a name that points to an object in memory. this is called reference semantics, and it has a surprising consequence:

 python
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
b.append(4)
print(a) [1, 2, 3, 4] ← a changed too!


 why? because b = a didn't copy the list. it made b point to the same list object as a. one object; two names. modify through either name and the change is visible from both.

 this matters because python has two kinds of objects: mutable (lists, dicts, sets — you can modify them in place) and immutable (ints, strings, tuples — you can't). with immutables, the trap doesn't arise, because there's no "modify in place" to share. with mutables, you need b = a.copy() or b = a[:] to get an independent copy.

 python also uses dynamic typing — a variable

title: Ratios, Proportions & Percentages
tagline: The mathematics of comparison — from recipe scaling to population growth rates.
searchText:
ratios, proportions & percentages the mathematics of comparison — from recipe scaling to population growth rates. ratios — comparing quantities proportions — scaling and cross-multiplication percentages — converting, calculating, and applying real-world applications — the rice cake her grandmother taught her. the recipe is simple: ratio 2 : 1 three ways to write the same ratio. part-to-part vs part-to-whole. simplifying a ratio works just like simplifying a fraction. walked example — sharing 30 sweets in the ratio 2 : 3. 12 sweets 18 sweets where ratios appear that you might not notice. ratios are unit-free. check yourself. 15 boys, 9 girls three-part ratios — splitting in proportion. 150 g 225 g 375 g map scale problems. equivalent ratios. dimensional analysis — ratios checking your physics homework. allometric scaling — biology in ratios. why an ant can lift 50× its weight but an elephant cannot lift its own weight. tara's pitha recipe feeds 4. today she is cooking for 10 people. 2.5 a proportion is two ratios that are equal. cross-multiplication — a shortcut for solving any proportion. walked example — buying tea. x = ₹2,100 direct vs. inverse — which kind of proportion is this? same factor down the inverse-proportion trick. 3 hours x = ₹480 inverse proportion — worked example. 4 days combined — direct and inverse together. 18 machines recipe scaling has limits in real life. square-cube law — when proportions break. allometric scaling in biology. kleiber's law pharmacology — why dosage by body surface area. body surface area tara walks into a tea shop. the sign says "assam tea — ₹500 / 500g". a percentage is just a fraction with denominator 100. conversion shortcuts. walked example — tara's gst. ₹590 walked example — pass percentage. 80% percentage change — increase or decrease. a trap that catches everyone. not ₹210 the percentage-trap formula. of the original. a 50% up-and-down loses 0.25 = compound interest. ₹14,693.28 the rule of 72 — mental compounding. where the rule fails. why exponential thinking matters. bipin's aunt runs a tea shop, and bipin spends saturdays helping her blend. tea industry numbers — the math behind every cup. population analysis — percent change at scale. 288% increase sports statistics — ratios disguised as performance. health and dosing — when ratios save lives. 68% unit pricing — comparing value across different quantities. ₹0.90 / g improving an average — the cricket conundrum. 70 runs per innings epidemiology — ratios that decide policy. herd immunity threshold. ratios-and-proportions ratios, proportions & percentages the mathematics of comparison — from recipe scaling to population growth rates. little-chef grandmothers-pitha holi-tea-gardens ratios — comparing quantities tara is making pitha — the rice cake her grandmother taught her. the recipe is simple: 2 cups of rice flour for every 1 cup of jaggery. that "for every" is the key word.

 

 whether she's making one pitha or fifty, the rule stays the same: twice as much flour as jaggery. we say the ratio of flour to jaggery is 2 : 1 (read "two to one").

 three ways to write the same ratio.

 | notation | looks like | read aloud |
 |----------|-----------|-----------|
 | colon | 2 : 1 | "two to one" |
 | fraction | 2/1 or just 2 | "two to one" |
 | words | "2 parts to 1 part" | as is |

 all three say exactly the same thing. the colon notation is the friendliest for ratios with more than two parts (3 : 4 : 7).

 part-to-part vs part-to-whole. tara's pitha mix has 2 cups of flour, 1 cup of jaggery — 3 cups total.

 | comparison | ratio |
 |------------|-------|
 | flour to jaggery (part : part) | 2 : 1 |
 | flour to total mix (part : whole) | 2 : 3 |
 | jaggery to total mix (part : whole) | 1 : 3 |

 always check which question you're answering. "how much flour do i need out of the mix?" wants part-to-whole. "how much flour for each cup of jaggery?" wants part-to-part.

 simplifying a ratio works just like simplifying a fraction. divide every term by the same number.

 | ratio | common factor | simplified |
 |-------|---------------|------------|
 | 12 : 8 | 4 | 3 : 2 |
 | 250 : 1000 | 250 | 1 : 4 |
 | 18 : 24 : 30 | 6 | 3 : 4 : 5 |

 walked example — sharing 30 sweets in the ratio 2 : 3.

 | step | action | result |
 |------|--------|--------|
 | 1 | add the parts: 2 + 3 | 5 parts total |
 | 2 | each part = 30 ÷ 5 | 6 sweets per part |
 | 3 | first share = 2 parts | 2 × 6 = 12 sweets |
 | 4 | second share = 3 parts | 3 × 6 = 18 sweets |
 | 5 | check: 12 + 18 | = 30 ✓ |

 where ratios appear that you might not notice.

 | place | ratio in action |
 |-------|-----------------|
 | map scale 1 : 50,000 | 1 cm on the map = 50,000 cm (500 m) on the ground |
 | water (h₂o) | hydrogen to oxygen atoms = 2 : 1, always |
 | tea garden | 25 pickers per supervisor → 25 : 1 |
 | aspect ratios of screens | 16 : 9 (widescreen tv), 4 : 3 (older monitors), 21 : 9 (cinema) |
 | cricket strike rate | runs per 100 balls — a ratio in disguise |

 ratios are unit-free. a 2 : 1 ratio is the same in cups, in grams, in tonnes. that is what makes ratios so portable — they describe a relationship, not a quantity. scale everything by 10 and the ratio doesn't budge.

 check yourself. a class has 24 students. the boys-to-girls ratio is 5 : 3. how many boys, how many girls? (total parts = 5+3 = 8; each part = 24÷8 = 3 students; 15 boys, 9 girls.) three-part ratios — splitting in proportion. splitting 750 g of dry mix in the ratio 2 : 3 : 5.

 | step | calculation | result |
 |------|-------------|--------|
 | 1 | total parts: 2 + 3 + 5 | 10 |
 | 2 | each part: 750 ÷ 10 | 75 g |
 | 3 | first portion: 2 × 75 | 150 g |
 | 4 | second: 3 × 75 | 225 g |
 | 5 | third: 5 × 75 | 375 g |
 | 6 | check sum | 150 + 225 + 375 = 750 ✓ |

 map scale problems. a 1 : 50,000 map says: "1 cm on paper = 50,000 cm in real life." 50,000 cm = 500 m, so 1 cm on the map = 500 m on the ground.

 q: a trail measured 4.6 cm on the map. how long is the actual trail?

 actual = 4.6 × 50,000 cm = 230,000 cm = 2.3 km

 q: two villages 8.5 km apart. how far apart on the map?

 map distance = 8500 m / (500 m per cm) = 17 cm

 equivalent ratios. 6 : 9 simplifies to 2 : 3. multiply or divide every term by the same number to get an equivalent ratio. this is the key for proportion problems in the next section. dimensional analysis — ratios checking your physics homework. every formula in physics has units. a correct formula must have consistent units on both sides.

 kinetic energy: e = ½ m v². units: [kg] × [m/s]² = kg·m²/s² = joules ✓.

 if you mistakenly wrote e = ½ m v (forgetting the square), units become kg·m/s — that is momentum, not energy. dimensional analysis catches the error before any numbers go in.

 allometric scaling — biology in ratios. animals don't follow simple proportions. a mouse's heart beats 600 times/min; an elephant's 30 times/min. their masses differ by a factor of 250,000, but heart rate differs by only 20×. why?

 kleiber's law (1932): metabolic rate scales as m^0.75, where m is body mass. bigger animals burn proportionally less energy per kilogram than smaller ones. the 0.75 exponent isn't magic — it falls out of how heat dissipates from a fractal-like circulatory network. allometric ratios connect simple maths to deep biology.

 why an ant can lift 50× its weight but an elephant cannot lift its own weight. the square-cube law. scale an animal by factor k:

 | quantity | scales as |
 |----------|-----------|
 | length | k |
 | cross-section of a muscle | k² |
 | body mass (volume × density) | k³ |

 strength ÷ weight scales as k² / k³ = 1/k. double the size → halve the strength-to-weight ratio. an ant with k = 0.001 of human size has a k³/k² = 0.001 disadvantage in mass-per-cross-section, so its tiny legs can carry many times its tiny weight. pitharatiodiagram proportions — scaling and cross-mu

title: Rhythm, Beats & Percussion
tagline: From your heartbeat to polyrhythms — the science of rhythm and how drums make sound.
searchText:
rhythm, beats & percussion from your heartbeat to polyrhythms — the science of rhythm and how drums make sound. your body is a clock what makes a beat? polyrhythms — two rhythms at once match the polyrhythm to its cycle length the physics of drums syncopation — the art of the unexpected beat build a beat — constructing rhythm from simple rules bpm (beats per minute) try this right now: entrainment 40-60 bpm 60-80 bpm 100-120 bpm 120-140 bpm 160+ bpm check yourself: 500 ms just noticeable difference (jnd) predictive coding rhythmic auditory stimulation strong weak 4/4 time one three 3/4 time 6/8 time four grouping creates feeling predict: irregular time signatures tala tihai polyrhythm dhol pepa taal the mathematics of polyrhythm: polymetric drum language stern-brocot tree membrane two-dimensional nodal lines inharmonic how drum size affects pitch: bessel functions syahi between jazz funk reggae bihu dance music try this: longuet-higgins and lee metric microtiming step 1: choose your grid. step 2: place the foundation. step 3: add the backbeat. step 4: fill the gaps. step 5: syncopate. the euclidean rhythm insight: l-system rhythm-and-percussion rhythm, beats & percussion music-arts from your heartbeat to polyrhythms — the science of rhythm and how drums make sound. music-dimasa basket-weavers-song your body is a clock before humans invented metronomes or drum machines, we had the most reliable rhythm source imaginable: our own bodies. your heart beats roughly 60-100 times per minute at rest — a built-in tempo. in music, we measure tempo in bpm (beats per minute), and it is no coincidence that many comfortable musical tempos fall right in the range of a resting heartbeat.

try this right now: place two fingers on your wrist or the side of your neck and find your pulse. count the beats for 15 seconds, then multiply by 4. that is your personal bpm.

studies show that music with a tempo matching your resting heart rate (around 60-80 bpm) tends to feel calming, while faster tempos (120-140 bpm) make you want to move. this is not a coincidence — your brain naturally synchronizes internal rhythms with external ones, a phenomenon called entrainment. when you walk, your steps tend to match the music playing in your headphones. when a crowd claps along to a song, individual clapping patterns converge into a unified pulse.

here is a rough tempo guide:
- 40-60 bpm — very slow. lullabies, meditative chants, slow borgeet
- 60-80 bpm — resting heart rate. relaxed walking. ballads.
- 100-120 bpm — brisk walking. most pop music.
- 120-140 bpm — dance music. fast bihu rhythms. running pace.
- 160+ bpm — sprinting. punk, drum & bass, the fastest dhol patterns.

check yourself: a bihu dancer's heart rate during vigorous dancing might reach 150 bpm. if the dhol player matches this tempo, how many beats occur in 10 seconds? (answer: 150 / 60 x 10 = 25 beats.) the mathematical relationship between bpm and milliseconds: at 120 bpm, each beat lasts 60,000/120 = 500 ms. at 60 bpm: 1,000 ms per beat. at 180 bpm: 333 ms. human perception can distinguish timing differences as small as 2-5 ms in trained musicians — explaining why a drummer who is consistently 10 ms late sounds "dragging." the just noticeable difference (jnd) for tempo is about 2-3% — at 120 bpm, you can detect a change to about 117 or 123 bpm, but not 119 or 121. entrainment (neural synchronization to external rhythm) involves the motor cortex, cerebellum, and basal ganglia working together. the neuroscience of rhythm involves predictive coding — your brain generates expectations about when the next beat will occur and produces a neural response (the p300 event-related potential) when expectations are violated. functional mri studies show that rhythm activates the supplementary motor area even when you are sitting still — your brain is "virtually moving." patients with parkinson's disease (damaged basal ganglia) have difficulty maintaining internal tempo, but external rhythmic cues (metronomes, music) can dramatically improve their gait — a therapeutic approach called rhythmic auditory stimulation. beat perception appears to be uniquely developed in humans and a few species (parrots, sea lions) capable of vocal learning, suggesting a deep evolutionary link between rhythm and language. did-you-know premature babies in hospital grow faster when they hear music at a tempo matching a resting heartbeat. soldiers have marched to drums for thousands of years because rhythmic sound naturally synchronizes group movement. the world record for the fastest drumming is over 1,200 strokes per minute — 20 hits per second. what makes a beat? rhythmbeatdiagram a beat is the basic unit of musical time — the steady pulse. but not all beats are equal. in any time signature, some beats are naturally strong (accented) and others are weak.

4/4 time (also called "common time") is the most widespread: four beats per measure, with beat 1 strongest, beat 3 moderately strong, and beats 2 and 4 weak. count along: one two three four, one two three four. this is the pulse behind most rock, pop, hip-hop, and marching music. it feels stable and driving.

3/4 time has three beats per measure: one two three, one two three. only beat 1 is strong, creating a lilting, swaying feel — the waltz. many folk dances worldwide use 3/4 because the asymmetry naturally creates a rocking motion.

6/8 time has six eighth-note beats per measure, felt as two groups of three: one two three four five six. it sounds like a gentle gallop and appears in folk music from ireland to assam to west africa.

the key insight is that grouping creates feeling. the exact same tempo can feel marching (in 4/4), dancing (in 3/4), or galloping (in 6/8) depending on which beats get the accent. the notes themselves might be identical — what changes is the pattern of emphasis.

predict: if a song switches from 4/4 to 3/4 without changing the tempo, what will happen to the feel? (answer: the marching/driving feel will transform into a swaying/dancing feel, because the accent pattern shifts from every-other-beat to every-third-beat.) time signatures encode two numbers: the top number = beats per measure, the bottom = note value that gets one beat. 4/4 means 4 quarter-note beats per measure. 6/8 means 6 eighth-note beats. the distinction between 3/4 and 6/8 is feel: 3/4 has 3 main beats (waltz), while 6/8 has 2 main beats with triple subdivision (jig). irregular time signatures like 5/4 (dave brubeck's "take five"), 7/8 (common in balkan folk music), and 11/8 appear in progressive rock and film scores. bihu rhythms occasionally use asymmetric groupings that do not fit neatly into western time signatures. in indian classical music, the tala system organizes rhythm into cycles (avartans) of varying lengths. tintal has 16 beats (4+4+4+4), rupak has 7 beats (3+2+2), and jhaptaal has 10 beats (2+3+2+3). the first beat (sam) is the most important — the point where melodic and rhythmic cycles converge. tihai is a rhythmic cadence where a phrase is played 3 times, calculated to land exactly on sam. if 14 beats remain and you play a 4-beat phrase 3 times with 1-beat gaps: 3×4 + 2×1 = 14 ✓. this mathematical precision within improvisational freedom is what makes indian rhythm systems among the most sophisticated in the world. beat-machine polyrhythms — two rhythms at once a polyrhythm is what happens when two or more independent rhythmic patterns happen simultaneously. the simplest example: tap a steady 2 beats with your left hand while tapping a steady 3 beats with your right hand, over the same time span. the two patterns create a texture that is more complex than either alone.

polyrhythm is fundamental to west african drumming, where an ensemble might have one drummer playing a pattern of 12, another playing 8, and another playing 6, all interlocking. it is equally central to ne indian percussion traditions. during bihu, the dhol (double-headed drum) and pepa (bu

title: Rivers & Landforms
tagline: How rivers carve valleys, build deltas, and create the world\
searchText: rivers & landforms how rivers carve valleys, build deltas, and create the world\ how rivers begin erosion — how rivers shape the land match each river term to its meaning transportation and deposition braided rivers and river islands flood plains — fertile but dangerous deltas — where rivers meet the sea oxbow lakes and meanders manning equation hjulström’s curve long profile stream power equation graded profile knickpoint retreat insar rivers-and-landforms rivers & landforms how rivers carve valleys, build deltas, and create the world\'s largest river island. how-majuli-island-was-born river-braid siang-river turtle-mountain ferrymans-riddle how rivers begin riversourcediagram every river starts as a tiny trickle. rain falls on high ground, collects in small channels, and gravity pulls it downhill. these small streams merge into larger ones, which merge into rivers. the area of land that drains into a single river is called its catchment or watershed. the brahmaputra\'s watershed is enormous — about 580,000 square kilometres spanning tibet, india, and bangladesh. the river begins as the yarlung tsangpo high on the tibetan plateau, fed by snowmelt and glaciers. it crashes through the deepest gorge on earth (the yarlung tsangpo grand canyon, over 5,000 metres deep) before entering assam as the siang, then broadening into the mighty brahmaputra — one of the largest rivers in the world by water volume. river discharge q (m³/s) = cross-sectional area a × average velocity v. the manning equation v = (1/n) × r²″³ × s¹′² relates velocity to hydraulic radius r (area/wetted perimeter), channel slope s, and roughness coefficient n (smooth concrete ≈ 0.012; rocky mountain stream ≈ 0.05). the brahmaputra’s average discharge at guwahati is about 19,800 m³/s, carrying roughly 735 million tonnes of sediment per year. hjulström’s curve shows that fine clay requires higher velocity to erode than sand (clay particles are cohesive) but lower velocity to transport once entrained. a river’s long profile — a plot of elevation against distance from source — is typically concave, steepest near the source and nearly flat near the mouth, reflecting the balance between erosion and deposition along its length. fluvial geomorphology uses the stream power equation ω = ρgqs (watts per metre of channel) to predict erosion rates, where ρ is water density, g is gravitational acceleration, q is discharge, and s is slope. alluvial rivers self-organise toward a graded profile where sediment transport capacity matches sediment supply. knickpoint retreat — the upstream migration of waterfalls — propagates base-level changes through river networks at rates modelled by the stream-power incision model dz/dt = −k·a^m·s^n, where a is upstream drainage area and k, m, n are empirical constants. satellite-based insar (interferometric synthetic aperture radar) now measures riverbank erosion to millimetre precision. for the brahmaputra, morphodynamic models coupling hydrodynamics (saint-venant equations) with sediment transport (meyer-peter and müller equation) help predict channel migration and island formation — critical for planning flood defences in assam. erosion — how rivers shape the land rivers are powerful sculptors. they shape the landscape through four types of erosion. hydraulic action: the sheer force of moving water loosens and removes material from the riverbed and banks. abrasion: rocks and sediment carried by the river act like sandpaper, grinding away the channel. attrition: rocks tumbling in the current collide with each other and break into smaller, rounder pieces. solution: slightly acidic river water dissolves certain minerals from the rock. in its upper course, where the gradient is steep, a river cuts downward to create v-shaped valleys, waterfalls, and gorges. the siang river in arunachal pradesh demonstrates this dramatically — it churns through narrow gorges with such force that the roar can be heard from kilometres away. rivererosiondiagram match each river term to its meaning a curve or bend in a river\'s course oxbow lake a crescent-shaped lake formed when a meander is cut off flood plain flat land beside a river that is covered with silt during floods a fan-shaped deposit of sediment where a river meets the sea braided river a river split into multiple channels by islands and sandbars the entire area of land that drains into a single river transportation and deposition rivertransportdiagram rivers carry material downstream in several ways. large boulders are rolled or bounced along the bed (traction and saltation). sand and silt are carried suspended in the water (suspension). dissolved minerals are carried invisibly (solution). the faster the river flows, the larger the particles it can carry. when a river slows down — at a bend, where it enters a lake, or where it meets the sea — it drops its load, largest particles first. this deposited material is called sediment or alluvium. the brahmaputra carries an extraordinary sediment load — about 735 million tonnes per year, making it one of the most sediment-laden rivers on earth. this sediment builds and reshapes islands, sandbars, and flood plains constantly. braided rivers and river islands riverbraideddiagram when a river carries a very heavy sediment load across a wide, flat valley, it often splits into multiple channels separated by islands and sandbars. this is a braided river, and the brahmaputra in assam is one of the best examples on earth — at some points, it is over 10 kilometres wide during the monsoon. these braided channels constantly shift. sand and silt are deposited to form new islands, while existing islands are eroded away. majuli, situated in the brahmaputra, is often called the world\'s largest river island. it was once over 1,200 square kilometres but has shrunk to about 350 square kilometres due to erosion. the island\'s communities live with the constant reality that their land is being reshaped by the river — some villages have been relocated multiple times. flood plains — fertile but dangerous riverfloodplaindiagram when a river floods, it spills over its banks and deposits a layer of fine, nutrient-rich silt across the surrounding flat land — the flood plain. this silt makes flood plains some of the most fertile agricultural land in the world. the brahmaputra valley in assam owes its rich tea gardens and rice paddies to millennia of flood-deposited nutrients. but the same flooding that creates this fertility also causes devastation. assam experiences severe floods almost every monsoon season, displacing millions of people, destroying crops, and washing away roads and bridges. flood management is one of the most important and difficult challenges facing the region — you cannot simply stop a river that carries the meltwater of the himalayas. deltas — where rivers meet the sea riverdeltadiagram when a river reaches the sea or a large lake, it slows dramatically and dumps its remaining sediment. over thousands of years, this builds a fan-shaped landform called a delta. the ganges-brahmaputra delta in bangladesh is the largest delta in the world — about 100,000 square kilometres of incredibly flat, fertile land barely above sea level. deltas are formed of layers of silt, sand, and clay deposited in a pattern: the heaviest material settles first (near the river mouth) and the finest material is carried furthest out. deltas are biologically rich — the sundarbans mangrove forest in the ganges-brahmaputra delta is home to bengal tigers, saltwater crocodiles, and hundreds of bird species. but deltas are also extremely vulnerable to rising sea levels. oxbow lakes and meanders riveroxbowdiagram in its middle and lower course, a river rarely flows in a straight line. instead, it develops curves called meanders. the water flows faster on the outside of each curve, eroding the bank, while it flows slower on the inside, depositing sediment. over time, this makes the curves more and more pronounced. eventuall

title: Rocks, Minerals & Soil
tagline: The building blocks of Earth — from granite mountains to the red soil that grows Assam\
searchText:
rocks, minerals & soil the building blocks of earth — from granite mountains to the red soil that grows assam\ three types of rocks the rock cycle texture composition felsic mafic ultramafic bowen’s reaction series metamorphic grade thermodynamics phase diagrams metamorphic facies garnet zone geochronology geochemistry rocks-minerals-soil rocks, minerals & soil the building blocks of earth — from granite mountains to the red soil that grows assam's tea. little-potter lost-temple siang-river three types of rocks every rock on earth falls into one of three categories based on how it was formed. igneous rocks form when molten rock (magma underground, lava on the surface) cools and solidifies. if it cools slowly deep underground, large crystals have time to grow, producing coarse-grained rocks like granite — the bedrock beneath much of the meghalaya plateau and the foundation of the ancient gneissic complex in parts of ne india. if magma erupts at the surface and cools quickly, small crystals form, producing fine-grained rocks like basalt — the rock that makes up most of the ocean floor and forms the massive deccan traps of western india. some volcanic rocks cool so rapidly that no crystals form at all, producing volcanic glass like obsidian.

sedimentary rocks form from the accumulation and compaction of sediment — fragments of other rocks, mineral grains, or biological material deposited in layers by water, wind, or ice. over millions of years, these layers are compressed and cemented together (a process called lithification). common sedimentary rocks include sandstone (from sand grains), limestone (from calcium carbonate, often from marine shells and coral), shale (from fine mud and clay), and coal (from compressed ancient plant material). the brahmaputra valley is underlain by thick sequences of sedimentary rocks deposited over millions of years, containing some of india's richest petroleum reserves. sedimentary rocks are the only type that can contain fossils, making them crucial for understanding earth's biological history.

metamorphic rocks form when existing rocks (igneous, sedimentary, or even other metamorphic rocks) are subjected to intense heat, pressure, or chemically active fluids — typically deep in the earth's crust or at tectonic plate boundaries — without actually melting. the original rock's minerals recrystallise into new, more stable forms. limestone transforms into marble; shale becomes slate and then, under greater pressure, schist and eventually gneiss; sandstone becomes quartzite. the eastern himalayas and the hills of ne india contain abundant metamorphic rocks — gneisses and schists that reveal the immense forces that have shaped the region over hundreds of millions of years. metamorphic rocks tend to be very hard and resistant to weathering, which is why they often form the high ridges and peaks of mountain ranges. rock classification uses texture and composition. igneous rocks are classified by silica content: felsic (>65% sio₂; granite, rhyolite) are light-coloured and less dense; mafic (45–52% sio₂; basalt, gabbro) are dark and dense; ultramafic (<45% sio₂; peridotite) dominate the mantle. bowen’s reaction series predicts the crystallisation order from a cooling magma: olivine and calcium plagioclase crystallise first (high temperature), followed by pyroxene, amphibole, biotite, and finally quartz and potassium feldspar (low temperature). sedimentary rocks are classified by grain size: conglomerate (>2 mm), sandstone (0.06–2 mm), siltstone (0.004–0.06 mm), and shale (<0.004 mm). metamorphic grade increases with temperature and pressure: shale → slate → phyllite → schist → gneiss, each grade showing larger crystal size and more pronounced foliation. the rock cycle is governed by thermodynamics. minerals are stable over specific pressure–temperature (p–t) ranges shown on phase diagrams. metamorphic facies (greenschist, amphibolite, granulite, eclogite) correspond to p–t fields where characteristic mineral assemblages are stable. the garnet zone in metapelites (metamorphosed mudstones) indicates temperatures above 500°c. geochronology dates rocks absolutely: u–pb dating of zircon crystals (half-life of ²³⁸u = 4.47 billion years) gives ages precise to ±0.1%, revealing that the shillong plateau’s gneissic basement is 1.6 billion years old (mesoproterozoic). geochemistry uses trace elements and isotope ratios (e.g., ⁸⁷sr/⁸⁶sr, ¹⁴³nd/¹⁴⁴nd) to fingerprint magma sources and track tectonic processes. ne india’s ophiolite belt (naga hills) contains fragments of ancient ocean crust thrust onto land during the india–eurasia collision — studied to reconstruct the closure of the tethys sea. rockcyclediagram the rock cycle the three rock types are not fixed — they are stages in a continuous recycling process called the rock cycle. igneous rocks at the surface are broken down by weathering (physical breakdown by temperature changes, ice, and plant roots; chemical breakdown by water, acids, and oxygen) and erosion (transport by water, wind, and ice). the fragments are deposited as sediment, which is compacted and cemented into sedimentary rock. if sedimentary rock is buried deep enough, heat and pressure transform it into metamorphic rock. if metamorphic rock is heated further — past its melting point — it becomes magma, which can cool into new igneous rock, completing the cycle. but the cycle is not a one-way loop: any rock type can transform into any other, and rocks can cycle through the same stage multiple times.

the rock cycle operates over millions to billions of years, driven by the same forces that drive plate tectonics — internal heat from the earth's core and mantle, and external energy from the sun (which powers the weather and erosion). in ne india, all stages of the rock cycle are visible in a relatively small area. the barren island volcano in the andaman sea is creating new igneous rock right now. the brahmaputra carries millions of tonnes of sediment that will eventually become sedimentary rock. the high-grade metamorphic rocks of the shillong plateau have been transformed by the enormous pressures of the india-eurasia collision. and deep beneath the himalayas, rocks are being pushed to depths where they melt, generating the magma that fuels volcanic activity further along the plate boundary. a mineral is a naturally occurring, inorganic solid with a definite chemical composition and a crystalline structure (its atoms are arranged in a regular, repeating pattern). there are over 5,000 known minerals, but only about 30 are common in everyday rocks. minerals are identified by their physical properties: hardness (resistance to scratching, measured on the mohs scale from 1 for talc to 10 for diamond), lustre (how the surface reflects light — metallic, glassy, pearly, earthy), colour, streak (the colour of the powder when scraped on a porcelain plate), cleavage (tendency to break along flat planes), and crystal shape. quartz (hardness 7, glassy lustre, various colours) and feldspar (hardness 6, often pinkish or white) together make up over 60% of the earth's crust.

northeast india is rich in mineral resources. assam and arunachal pradesh have significant petroleum and natural gas reserves — digboi in upper assam was the site of asia's first commercial oil well, drilled in 1889, and the region continues to produce oil today. meghalaya contains substantial deposits of coal (primarily sub-bituminous and tertiary coal), limestone (used in cement manufacturing — the large chhatak and lumshnong cement plants depend on it), sillimanite, and uranium. the mikir hills of assam have deposits of granite used as building stone. however, mining in ne india is controversial: coal mining in meghalaya's jaintia hills has caused severe environmental damage, including acidification of rivers and destruction of forests, leading to the national green tribunal banning rat-hole mining in 2014. soil is far more than just dirt. it i

at every scale — from quantum to cosmic: spin qubits in quantum computing: bloch sphere nmr and mri — medical imaging from nuclear spin: ω_l = γb millisecond pulsars — nature's most precise clocks: rotational-mechanics rotational mechanics engineering angular momentum moment of inertia centripetal angular velocity why wheels changed everything, figure skaters spin faster with arms in, and gears trade speed for force. wheel-of-dharma potters-first-bowl kite-festival why wheels changed everything the problem the wheel solved:

 before the wheel, moving heavy objects meant dragging them across the ground. dragging means fighting sliding friction — every bump and groove on the two surfaces interlocks, resisting motion. the wheel replaced sliding with rolling friction, which is dramatically smaller.

 when a wheel rolls, only a tiny patch touches the ground at any instant, and that patch does not slide — it just presses down and lifts off. the result: a wheeled cart requires roughly 20 to 50 times less force to move than dragging the same load.

 | type of friction | coefficient (typical) | force to move 500 kg |
 |-----------------|----------------------|---------------------|
 | sliding (wood on ground) | 0.3 – 0.5 | 1,470 – 2,450 n |
 | rolling (wooden wheel on road) | 0.01 – 0.05 | 49 – 245 n |
 | rolling (rubber tyre on tarmac) | 0.005 – 0.01 | 25 – 49 n |
 | rolling (steel wheel on steel rail) | 0.001 – 0.002 | 5 – 10 n |

 a child can pull a 500 kg cart on smooth ground — but could never drag it without wheels.

 analogy: imagine dragging a heavy suitcase across an airport floor versus pulling one with wheels. that relief you feel when you tilt the suitcase onto its wheels is the entire history of civilisation's relationship with the wheel.

 why it matters beyond transport:

 | invention | what it rotates | what it enabled |
 |-----------|----------------|----------------|
 | potter's wheel (3500 bc) | clay on a disc | mass production of pottery |
 | waterwheel (300 bc) | paddles in a river | grinding grain without manual labour |
 | spinning wheel (1000 ad) | spindle for thread | faster textile production |
 | turbine (1884) | bladed rotor | electricity generation |
 | hard drive (1956) | magnetic platter | digital data storage |

 nearly every machine that converts energy into motion uses rotation somewhere in its design. in assam, traditional jaapi weavers spin bamboo strips on a rotating frame, and the dheki (foot-operated rice husker) uses a pivoting beam — both ancient rotational tools still in daily use. quantifying the advantage — rolling vs sliding friction:

 the coefficient of rolling friction (μr) is typically 0.001–0.01, compared to sliding friction μs ≈ 0.2–0.8.

 | surface combination | μsliding | μrolling | reduction factor |
 |--------------------|----------|----------|------------------|
 | wood on wood | 0.40 | 0.05 | 8× |
 | rubber on concrete | 0.70 | 0.01 | 70× |
 | steel on steel | 0.60 | 0.002 | 300× |
 | with ball bearings | — | 0.001 | 600× |

 worked example — cart on different surfaces:

 for a 1000 kg cart, the force needed to pull it:

 - sliding: f = μs × mg = 0.5 × 1000 × 9.81 = 4,905 n
 - rolling on

title: The Scientific Method
tagline: How scientists ask questions, test ideas, and build knowledge — the engine of all discovery.

hypothesis educated prediction tested and potentially proven wrong "if [condition], then [predicted outcome], because [reasoning]." then because null hypothesis (h₀) reject confirmation bias multiple hypotheses are normal. multiple quick check: hypothesis testing — the statistical framework: two types of errors: type i type ii worked example — why sample size matters: sample size calculations must be done before the experiment three philosophies of hypothesis testing: popperian falsificationism frequentist testing bayesian inference worked example — bayesian update: 0.93 the replication crisis: controlled test one thing everything else the same the four essential components: independent variable (iv) dependent variable (dv) controlled variables control group why replication matters: three safeguards against bias: randomisation blinding replication ne india experiment idea: power analysis — calculating sample size before you start: worked example — tea seedling experiment: 8.82 → 9 plants per group 36 total plants anova — comparing multiple groups at once: 26% post-hoc tests advanced experimental designs — beyond one-factor-at-a-time: factorial interactions randomised complete block (rcbd) latin square split-plot repeated measures adaptive factorial interactions — worked example: 5.7 cm 1.6 cm interaction: the effect of water depends on ph mill's method of difference step 1: descriptive statistics — summarise the pile. mean 12.0 cm median 12 cm standard deviation (sd) 1.58 cm range 4 cm step 2: visualise — make the patterns visible. bar chart line graph scatter plot histogram step 3: draw conclusions — but carefully. supports or contradicts size limitations next steps example conclusion (tea experiment): the t-test — is the difference real? worked example — ph 5 vs ph 7: 10.47 p << 0.001 confidence intervals — expressing uncertainty: (11.77, 12.83) cm key statistics summary: t-test anova correlation (r) chi-squared (χ²) regression — modelling relationships mathematically: ols assumptions and diagnostics: when ols fails — alternatives: the reproducibility movement: pre-registration effect sizes open data + open code meta-analysis peer review how peer review works: what reviewers look for: why does this matter? conflicts of interest? effect size? correlation ≠ causation? worked example — evaluating a claim: interesting but far from proven the hierarchy of scientific evidence: systematic review + meta-analysis randomised controlled trial (rct) cohort study case-control study case report / expert opinion paradigm shifts and scientific revolutions: normal science revolutions demarcation problem — what is science? cluster of features entire scientific method scenario: 1. observation 2. question 3. hypothesis 4. null hypothesis 5. experiment design 6. data collection 7. analysis 8. conclusion 9. communication key takeaway: cycle the method also applies to everyday life: putting it all together — the deepor beel study in statistical detail: the scientific paper structure (imrad): nd | — | — |
' + '| common logical errors in scientific reasoning: affirming the consequent post hoc ergo propter hoc cherry-picking appeal to authority the scientific method in historical perspective: how the method adapts to different sciences: the deepor beel study — what a reviewer would ask: scientific-method the scientific method how scientists ask questions, test ideas, and build knowledge — the engine of all discovery. boy-counted-butterflies girl-who-spoke-to-elephants witch-doctor observation and questioning science starts the same way every time: someone notices something and asks why.

 think of a detective arriving at a scene. before forming theories, they look. they measure. they record. that's observation — the foundation of all science.

 observations come in two flavours:

 | type | what you record | example from ne india |
 |------|----------------|----------------------|
 | qualitative | qualities — colour, shape, behaviour | "the one-horned rhinos in kaziranga prefer the western grasslands" |
 | quantitative | numbers — measurements, counts | "western grasslands have 3.2× more mineral-rich grass per hectare" |

 quantitative observations are more powerful because they can be compared, graphed, and tested statistically. but qualitative observations often come first — they tell you where to look.

 from observation to question:

 | observation | vague question ❌ | focused, testable question ✅ |
 |-------------|-------------------|-------------------------------|
 | tea on upper slopes looks yellower | "why do plants grow differently?" | "does soil ph affect camellia sinensis growth rate in jorhat?" |
 | cherrapunji gets extreme rain some months | "why does it rain?" | "what is the relationship between wind direction and daily rainfall in the khasi hills?" |
 | majuli island is shrinking each year | "why do rivers erode?" | "how many hectares does majuli lose per monsoon cycle, and does upstream dam release timing affect it?" |

 notice the pattern: a good question names specific variables, can be answered by measurement, and could be proven wrong (falsifiable). if no result could ever disprove it, it's not a scientific question — it's an opinion.

 analogy: observation is like tuning a radio. you scan through static (the world) until you pick up a signal (a pattern). the question is you asking: "what station is this, and why is it playing this song?" precision vs accuracy — the two pillars of measurement:

 imagine shooting arrows at a target.

 | scenario | precision | accuracy | what it means |
 |----------|-----------|----------|---------------|
 | arrows clustered near bullseye | ✅ high | ✅ high | instrument is calibrated and reliable |
 | arrows clustered but off-centre | ✅ high | ❌ low | systematic error (bias) — needs recalibration |
 | arrows scattered around bullseye | ❌ low | ✅ high (on average) | random error — need more readings |
 | arrows scattered and off-centre | ❌ low | ❌ low | instrument is unreliable and biased |

 quantifying precision:

 | concept | symbol | what it tells you | example |
 |---------|--------|-------------------|---------|
 | standard deviation | σ | spread of repeated measurements | σ = 0.3 cm means most readings fall within ±0.3 cm of the mean |
 | significant figures | — | how many digits are meaningful | 42.3 cm = 3 sig figs → uncertainty ±0.05 cm |
 | resolution | — | smallest detectable change | a ruler marked in mm has 1 mm resolution |

 worked example — significant figures in calculation:

 a tea seedling bed measures 42.3 cm × 3.1 cm.
 - raw calculation: 42.3 × 3.1 = 131.13 cm²
 - 42.3 has 3 sig figs; 3.1 has 2 sig figs
 - rule: keep the fewest sig figs → 130 cm² (2 sig figs)
