mutualism to what each partner gives and gets fig tree + fig wasp tree gets pollination; wasp gets a place to lay eggs and food for larvae plant roots + mycorrhizal fungi plant gets water and minerals; fungus gets sugars from photosynthesis rhododendron + sunbird flower gets pollinated; bird gets nectar as food legume + rhizobium bacteria plant gets nitrogen fixed from air; bacteria get sugars and shelter in root nodules think of your best friendship.

 you help your friend with maths homework; they share their lunch when you forget yours. both of you are better off because of the relationship. nature is full of partnerships exactly like this — biologists call them mutualism.

 what is mutualism?

 mutualism is an interaction where both species gain a fitness advantage — they survive or reproduce better together than apart. some mutualisms are so tightly woven that neither partner can survive alone. these are called obligate mutualisms.

 the fig and its wasp — a marriage millions of years old

 each of the roughly 750 species of fig tree is pollinated by its own specific species of fig wasp. the wasp enters the fig through a tiny opening called the ostiole, pollinates the internal flowers, lays her eggs, and dies inside. her larvae develop within the fig, and emerging adults carry pollen to the next fig. neither partner can reproduce without the other.

 fig trees are keystone species in tropical forests — they produce fruit year-round when other trees are bare, sustaining hornbills, gibbons, macaques, and fruit bats through lean months.

 | mutualism | what partner a gives | what partner b gives | type |
 |---|---|---|---|
 | fig tree + fig wasp | shelter, egg-laying site | pollination | obligate |
 | mycorrhizal fungi + plant roots | water, phosphorus (2-10x more) | sugars (up to 20% of photosynthate) | facultative to obligate |
 | rhododendron + sunbird | nectar | pollen transfer between flowers | facultative |
 | legume + rhizobium bacteria | sugars, root-nodule shelter | atmospheric nitrogen fixed into usable form | facultative |
 | acacia + ant | hollow thorns for nesting, nectar | defence — ants attack herbivores | obligate |

 the underground internet: mycorrhizal networks

 over 90% of land plants have fungi growing on or inside their roots — a partnership called mycorrhizae (literally "fungus-roots"). the fungal hyphae are incredibly thin threads that extend far beyond the root system, dramatically increasing the plant's access to water and minerals, especially phosphorus.

 in return, the plant feeds the fungus with sugars from photosynthesis. but here is the remarkable part: these fungal threads connect tree to tree, creating underground networks through which carbon, water, and even chemical defence signals can flow. scientists sometimes call this the "wood wide web." research has shown that large mother trees channel resources to their own seedlings through these networks — the forest is a cooperative community, not just a collection

title: Tides & Oceanography
tagline: Why the ocean breathes with the Moon, waves carry energy but not water, and coral reefs are underwater cities.
searchText:
tides & oceanography why the ocean breathes with the moon, waves carry energy but not water, and coral reefs are underwater cities. why the ocean breathes — the moon pulling water spring tides and neap tides match each tidal condition to its cause ocean currents — rivers inside the ocean waves — energy traveling, not water storm surge — when wind pushes the ocean onto land the deep ocean — darker than space, crushing pressure coral reefs — underwater cities why the ocean matters match each ocean function to its impact difference tidal harmonics laplace tidal equations amphidromic points tidal energy tides-and-oceanography tides & oceanography marine-science why the ocean breathes with the moon, waves carry energy but not water, and coral reefs are underwater cities. fishermans-storm-shelter river-dolphins-secret parting-red-sea why the ocean breathes — the moon pulling water tidemoondiagram twice a day, the ocean rises and falls along coastlines around the world. this rhythmic breathing is caused by the moon's gravity. the moon pulls on everything on earth, but it pulls on the water closest to it slightly more than on the solid earth beneath, because gravitational force decreases with distance. this extra pull stretches the ocean into a bulge on the side facing the moon — a high tide.

but there is a second bulge on the opposite side of earth too, which surprises many people. on the far side, the solid earth is pulled toward the moon more than the distant water, effectively leaving the water behind — creating another bulge. so earth actually has two tidal bulges at any time: one facing the moon and one facing away. as the earth rotates on its axis every 24 hours, any given coastline passes through both bulges, producing two high tides and two low tides each day.

the difference between high tide and low tide (the tidal range) varies enormously. in the mediterranean sea, it can be less than 30 centimetres. in canada's bay of fundy, the tidal range can exceed 16 metres — enough to cover a four-storey building. the bay's funnel shape amplifies the tidal wave as it is squeezed into a narrowing channel. the tidal force is the difference in gravitational pull across earth’s diameter. the moon’s gravitational acceleration at earth’s centre is gmoon = gmmoon/d², where d ≈ 384,400 km. the tidal acceleration δg ≈ 2gmmoon · rearth / d³, yielding about 1.1 × 10⁻⁶ m/s² — tiny compared to earth’s gravity (9.8 m/s²) but enough to raise ocean bulges of about 0.5 m in open ocean. the sun’s tidal acceleration is about 46% of the moon’s (its greater mass is offset by far greater distance). tidal harmonics decompose the complex observed tide into sinusoidal components (m₂ = principal lunar semidiurnal at 12.42 hours, s₂ = principal solar, k₁ = lunisolar diurnal), with amplitudes and phases determined locally by basin geometry. coastal amplification via resonance can multiply the open-ocean tide by 10× or more, as in the bay of fundy. the laplace tidal equations model tides as shallow-water waves in ocean basins, incorporating earth’s rotation (coriolis effect), basin geometry, and seafloor friction. tides rotate around amphidromic points — nodal points of zero tidal amplitude where cotidal lines (lines of simultaneous high tide) radiate outward like spokes. the north sea has three amphidromic points; the bay of bengal has one near sri lanka. modern tidal prediction uses satellite altimetry (topex/poseidon, jason series) measuring sea-surface height to centimetre precision, feeding global models like fes2014 with over 30 harmonic constituents. tidal energy harvesting uses turbines in high-current channels (e.g., the 240 mw sihwa lake plant in south korea) or oscillating water columns. the sundarbans tidal channels experience 2–5 m tidal ranges — potentially viable for small-scale tidal stream generators to power remote communities. spring tides and neap tides tidespringneapdiagram the sun also pulls on earth's oceans, but because it is so much further away (despite being far more massive), its tidal effect is only about 46% of the moon's. when the sun and moon align — during a new moon or a full moon — their gravitational pulls add together, producing extra-large tides called spring tides (the name has nothing to do with the season — it comes from the old english word "springan," meaning to surge). during spring tides, high tides are higher and low tides are lower than average.

when the sun and moon are at right angles to each other (during quarter moons), their pulls partially cancel out, producing smaller tides called neap tides. during neap tides, high tides are lower and low tides are higher than average — the tidal range is at its minimum.

fishermen and sailors have tracked this cycle for thousands of years. in coastal assam and the sundarbans, fishermen plan their trips around the tidal calendar — strong spring tides mean stronger currents that can be dangerous for small boats, but also bring nutrient-rich water that attracts fish. understanding tides is not abstract science — it is a matter of livelihood and safety. match each tidal condition to its cause spring tide (largest tidal range) sun and moon aligned — gravitational pulls add together (new or full moon) neap tide (smallest tidal range) sun and moon at right angles — gravitational pulls partially cancel (quarter moons) two tides per day earth rotates through two tidal bulges — one facing moon, one on opposite side bay of fundy's extreme 16 m range funnel-shaped bay amplifies the tidal wave as it squeezes into a narrow channel ocean currents — rivers inside the ocean oceancurrentdiagram the ocean is not a still body of water — it is crisscrossed by enormous currents that flow like rivers, some warm, some cold, carrying heat and nutrients around the planet. the gulf stream, for example, is a warm current that flows from the gulf of mexico northeast across the atlantic. it carries so much heat that it keeps western europe significantly warmer than it would otherwise be — london, at the same latitude as calgary in canada, rarely sees temperatures below -5°c.

these currents are driven by two forces: wind (which drives surface currents) and differences in water density (which drives deep currents). cold water is denser than warm water, and salty water is denser than fresh water. near the poles, ocean water cools and becomes saltier as ice forms (ice excludes salt), making it very dense. this dense water sinks to the ocean floor and flows toward the equator as a deep, cold current. meanwhile, warm surface water flows from the equator toward the poles to replace it. this global circulation — called the thermohaline circulation (thermo = heat, haline = salt) — acts like a giant conveyor belt, moving heat from the tropics to the poles and taking about 1,000 years to complete one full cycle. it is one of the most important regulators of earth's climate. waves — energy traveling, not water oceanwaveanatomydiagram watch a seagull floating on ocean waves. it bobs up and down, rises with each passing wave crest and drops into each trough — but it barely moves horizontally. this reveals a counterintuitive truth: ocean waves transport energy, not water. each water molecule moves in a roughly circular path, returning almost to its starting position after each wave passes. the wave is a pattern of motion that travels across the surface, like a ripple running through a crowd doing "the wave" at a stadium — the people move up and down, but they stay in their seats.

waves are born when wind blows across the ocean surface. the wind's energy transfers to the water, creating small ripples that grow into larger waves as the wind continues to push. the size of a wave depends on three factors: wind speed, wind duration (how long the wind blows), and fetch (the distance of open water over which the wind blows). this is why the largest ocean waves are found in the southern ocean, which circles antarctica with

' + ' a = 3 b = 2 π/4 right d = 1 [−2, 4] modelling tides — building a sinusoid from scratch. 1.5 0.507 h(t) = 1.5 cos(0.507 t) + 2 fourier's theorem (1807). building a square wave from sines. but not quite gibbs phenomenon the heisenberg uncertainty in signal processing. why audio engineers think in terms of sine waves. tara wants to know the width of a river — without crossing it. the original use of trigonometry. two angles you need to know. angle of elevation angle of depression worked example 1 — height of a tree. 31.2 + 1.6 ≈ 32.8 m worked example 2 — boat from a cliff. ≈ 220 m worked example 3 — width of a river without a boat. ≈ 57 m (b) 50 m two-observation problems — when you cannot directly access the base. worked example. d = 25 m h ≈ 43.3 m 75 m verification. why this method matters. lunar parallax stellar parallax spherical trigonometry — when the ground curves. the haversine formula worked numerical example — mumbai to guwahati. the angular excess — earth's triangles bulge. an identity is an equation that is always true the most important identity of all. why identities matter — they let you simplify. double angle formulas. why the cosine version has three forms. where you will encounter identities. 0.64 the key identity families — when you need them. pythagorean sum double angle half angle sum-to-product product-to-sum worked example — simplify sin 75° + sin 15° without a calculator. √6/2 ≈ 1.225 proving an identity — work one side at a time. tan θ ✓ identities from euler's formula — algebra replaces cleverness. chebyshev polynomials. minimax the hilbert transform — a 90° phase shifter. analytic signal one identity that runs the world: parseval's theorem. trigonometry trigonometry triangles unlock the heights of mountains and the orbits of planets. snow-leopards-promise turtle-mountain stars-ziro-valley soh cah toa meet tara. she is standing 50 paces from a tall tree, tilting her head up until she is looking right at the top.

 

 the angle her gaze makes with the ground (call it θ) is fixed by two things: how far away she stood, and how tall the tree is. if somebody told her the angle and the distance, could she figure out the height without climbing?

 that is the entire job of trigonometry: turn an angle and one side of a right triangle into the other sides.

 same picture, just the math. strip the tree, the grass, and tara herself, and what remains is a right triangle with one acute angle marked θ:

 three names for three sides. pick one of the two non-right angles and call it θ (theta). now look at the triangle from θ's point of view:

 | side | what it means | how to spot it |
 |------|---------------|----------------|
 | opposite | the side directly across from θ | stand at θ; the opposite side is the one you can't touch. |
 | adjacent | the side next to θ that is not the long slanted one | stand at θ; one of your two arms touches it. |
 | hypotenuse | the longest side, always opposite the right angle | the slanted side. always the same regardless of which θ you pick. |

 the three ratios — soh cah toa.

 | ratio | spelled out | mnemonic letter |
 |-------|-------------|-----------------|
 | sin θ | opposite / hypotenuse | soh |
 | cos θ | adjacent / hypotenuse | cah |
 | tan θ | opposite / adjacent | toa |

 why ratios and not lengths? a photo of a triangle and the real triangle look different in size — but the ratios of their sides are identical. two right triangles with the same angle θ are similar; their sides scale together. so sin 30° is the same number for a triangle the size of your hand and one the size of a building. trig captures the shape, not the size.

 walked example — the 30° triangle. a right triangle has hypotenuse 10 cm and one of its angles is 30°. find the other two sides.

 | step | what to do | result |
 |------|-----------|--------|
 | 1 | we know the hypotenuse and want the side opposite the 30° angle. the ratio that connects them is sin: sin 30° = opposite / 10. | — |
 | 2 | sin 30° = 0.5 (memorise this — it shows up everywhere). | — |
 | 3 | solve: opposite = 10 × 0.5 | 5 cm |
 | 4 | for the adjacent side use cos: cos 30° = adjacent / 10, and cos 30° ≈ 0.866. | — |
 | 5 | adjacent = 10 × 0.866 | ≈ 8.66 cm |
 | 6 | sanity check with pythagoras: 5² + 8.66² should equal 10². | 25 + 75 = 100 ✓ |

 check yourself — predict before you compute. a right triangle has hypotenuse 12 m and θ = 45°. without a calculator, are the other two sides going to be (a) both equal, (b) one bigger and one smaller, (c) impossible to say? (answer at the end of this section.)

 some special angles you should just know.

 | θ | sin θ | cos θ | tan θ |
 |---|-------|-------|-------|
 | 0° | 0 | 1 | 0 |
 | 30° | 1/2 | √3/2 ≈ 0.866 | 1/√3 ≈ 0.577 |
 | 45° | √2/2 ≈ 0.707 | √2/2 ≈ 0.707 | 1 |
 | 60° | √3/2 ≈ 0.866 | 1/2 | √3 ≈ 1.732 |
 | 90° | 1 | 0 | undefined |

 notice the sin column going 0 → 1/2 → √2/2 → √3/2 → 1, and the cos column going the same sequence in reverse. that symmetry is not a coincidence — we will see why in the unit-circle section.

 answer to the predict-yourself question: both sides equal. at 45°, opposite = adjacent because sin 45° = cos 45°. the triangle is isosceles. each side ≈ 12 × 0.707 ≈ 8.49 m. inverse functions: from a ratio back to an angle. soh cah toa gives you a side when you know the angle. often you know two sides and need the angle. the inverse functions arcsin (sin⁻¹), arccos (cos⁻¹), arctan (tan⁻¹) reverse the process.

 example. a ramp rises 1.5 m over a horizontal distance of 6 m. what angle does it make with the ground?

 tan θ = opposite / adjacent = 1.5 / 6 = 0.25 → θ = arctan(0.25) ≈ 14.04°

 reciprocal ratios. three more ratios fill out the family — they are just upside-down versions of sin, cos

searchText:
waves & wave properties from ocean waves to wifi signals — the universal language of oscillation. transverse vs longitudinal waves match each wave to its type the wave equation: v = fλ try it — wave equation calculator interference and superposition diffraction match each diffraction concept to its formula or meaning the doppler effect energy how do the particles move relative to the wave's direction? transverse longitudinal transverse waves longitudinal waves compressions rarefactions both check yourself: wave speed depends on the medium — and the wave type. 343 m/s 1,480 m/s 5,960 m/s worked example — guitar string: 126.5 m/s worked example — sound speed at altitude: 334 m/s worked example — seismograph distance: 67.2 km 

' + 'transverse waves require the medium to resist liquid solid polarisation — proof that light is transverse: malus's law: i = i₀ cos²θ gravitational waves in superfluids v = fλ worked example 1 — middle c on a piano: 1.31 m worked example 2 — the highest and lowest piano notes: 12.5 m 0.082 m worked example 3 — red light: 4.3 × 10¹⁴ hz why fm antennas are about 1.5 m long: 3 m more worked examples across the spectrum: 3.2 m/s 12.2 cm 6 cm 1,200 m 1.85 cm related quantities: worked example — period of middle c: 3.82 ms the wave equation as a pde: ∂²y/∂t² = v² ∂²y/∂x² y(x,t) = f(x − vt) + g(x + vt) phase velocity dispersion — when speed depends on wavelength: group velocity why long ocean swells arrive before choppy waves: quantum connection: sum principle of superposition constructive destructive everyday example — noise-cancelling headphones: zero musical beats — interference you can hear: f_beat = |f₁ − f₂| = 2 beats/second path difference determines everything: path difference | |
' + '| destructive | δ = (n + ½)λ | young's double-slit experiment — proof that light is a wave: δy = λd/d worked example: 6 mm 4.5 mm michelson interferometry — measuring 10⁻¹⁸ m: 4 km 10⁻¹⁸ m thin-film interference — colours in soap bubbles and oil slicks: 2nt = (m + ½)λ anti-reflection coatings diffraction gap or obstacle size ≈ wavelength why you can hear around corners but not see around them: radio waves and mountains: single-slit diffraction — the maths: sin θ = λ/a worked example 1 — sound through a doorway: 43.3° worked example 2 — light through a narrow slit: 0.34° the rayleigh criterion — resolving two objects: fraunhofer diffraction as a fourier transform: fourier transform airy pattern x-ray crystallography — diffraction at the atomic scale: bragg's law: 2d sin θ = nλ electron diffraction doppler effect the ambulance siren test: compressed stretched everyday doppler: the doppler effect works for light too: redshift universe is expanding blueshift practical uses: police radar guns weather radar medical ultrasound the doppler formula for sound: f_obs = f_s × (v ± v_obs) / (v ∓ v_src) worked example 1 — approaching ambulance: 767 hz worked example 2 — receding ambulance: 644 hz 1.19 worked example 3 — police radar: 4,448 hz worked example 4 — hubble's law and galaxy redshift: v = h₀d the relativistic doppler effect: f_obs = f_s × √[(1 + β)/(1 − β)] the transverse doppler effect f_obs = f_s / γ pure time dilation doppler broadening doppler cooling microkelvin temperatures waves-and-properties waves & wave properties from ocean waves to wifi signals — the universal language of oscillation. fishermans-daughter-storm river-dolphins-secret mountain-echoes transverse vs longitudinal waves transverselongitudinaldiagram all waves carry energy from one place to another — but the medium itself doesn't travel. a wave on the brahmaputra doesn't carry water downstream; it passes through the water. the key question is: how do the particles move relative to the wave's direction?

 | wave type | particle motion | wave direction | analogy |
 |-----------|----------------|----------------|----------|
 | transverse | perpendicular (up-down) | horizontal | shaking a rope side-to-side |
 | longitudinal | parallel (back-forth) | same as particle | pushing a slinky from one end |

 transverse waves — the particles oscillate at right angles to the wave's travel. imagine tying a rope to a post and flicking your wrist up and down. a wave pulse travels along the rope, but each point on the rope moves only up and down. light, radio waves, and all electromagnetic radiation are transverse.

 longitudinal waves — the particles oscillate along the same line the wave travels. sound is the classic example. when a dhol drum skin pushes forward, it compresses the air in front of it. that compression travels outward as a wave. the regions of high pressure are called compressions, and the stretched-apart regions are rarefactions.

 | example | type | why |
 |---------|------|-----|
 | light from a saaki (oil lamp) | transverse | e and b fields oscillate perpendicular to light's travel |
 | sound of a pepa (horn) at bihu | longitudinal | air molecules push back and forth along the sound direction |
 | water wave on the brahmaputra | both | water molecules move in elliptical paths — partly up-down, partly back-forth |
 | earthquake p-wave | longitudinal | fastest seismic wave — arrives first at stations |
 | earthquake s-wave | transverse | slower, more destructive — side-to-side shaking |

 check yourself: you're standing on the bank of the brahmaputra and a ferry passes. the wake reaches you, bobbing your fishing cork up and down. is this transverse, longitudinal, or both?

 it's both — surface water waves combine up-down and back-forth motion in elliptical paths. the cork traces a small circle, not just a straight line. wave speed depends on the medium — and the wave type.

 | medium / situation | formula | typical speed |
 |--------------------|---------|---------------|
 | string (guitar, sitar) | v = √(t/μ), t = tension, μ = mass/length | 100–400 m/s |
 | sound in air (20°c) | v ≈ 331 + 0.6t(°c) m/s | 343 m/s |
 | sound in water | v = √(b/ρ), b = bulk modulus | 1,480 m/s |
 | sound in steel | v = √(y/ρ), y = young's modulus | 5,960 m/s |
 | seismic p-wave (granite) | vp = √[(k + 4g/3)/ρ] | 6,000 m/s |
 | seismic s-wave (granite) | vs = √(g/ρ) | 3,500 m/s |

 worked example — guitar string:

 a sitar wire has tension t = 80 n and linear mass density μ = 0.005 kg/m.

 v = √(t/μ) = √(80/0.005) = √16,000 = 126.5 m/s

 worked example — sound speed at altitude:

 at the summit of saramati peak (nagaland, 3,841 m) the temperature is about 5°c.

 v = 331 + 0.6 × 5 = 334 m/s — about 2.6% slower than at sea-level guwahati (35°c → 352 m/s).

 worked example — seismograph distance:

 p-waves travel at 6,000 m/s, s-waves at 3,500 m/s. a seismograph records p-waves 8 seconds before s-waves.

 d = δt / (1/vs − 1/vp) = 8 / (1/3500 − 1/6000) = 8 / (0.0002857 − 0.0001667) = 8 / 0.0001190 = 67.2 km

 the 2016 manipur earthquake (6.7 magnitude) was located using exactly this p-s time delay method from seismograph stations across ne india. why s-waves cannot travel through liquids — and what that tells us about earth's interior:

 transverse waves require the medium to resist shear (sideways deformation). liquids have zero shear modulus — they flow instead of springing back. this means s-waves cannot propagate through any liquid.

 | seismic observation | what it proves |
 |---------------------|----------------|
 | s-waves vanish in the "shadow zone" (104°–140° from epicentre) | earth's outer core is liquid (oldham, 1906) |
 | p-waves refract and slow in outer core | outer core is liquid iron-nickel alloy |
 | pkjkp phases detected (faintly) | inner core is solid — it supports shear |
 | vp/vs ratio in crust | helps identify rock types and fluid content |

 polarisation — proof that light is transverse:

 if light were longitudinal, it could not be polarised. a polarising filter passes only the component of the e-field along one axis. rotating a second polariser (analyser) from parallel to perpendicular drops t

biometeorological research infrasound co-production of climate services weather-and-climate weather & climate meteorology monsoons, cyclones, and why cherrapunji gets 12 meters of rain a year. fishermans-daughter-storm boy-who-talked-to-clouds monsoon-home cloud-refused-rain weather vs climate weather is what is happening right now — today's temperature, whether it is raining, how fast the wind blows. climate is the long-term average of weather patterns in a region over decades or centuries. you can think of climate as a person's personality and weather as their mood on any given day.

assam's climate is subtropical monsoon — hot, humid summers with heavy rain and mild, drier winters. but on any given summer day, the weather might be sunny and dry. a single cold snap does not disprove global warming, just as a single bad day does not change someone's overall temperament. scientists study climate by averaging weather data over at least 30 years, which smooths out the day-to-day noise and reveals the underlying trends. climate is formally defined by the world meteorological organization as the statistical description of weather over a period of at least 30 years (a "climate normal"). the current reference period is 1991-2020. key climate variables include temperature, precipitation, humidity, wind speed, and cloud cover, each characterized by its mean, variance, extremes, and seasonal cycle. assam's climate statistics: mean annual temperature 24 °c, annual rainfall 1,800-2,500 mm (80% during june-september monsoon), relative humidity 75-90%. the köppen-geiger classification categorizes assam as cwa (monsoon-influenced humid subtropical) — "c" = warm temperate, "w" = dry winter, "a" = hot summer (warmest month >22 °c). this system uses thresholds of temperature and precipitation to divide earth into 30 climate types. the distinction between weather and climate maps onto initial-value problems versus boundary-value problems in mathematics. weather prediction requires precise initial conditions (current atmospheric state) and solves the navier-stokes equations forward in time — errors grow exponentially due to chaos, limiting forecasts to 10-14 days. climate prediction, by contrast, depends on boundary conditions (solar input, greenhouse gas concentrations, ocean heat content, land surface albedo) and produces statistical distributions of weather states. this is why climate models can project temperature trends 100 years ahead while weather models cannot forecast 100 days ahead. the ergodic hypothesis in climate science assumes that time averages equal ensemble averages — meaning one long climate record is statistically equivalent to many parallel earths observed simultaneously. climate sensitivity — the equilibrium warming per doubling of co₂ — is constrained by paleoclimate data, observational energy budgets, and model ensembles to 2.5-4.0 °c (ipcc ar6), with the best estimate at 3.0 °c. the water cycle all the water on earth is constantly recycled through a process called the water cycle (or hydrological cycle). the sun heats surface water in oceans, rivers, and lakes, causing it to evaporate into water vapor. plants also release water vapor through their leaves in a process called transpiration. this moist air rises, cools, and the water vapor condenses around tiny dust particles to form clouds.

when cloud droplets merge and grow heavy enough, they fall as precipitation — rain, snow, sleet, or hail depending on the temperature. some precipitation soaks into the ground to become groundwater. some flows over the surface into streams and rivers, eventually reaching the ocean, where the cycle begins again. the water you drink today may have fallen as rain on a himalayan glacier thousands of years ago, flowed down the brahmaputra, evaporated from the bay of bengal, and returned as monsoon rain over assam. earth holds approximately 1.386 × 10⁹ km³ of water. distribution: oceans 96.5%, ice caps and glaciers 1.74%, groundwater 1.69%, surface freshwater (lakes, rivers) 0.013%, atmosphere 0.001%. the atmosphere holds only 12,900 km³ of water at any time — yet global annual precipitation is 505,000 km³, meaning the atmosphere's water content is recycled about 39 times per year (average residence time ≈ 9 days). evaporation requires energy: the latent heat of vaporization is lv = 2.26 × 10⁶ j/kg. the global evaporation rate (505,000 km³/yr) requires a power input of 505,000 × 10⁹ × 1000 × 2.26×10⁶ / (365.25×86400) ≈ 3.6 × 10¹⁶ w — about 25% of the total solar energy absorbed by earth. the brahmaputra basin discharges 19,800 m³/s on average, making it the 4th largest river by discharge. the water cycle is governed by the global energy budget and the clausius-clapeyron relation: the saturation vapor pressure of water increases approximately exponentially with temperature at 7% per °c: des/dt = lv × es/(rv × t²), where rv = 461 j/(kg·k). this means a warmer atmosphere holds more moisture, intensifying the hydrological cycle. under global warming, total precipitation increases at 2-3% per °c (constrained by the energy budget), but extreme precipitation intensifies at the full clausius-clapeyron rate of 7% per °c — explaining why both floods and droughts worsen simultaneously. isotope hydrology uses the ratio of heavy water (hdo, h₂¹⁸o) to normal water as a tracer: heavier isotopes preferentially condense out during precipitation, so himalayan glacial ice has lower δ¹⁸o values than tropical ocean water. this rayleigh distillation process allows paleoclimatologists to reconstruct past temperatures from ice cores — the vostok core revealed 800,000 years of co₂-temperature correlation. the monsoon — india's life-giving engine the indian monsoon is the most important weather pattern on earth, affecting over a billion people. in summer, the asian landmass heats up faster than the indian ocean. hot air rises over the land, creating a zone of low pressure. moist ocean air rushes in to fill the gap, bringing torrential rain from june to september. this is the southwest monsoon.
